pages.html:
Robot Hardware Wiki — Index #

Auto-maintained catalog of all wiki pages. Updated on every ingest or wiki maintenance pass.

Concepts #

Actuator Design (wiki/concepts/actuator-design/) #

  • pareto-optimality — Pareto front framework for actuator design trade-offs
  • actuator-performance-metrics — Mass, rated torque, peak torque, joint/output inertia, and reflected gear-ratio effects
  • actuator-design-principles — Three principles: winding independence, driver matching, inertia determinism
  • motor-constant — Motor constant Km and actuator constant C as coupled actuator quality metrics
  • legged-robot-actuation — Why legged robots use heavy motors with minimum gearing (QDD)
  • actuator-requirement-selection — Choosing actuator targets on the current Pareto front before hardware optimization
  • design-intentions-and-contributions — Practical pre-build design intentions versus retrospective hardware contribution claims
  • torque-mass-inertia-selection — Distal-to-proximal T-M-I workflow, payload torque, reduction ratio, and inertia matching
  • robot-hardware-iteration — Concept-to-demo hardware loop: drafts, CAD iteration, drawings, prototypes, digital interface, and demos

Transmissions (wiki/concepts/transmissions/) #

  • backdrivability — Backward efficiency, reverse-drive friction, and reflected inertia terminology for reducers
  • robot-actuator-reducer-selection — Planetary, cycloidal, and harmonic reducer trade-offs for robot actuators
  • transmission-transparency — Low-impedance/backdrivable transmission design for proprioceptive force control

Motor Fundamentals (wiki/concepts/motor-fundamentals/) #

  • motor-actuator-structure — Motor vs actuator terminology: stator, rotor, air gap, winding, gearbox, driver, housing
  • bldc-pmsm-spmsm — Relationship map for BLDC/PMSM/SPMSM/IPMSM terminology, MTPA, and high-speed topology implications
  • bldc-electronic-commutation — How BLDC drivers replace brushes/commutators by switching stator windings
  • electrical-angle — Magnetic/electrical angle vs physical shaft angle in motors
  • motor-winding-configuration — Low-turn/high-turn, series/parallel, and wye/delta winding choices as voltage/current driver matching
  • motor-constant-unit-conversions — $K_v$, back EMF, $K_t$, oscilloscope estimation, and convention-dependent constant conversions
  • wye-delta-parameter-conventions — Wye/delta terminal equivalence and resistance/inductance convention checks
  • magnetic-reluctance-torque — Magnet-field torque vs reluctance-alignment torque across SPMSM/IPMSM/SynRM motor types
  • motor-operating-region — Speed-current/torque feasibility from voltage/current limits, back EMF, flux weakening, and battery behavior
  • dc-motor-control — DC motor dynamic model, speed-control regions, cascaded control, and H-bridge PWM
  • induction-motor-basics — Rotating magnetic field, slip, equivalent circuit, vector control, and induction field weakening

Motor Modeling (wiki/concepts/motor-modeling/) #

  • abc-to-dq-transform-derivation — Homework-oriented derivation from ABC phase equations to rotor-frame d-q voltage equations
  • spmsm-dq-voltage-equations — Rotor-frame d-q SPMSM model, cross-coupling terms, feedforward, field weakening, and sensorless-model connection
  • spmsm-torque-equation — Flux-linkage torque form, ideal SPMSM q-axis torque, saliency/reluctance term, and MTPA caveats
  • spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions — Exact flux-linkage forms vs constant-inductance and constant-flux assumptions
  • motional-emf-and-flux-linkage — Moving-conductor EMF, voltage distinction, and flux-linkage bridge for torque derivation
  • winding-function-and-factor — Conductor density, winding function, winding factor, and pole-count interpretation
  • analytical-torque-constant-spmsm — Geometry-level SPMSM torque constant estimate from magnet, air-gap, winding, radius, and length

Motor Drivers and Control (wiki/concepts/motor-drivers/, Lecture 3B / Juminchan seminar) #

  • motor-driver-inverter — Voltage-source inverter, half-bridges, active/zero space-vector states, and gate-drive basics
  • space-vector-pwm — SVPWM modulation, zero vectors, voltage hexagon, modulation index, offset PWM, and overmodulation
  • motor-current-sensing — Shunt/Hall current sensing, ADC timing windows, PWM-synchronized sampling, CMRR, noise, and scaling pitfalls
  • motor-current-control — PMSM d-q current PI loop, textbook gain rules, feedforward, voltage saturation, feedback, and anti-windup
  • motor-driver-nonidealities — Dead time, current-direction voltage error, body diode effects, MOSFET delay, ringing, and sensorless-control impact
  • resistive-braking — Braking by electrically loading a spinning PM motor for safety energy dissipation

Sensors (wiki/concepts/sensors/) #

  • hall-effect-position-sensing — Stationary Hall sensor IC rotor-position feedback for BLDC commutation, contrasted with resolver/encoder/sensorless feedback

Summaries #

KAIST DRCD Lectures (wiki/summaries/lectures/) #

  • lecture-01-actuator-design-introduction — Lecture 1: 구동기 설계 개론, re-ingested with transcript/slides/listening notes
  • lecture-02-spmsm-modeling — Lecture 2: SPMSM modeling, corrected motor-constant note, operating region, and d-q voltage equations
  • lecture-03-motor-driver-and-homework-review — Lecture 3 split into 3A homework/modeling review and 3B Juminchan motor-driver/current-control seminar
  • lecture-04-actuator-design-process — Lecture 4: actuator requirement definition, T-M-I workflow, and reducer type selection
  • lecture-05-analytical-torque-constant — Lecture 5: analytical SPMSM torque-constant calculation, winding function, and resistive-braking/MOR homework prelude
  • raipal-mechanism-development — Choi Hyung-ho special lecture on RAIPAL mechanism development from concept comparison to production, URDF/middleware, demos, and retrospective

Textbooks (wiki/summaries/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/) #

  • chapter-01-fundamentals — Textbook Chapter 1: motor taxonomy, magnetic energy conversion, load dynamics, quadrants, and drive-system architecture
  • chapter-02-dc-motor-control — Textbook Chapter 2: DC motor model, speed regions, cascaded control, PI current loop, anti-windup, and H-bridge PWM
  • chapter-03-ac-motors — Textbook Chapter 3: induction motor slip/equivalent circuit/operating regions and synchronous motor steady-state torque
  • chapter-04-ac-motor-modeling — Textbook Chapter 4: AC motor abc-to-d-q modeling, reference frames, induction model, and PMSM flux/torque equations
  • chapter-05-vector-control — Textbook Chapter 5: induction and PMSM vector control, flux orientation, detuning, flux estimation, and MTPA
  • chapter-06-current-regulators — Textbook Chapter 6: hysteresis, ramp-comparison, synchronous-frame PI current regulation, gains, and feedforward
  • chapter-07-pwm-inverters — Textbook Chapter 7: voltage-source inverters, SPWM, THIPWM, SVPWM, overmodulation, dead time, and current measurement
  • chapter-08-high-speed-operation — Textbook Chapter 8: induction field weakening and PMSM flux weakening under voltage/current limits
  • chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control — Textbook Chapter 9: resolver/encoder feedback, M/T speed estimation, and sensorless AC motor control

Papers (wiki/summaries/papers/) #

  • seok-2012-high-force-proprioceptive-actuation — Seok et al. 2012 paper on transparent high-force actuation for MIT Cheetah

Application Notes (wiki/summaries/application-notes/) #

  • honeywell-bldc-magnetic-position-sensing — Honeywell app note summary on Hall-effect commutation sensing

Web Clippings (wiki/summaries/web-clippings/) #

  • bldc-vs-pmsm-terminology — Web clipping summary on BLDC vs PMSM terminology, with caution on simplification
  • bldc-hall-effect-sensors — MECHTEX clipping summary on Hall sensor role in BLDC motors

Shared Notes (wiki/summaries/chatgpt-shares/) #

  • bldc-commutation-and-hall-sensors-chatgpt — Shared ChatGPT beginner notes on BLDC commutation and Hall sensors

Guides #

  • lecture-01-study-path — Recommended reading order for Lecture 1 actuator design concepts
  • lecture-02-study-path — Recommended reading order for Lecture 2 SPMSM modeling concepts, with textbook vector-control and flux-weakening extensions
  • lecture-02-homework-workthrough — Detailed Lecture 2 homework and Lecture 3A review route for ABC to d-q equations, constants, winding tests, and linearity assumptions
  • lecture-03-study-path — Recommended reading order for Lecture 3B, with textbook chapters 5-9 as the control/inverter/feedback reinforcement branch
  • lecture-04-study-path — Recommended reading order for Lecture 4 actuator design process concepts
  • lecture-05-study-path — Recommended reading order for Lecture 5 analytical torque-constant derivation, with chapter 8 field-weakening cross-check
  • actuator-design-to-current-control-map — Cross-lecture study route from actuator requirements to current control, with RAIPAL as the end-to-end hardware case study

pages.title: ABC to d-q Transform Derivation
pages.html:
ABC to d-q Transform Derivation #

ABC to d-q derivation is the route from three physical phase variables to the rotor-frame equations used in spmsm-dq-voltage-equations. The main study point is to separate physical terminal quantities from convention-dependent d-q coordinates.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2 and 7.

Start From Phase Equations #

For a three-phase stator, collect phase quantities as:

$$ \mathbf v_{abc} = \begin{bmatrix} v_a \ v_b \ v_c \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf i_{abc} = \begin{bmatrix} i_a \ i_b \ i_c \end{bmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol\lambda_{abc} = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_a \ \lambda_b \ \lambda_c \end{bmatrix} $$

The phase voltage equation is:

$$ \mathbf v_{abc} #

R_s\mathbf i_{abc} + \frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda_{abc}}{dt} $$

Expanded by phase:

$$ v_a = R_s i_a + \frac{d\lambda_a}{dt}, \quad v_b = R_s i_b + \frac{d\lambda_b}{dt}, \quad v_c = R_s i_c + \frac{d\lambda_c}{dt} $$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 452-459.

Flux Linkage Split #

The useful flux-linkage split is:

$$ \boldsymbol\lambda_{abc} #

\mathbf L_{abc}(\theta_e, \mathbf i_{abc})\mathbf i_{abc} + \boldsymbol\lambda_{pm,abc}(\theta_e) $$

For the linear, ideal SPMSM derivation, this is simplified to:

$$ \boldsymbol\lambda_{abc} #

\mathbf L_{abc}(\theta_e)\mathbf i_{abc} + \boldsymbol\lambda_{pm,abc}(\theta_e) $$

where $\theta_e$ is electrical rotor angle. The relation to mechanical angle is:

$$ \theta_e = p\theta_m, \quad \omega_e = \frac{d\theta_e}{dt} = p\omega_m, \quad p = \frac{P}{2} $$

Here $P$ is pole count and $p$ is pole-pair count.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; convention discussion in sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 473-482.

Why Trigonometric Terms Appear #

The trigonometric structure comes from projecting a rotating magnetic field onto three stator phase axes separated by $120^\circ$ electrical.

For an ideal sinusoidal permanent-magnet flux:

$$ \boldsymbol\lambda_{pm,abc} #

\phi_f \begin{bmatrix} \cos \theta_e \ \cos \left(\theta_e - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \ \cos \left(\theta_e + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \end{bmatrix} $$

The same projection idea explains common mutual-inductance terms. If two ideal phase axes are separated by $120^\circ$, their projection is:

$$ \cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right) = -\frac{1}{2} $$

So an ideal balanced inductance matrix often contains off-diagonal terms like:

$$ \mathbf L_{abc} \approx \begin{bmatrix} L_{\ell} + L_m & -\frac{1}{2}L_m & -\frac{1}{2}L_m \ -\frac{1}{2}L_m & L_{\ell} + L_m & -\frac{1}{2}L_m \ -\frac{1}{2}L_m & -\frac{1}{2}L_m & L_{\ell} + L_m \end{bmatrix} $$

This $-\frac{1}{2}$ is a symmetry result for an ideal sinusoidal, balanced, linear model. It is not a universal law for every real motor geometry.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 7-10; transcript caution in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 123-161.

Reduce Three Phases to Two Coordinates #

For a balanced three-wire motor with no neutral current:

$$ i_a + i_b + i_c = 0 $$

So the three phase currents only contain two independent degrees of freedom. Park/Clarke transformation rewrites those two degrees of freedom into a rotor-aligned frame.

One common amplitude-invariant convention is:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} x_d \ x_q \ x_0 \end{bmatrix} #

\frac{2}{3} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \cos\left(\theta_e - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) & \cos\left(\theta_e + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \ -\sin\theta_e & -\sin\left(\theta_e - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) & -\sin\left(\theta_e + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \ \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_a \ x_b \ x_c \end{bmatrix} $$

For a balanced three-wire motor, $x_0 = 0$.

WARNING Other books and control libraries change signs, scale factors, axis order, or whether the transform is amplitude-invariant or power-invariant. Those choices change numerical definitions of $i_d$, $i_q$, $\phi_f$, $L_s$, and torque constants. They do not change the physical motor. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 12-13.

The textbook uses the same amplitude-invariant $2/3$ matrix for arbitrary rotating reference-frame transformation:

$$ T(\theta)=\frac{2}{3} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \cos(\theta-\frac{2\pi}{3}) & \cos(\theta+\frac{2\pi}{3}) \ -\sin\theta & -\sin(\theta-\frac{2\pi}{3}) & -\sin(\theta+\frac{2\pi}{3}) \ \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix} $$

It also explicitly notes that, with this transform choice, instantaneous power in d-q-n variables carries a $\frac{3}{2}$ factor. That is why the same factor appears in the d-q torque derivation.

Figure: reference-frame transformations between abc, stationary d-q, and rotating d-q frames. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4—Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-_2017_Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 29, Fig. 4.22.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4—Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-_2017_Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 25 and 29.

Rotating-Frame Derivative #

With the above convention, use the rotor-frame flux vector:

$$ \boldsymbol\lambda_{dq} #

\begin{bmatrix} \lambda_d \ \lambda_q \end{bmatrix} $$

The rotating-frame derivative identity is:

$$ \mathbf v_{dq} #

R_s\mathbf i_{dq} + \frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda_{dq}}{dt} + \omega_e \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \boldsymbol\lambda_{dq} $$

So:

$$ v_d #

R_s i_d + \frac{d\lambda_d}{dt} #

\omega_e\lambda_q $$

$$ v_q #

R_s i_q + \frac{d\lambda_q}{dt} + \omega_e\lambda_d $$

This form is the safest intermediate result because it keeps flux linkage explicit.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.

Ideal SPMSM Substitution #

For an ideal non-salient SPMSM:

$$ \lambda_d = L_s i_d + \phi_f $$

$$ \lambda_q = L_s i_q $$

Assuming $L_s$ and $\phi_f$ are constant:

$$ v_d #

R_s i_d + L_s\frac{d i_d}{dt} #

\omega_e L_s i_q $$

$$ v_q #

R_s i_q + L_s\frac{d i_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_s i_d + \omega_e\phi_f $$

The terms to memorize structurally are:

Term Meaning
$R_s i_d$, $R_s i_q$ copper resistance voltage drop
$L_s\frac{d i_d}{dt}$, $L_s\frac{d i_q}{dt}$ current-changing inductive voltage
$-\omega_e L_s i_q$, $+\omega_e L_s i_d$ d-q cross-coupling from rotating coordinates
$+\omega_e\phi_f$ permanent-magnet back EMF

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.

Related #

  • spmsm-dq-voltage-equations
  • spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions
  • spmsm-torque-equation
  • motor-operating-region
  • motor-current-control
pages.text:
# ABC to d-q Transform Derivation

ABC to d-q derivation is the route from three physical phase variables to the rotor-frame equations used in spmsm-dq-voltage-equations. The main study point is to separate physical terminal quantities from convention-dependent d-q coordinates.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2 and 7.
# Start From Phase Equations

For a three-phase stator, collect phase quantities as:

$$
\mathbf v{abc} =
\begin{bmatrix}
va \\
vb \\
vc
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\mathbf i{abc} =
\begin{bmatrix}
ia \\
ib \\
ic
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\boldsymbol\lambda{abc} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\lambdaa \\
\lambdab \\
\lambdac
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The phase voltage equation is:

$$
\mathbf v{abc}
=
Rs\mathbf i{abc}
- \frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}}{dt}
$$

Expanded by phase:

$$
va = Rs ia + \frac{d\lambdaa}{dt},
\quad
vb = Rs ib + \frac{d\lambdab}{dt},
\quad
vc = Rs ic + \frac{d\lambdac}{dt}
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 452-459.
# Flux Linkage Split

The useful flux-linkage split is:

$$
\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}
=
\mathbf L{abc}(\thetae, \mathbf i{abc})\mathbf i{abc}
- \boldsymbol\lambda{pm,abc}(\thetae)
$$

For the linear, ideal SPMSM derivation, this is simplified to:

$$
\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}
=
\mathbf L{abc}(\thetae)\mathbf i{abc}
- \boldsymbol\lambda{pm,abc}(\thetae)
$$

where $\thetae$ is electrical rotor angle. The relation to mechanical angle is:

$$
\thetae = p\thetam,
\quad
\omegae = \frac{d\thetae}{dt} = p\omegam,
\quad
p = \frac{P}{2}
$$

Here $P$ is pole count and $p$ is pole-pair count.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; convention discussion in sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 473-482.
# Why Trigonometric Terms Appear

The trigonometric structure comes from projecting a rotating magnetic field onto three stator phase axes separated by $120^\circ$ electrical.

For an ideal sinusoidal permanent-magnet flux:

$$
\boldsymbol\lambda{pm,abc}
=
\phif
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \thetae \\
\cos \left(\thetae - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\
\cos \left(\thetae + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The same projection idea explains common mutual-inductance terms. If two ideal phase axes are separated by $120^\circ$, their projection is:

$$
\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right) = -\frac{1}{2}
$$

So an ideal balanced inductance matrix often contains off-diagonal terms like:

$$
\mathbf L{abc}
\approx
\begin{bmatrix}
L{\ell} + Lm & -\frac{1}{2}Lm & -\frac{1}{2}Lm \\
-\frac{1}{2}Lm & L{\ell} + Lm & -\frac{1}{2}Lm \\
-\frac{1}{2}Lm & -\frac{1}{2}Lm & L{\ell} + Lm
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This $-\frac{1}{2}$ is a symmetry result for an ideal sinusoidal, balanced, linear model. It is not a universal law for every real motor geometry.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 7-10; transcript caution in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 123-161.
# Reduce Three Phases to Two Coordinates

For a balanced three-wire motor with no neutral current:

$$
ia + ib + ic = 0
$$

So the three phase currents only contain two independent degrees of freedom. Park/Clarke transformation rewrites those two degrees of freedom into a rotor-aligned frame.

One common amplitude-invariant convention is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
xd \\
xq \\
x0
\end{bmatrix}
=
\frac{2}{3}
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\thetae & \cos\left(\thetae - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) & \cos\left(\thetae + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\
-\sin\thetae & -\sin\left(\thetae - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) & -\sin\left(\thetae + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\
\frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
xa \\
xb \\
xc
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For a balanced three-wire motor, $x0 = 0$.

> WARNING
> Other books and control libraries change signs, scale factors, axis order, or whether the transform is amplitude-invariant or power-invariant. Those choices change numerical definitions of $id$, $iq$, $\phif$, $Ls$, and torque constants. They do not change the physical motor.
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 12-13.

The textbook uses the same amplitude-invariant $2/3$ matrix for arbitrary rotating reference-frame transformation:

$$
T(\theta)=\frac{2}{3}
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & \cos(\theta-\frac{2\pi}{3}) & \cos(\theta+\frac{2\pi}{3}) \\
-\sin\theta & -\sin(\theta-\frac{2\pi}{3}) & -\sin(\theta+\frac{2\pi}{3}) \\
\frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

It also explicitly notes that, with this transform choice, instantaneous power in d-q-n variables carries a $\frac{3}{2}$ factor. That is why the same factor appears in the d-q torque derivation.

Figure: reference-frame transformations between abc, stationary d-q, and rotating d-q frames. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 29, Fig. 4.22.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 25 and 29.
# Rotating-Frame Derivative

With the above convention, use the rotor-frame flux vector:

$$
\boldsymbol\lambda{dq}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\lambdad \\
\lambdaq
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The rotating-frame derivative identity is:

$$
\mathbf v{dq}
=
Rs\mathbf i{dq}
- \frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{dq}}{dt}
- \omegae
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & -1 \\
1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\boldsymbol\lambda{dq}
$$

So:

$$
vd
=
Rs id
- \frac{d\lambdad}{dt}
- \omegae\lambdaq
$$

$$
vq
=
Rs iq
- \frac{d\lambdaq}{dt}
- \omegae\lambdad
$$

This form is the safest intermediate result because it keeps flux linkage explicit.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.
# Ideal SPMSM Substitution

For an ideal non-salient SPMSM:

$$
\lambdad = Ls id + \phif
$$

$$
\lambdaq = Ls iq
$$

Assuming $Ls$ and $\phif$ are constant:

$$
vd
=
Rs id
- Ls\frac{d id}{dt}
- \omegae Ls iq
$$

$$
vq
=
Rs iq
- Ls\frac{d iq}{dt}
- \omegae Ls id
- \omegae\phif
$$

The terms to memorize structurally are:

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $Rs id$, $Rs iq$ | copper resistance voltage drop |
| $Ls\frac{d id}{dt}$, $Ls\frac{d iq}{dt}$ | current-changing inductive voltage |
| $-\omegae Ls iq$, $+\omegae Ls id$ | d-q cross-coupling from rotating coordinates |
| $+\omegae\phif$ | permanent-magnet back EMF |

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.
# Related
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations
- spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions
- spmsm-torque-equation
- motor-operating-region
- motor-current-control

pages.title: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
pages.html:
Actuator Design to Current Control Map #

This guide connects the lecture sequence as one design chain. Lecture 3 is split in this map: Lecture 3A belongs to the Lecture 2 modeling/homework review, while Lecture 3B is Juminchan’s separate motor-driver/current-control seminar branch. Lecture 5 returns to motor quality and explains how to estimate torque constant analytically before detailed FEM.

flowchart LR A["Requirements"] --> B["Torque / Mass / Inertia"] B --> C["Motor Quality and Winding"] C --> C2["Lecture 5: Analytical Kt"] C2 --> D["SPMSM Model and MOR"] D --> D2["Lecture 3A: Homework / Modeling Review"] D2 --> E["Lecture 3B: Inverter and SVPWM"] E --> F["Current Sensing"] F --> G["d-q Current Control"] G --> H["Actuator-Level Revision"] H --> I["RAIPAL Case Study: Production / URDF / Demos"]

Use this after the lecture-specific paths when you want the system view rather than one lecture at a time.

Source: synthesis route across sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-166; Lecture 3A/3B boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 12-25; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 51-117; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 52-72; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 468-483.

1. Choose the Requirement Point #

Start with actuator-requirement-selection and pareto-optimality.

The first design decision is not “make every metric better.” It is choosing a point on the current Pareto front that matches the robot task. For actuator selection, keep primary requirements separate from secondary preferences so the optimization target does not move every time a design looks attractive.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 51-61; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 65-117.

2. Translate the Robot Task Into T-M-I #

Read torque-mass-inertia-selection, then return to actuator-performance-metrics.

The useful mechanical requirements are torque, mass, and output inertia. The Lecture 4 workflow moves distal to proximal: choose the far actuator first, then account for its mass when sizing the upstream joint. After the motor is selected, the reducer ratio becomes the main remaining knob for reflected rotor inertia.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 150-155; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 223-239; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 10-13.

3. Pick Motor Quality Before Winding Details #

Read motor-constant and actuator-design-principles.

Use motor constant and actuator constant to reason about intrinsic motor quality before treating winding as a magic performance change. Winding choice mainly matches the same motor geometry to the available voltage/current driver constraints; it does not remove copper loss or thermal limits.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31.

4. Estimate Torque Constant From Geometry #

Read analytical-torque-constant-spmsm, motional-emf-and-flux-linkage, and winding-function-and-factor.

Lecture 5 fills in how a designer estimates $K_t$ from cross-section geometry, magnet grade, axial length, and winding pattern before building hardware. Keep the result connected to motor-constant: analytical $K_t$ is one ingredient in the actuator-quality metric, not the whole actuator design.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 52-72; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 468-483; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

5. Match the Motor to the Driver #

Read motor-winding-configuration, motor-operating-region, and motor-constant-unit-conversions.

At this stage, check whether the selected motor and winding can live inside the driver and battery limits. Keep the convention questions explicit: phase vs line-line, peak vs RMS, q-axis current vs line current, and wye/delta equivalent parameters can change the numerical constants used by the controller.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-253; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 5; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 9.

6. Build the Model Used by the Controller #

Read abc-to-dq-transform-derivation, spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, spmsm-torque-equation, and spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions.

The controller-facing model turns phase variables into rotor-frame d-q variables. Keep the flux-linkage form visible before simplifying to constant $L_s$ and constant $\phi_f$, because saturation and saliency change what the simple SPMSM equations mean.

Use lecture-02-homework-workthrough for the Lecture 3A review questions that close this modeling block.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

7. Branch Into Lecture 3B: Turn Voltage Limits Into Usable Voltage #

Read motor-driver-inverter and space-vector-pwm.

This is the start of the Juminchan seminar branch. The inverter applies phase voltage through switching states, not through ideal analog voltage sources. SVPWM mixes active and zero vectors over a switching period, and the real controller keeps voltage margin so sensing and switching behavior remain usable.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-205; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 12-13.

8. Continue Lecture 3B: Measure Current Before Controlling It #

Read motor-current-sensing.

Current control quality depends on the current measurement path: shunt value, amplifier gain, ADC timing, CMRR, ground bounce, and unit scaling. A motor model can be clean while the measured current is still wrong enough to destabilize tuning.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 185-223; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 15-18.

9. Continue Lecture 3B: Close the d-q Current Loop #

Read motor-current-control, then motor-driver-nonidealities.

The d-q PI loop is where the actuator design becomes an implemented torque source. Bandwidth, feedforward, saturation, anti-windup, dead time, delay, and ringing decide how much of the selected actuator capability is actually reachable in hardware.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 21; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 24-25.

10. Add the Textbook Control Backbone #

Use the Electric Motor Control chapters as the control-theory spine behind the lecture chain:

  • chapter-05-vector-control links the SPMSM/IPMSM d-q model to vector-control torque commands.
  • chapter-06-current-regulators formalizes the current-loop gains and feedforward terms behind the Lecture 3B tuning advice.
  • chapter-07-pwm-inverters explains how the inverter actually spends switching time to produce voltage, and why overmodulation/dead time/current sampling constrain the clean control diagram.
  • chapter-08-high-speed-operation turns the MOR into current-circle and voltage-ellipse geometry.
  • chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control closes the loop on rotor-angle and speed feedback.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5—Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m_2017_Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 33-44; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6—Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren_2017_Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 11-17; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7—Pulse-width-modulation-inverters_2017_Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 37-76; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8—High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr_2017_Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9—Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a_2017_Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-15.

11. Feed Hardware Limits Back Into the Design #

Return to backdrivability, transmission-transparency, and robot-actuator-reducer-selection.

If the design fails at the current-control stage, the fix may be mechanical, electrical, or both: a different reduction ratio, winding, driver voltage/current rating, thermal path, sensing topology, or reducer type. Treat this as a loop back to requirements, not as a controller-only failure.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 300-345.

12. Study the RAIPAL End-to-End Case #

Read raipal-mechanism-development, design-intentions-and-contributions, and robot-hardware-iteration.

The RAIPAL special lecture is the end-to-end case study after the abstract design chain. It shows how control transparency and engineering simplicity become mechanism choices, how concept comparison uses T-M-I estimates before detailed CAD, how production drawings and prototypes expose new errors, and why digital interface work is part of hardware completion for RL-oriented systems.

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 5; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 14-17; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 25; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 28; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 63-75; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 127-177; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 9-39; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 88-95.

Review Checklist #

  • Can the required joint torque be traced to a payload/task assumption?
  • Is the actuator limited by torque, voltage, current, temperature, output inertia, or backdrivability?
  • Are $K_t$, $K_v$, $R_s$, and $L_s$ all using compatible conventions?
  • Does the operating-region plot use realistic voltage margin?
  • Is the current sensor measuring the same current variable used by the control law?
  • Is the rotor-angle/speed feedback path good enough for the intended low-speed, startup, and sensorless behavior?
  • If the loop saturates, is the next design change in the motor, reducer, driver, battery, sensor, or control tuning?

Source: checklist synthesized from sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-166; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 12-25; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 51-117; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 150-239; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9—Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a_2017_Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-15.

Related Lecture Paths #

  • lecture-01-study-path
  • lecture-02-study-path
  • lecture-02-homework-workthrough
  • lecture-03-study-path
  • lecture-04-study-path
  • lecture-05-study-path
  • raipal-mechanism-development

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 185-223; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 15-18.
# 9. Continue Lecture 3B: Close the d-q Current Loop

Read motor-current-control, then motor-driver-nonidealities.

The d-q PI loop is where the actuator design becomes an implemented torque source. Bandwidth, feedforward, saturation, anti-windup, dead time, delay, and ringing decide how much of the selected actuator capability is actually reachable in hardware.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 21; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 24-25.
# 10. Add the Textbook Control Backbone

Use the Electric Motor Control chapters as the control-theory spine behind the lecture chain:
- chapter-05-vector-control links the SPMSM/IPMSM d-q model to vector-control torque commands.
- chapter-06-current-regulators formalizes the current-loop gains and feedforward terms behind the Lecture 3B tuning advice.
- chapter-07-pwm-inverters explains how the inverter actually spends switching time to produce voltage, and why overmodulation/dead time/current sampling constrain the clean control diagram.
- chapter-08-high-speed-operation turns the MOR into current-circle and voltage-ellipse geometry.
- chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control closes the loop on rotor-angle and speed feedback.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 33-44; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 11-17; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 37-76; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-15.
# 11. Feed Hardware Limits Back Into the Design

Return to backdrivability, transmission-transparency, and robot-actuator-reducer-selection.

If the design fails at the current-control stage, the fix may be mechanical, electrical, or both: a different reduction ratio, winding, driver voltage/current rating, thermal path, sensing topology, or reducer type. Treat this as a loop back to requirements, not as a controller-only failure.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 300-345.
# 12. Study the RAIPAL End-to-End Case

Read raipal-mechanism-development, design-intentions-and-contributions, and robot-hardware-iteration.

The RAIPAL special lecture is the end-to-end case study after the abstract design chain. It shows how control transparency and engineering simplicity become mechanism choices, how concept comparison uses T-M-I estimates before detailed CAD, how production drawings and prototypes expose new errors, and why digital interface work is part of hardware completion for RL-oriented systems.

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 5; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 14-17; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 25; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 28; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 63-75; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 127-177; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 9-39; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 88-95.
# Review Checklist
- Can the required joint torque be traced to a payload/task assumption?
- Is the actuator limited by torque, voltage, current, temperature, output inertia, or backdrivability?
- Are $Kt$, $Kv$, $Rs$, and $Ls$ all using compatible conventions?
- Does the operating-region plot use realistic voltage margin?
- Is the current sensor measuring the same current variable used by the control law?
- Is the rotor-angle/speed feedback path good enough for the intended low-speed, startup, and sensorless behavior?
- If the loop saturates, is the next design change in the motor, reducer, driver, battery, sensor, or control tuning?

Source: checklist synthesized from sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-166; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 12-25; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 51-117; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 150-239; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-15.

pages.title: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
pages.html:
Actuator Performance Metrics #

Lecture 4 refines the actuator primary requirements to four coupled quantities:

  • mass
  • rated torque
  • joint/output inertia
  • peak or saturation torque

For off-the-shelf frameless motor selection, the lecture says this often reduces in practice to torque, mass, and inertia because motor constant and saturation torque are coupled inside a motor family. Power and max speed are not valid standalone primary metrics because they can be shifted by winding, driver voltage/current, and gear ratio.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 5-6.

1. Mass #

Total weight of the actuator assembly.

Scaling law:

  • Volume $\propto L^3$, magnetic shear area $\propto L^2$, torque arm $\propto L$
  • $\tau \propto$ shear stress $\times$ area $\times r \propto L^2 \times L = L^3$? No — torque scales roughly as $L^2$ relative to mass scaling of $L^3$
  • $10\times$ size → $1000\times$ mass, roughly $100\times$ torque → torque/mass ratio worsens with scale

Simply making a motor bigger gives diminishing returns.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-102.

2. Rated Torque and Peak Torque #

Lecture 4 separates two torque limits:

  • Rated torque: continuous or steady-state torque, limited mainly by heat dissipation under a defined thermal condition.
  • Peak / saturation torque: short-duration torque limited by magnetic saturation when heating is ignored or treated as transient.

Rated torque is tied to copper heat:

  • Copper heat loss: $P_{cu} = I^2R$
  • Steady-state thermal equilibrium: $P_{cu} = hA\Delta T$
  • Heat generation $\propto L^3$ (volume), heat dissipation $\propto L^2$ (surface area)
  • Larger motors are harder to cool — max torque is thermally constrained

Peak torque is tied to the nonlinear magnetic limit. Beyond saturation, additional current no longer produces proportional torque.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 104-114; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 107-109.

3. Joint Inertia (Output Inertia) #

The rotational inertia felt at the output shaft.

$$J_{out} = N^2 \times J_m + J_{gear}$$

Where:

  • $J_{out}$ = output-side or joint-side inertia of the actuator’s internal rotating parts.

  • $N$ = reduction ratio: the motor turns $N$ times for one output/joint turn.

  • $J_m$ = motor rotor inertia about the motor shaft, not total motor mass.

  • $J_{gear}$ = gearbox/reducer rotating inertia as seen at the output side.

  • Gear ratio $10\times$ → reflected inertia $100\times$ increase

  • Power is maintained, torque increases, but backdrivability degrades severely

  • This is the fundamental reason QDD (Quasi-Direct Drive) avoids high gear ratios

The formula is usually about the actuator’s internal inertia, not the external robot link/load inertia. A larger system model may add the link/load inertia separately.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 116-138.

Reference Frame Clarification #

“Output inertia,” “joint inertia,” and “total reflected rotor inertia” are often used inconsistently. In this lecture’s main design argument, the important quantity is the inertia felt at the joint/output, especially motor rotor inertia reflected through the reducer:

$$J_{rotor \rightarrow out} \approx N^2 J_m$$

The inverse statement is also true when looking from the motor side: a load inertia behind a reducer appears smaller to the motor by roughly $\frac{1}{N^2}$. These are not contradictory; they use opposite reference frames.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 118-124.

Key Insight #

Using power or max angular velocity as performance metrics is a misconception. Electric motors (unlike combustion engines) have no theoretical power limit — you can always adjust the operating region via gear ratio. The three metrics above are what actually matter.

Lecture 4 sharpens this point: velocity/power is a secondary requirement for actuator selection, not a primary physical trade-off like mass, rated torque, peak torque, and inertia. It still must be checked against winding and driver voltage/current limits, especially when using off-the-shelf motors.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 5.

Why “Bigger Motor + Bigger Gearbox” Is Not Free #

Two scaling traps explain why actuator design becomes a Pareto trade-off instead of a simple “make it stronger” problem.

1. Motor Scale: Mass Grows Very Fast #

If every linear dimension of a motor scales by $L$, its volume and mass scale roughly as:

$$Mass \propto Volume \propto L^3$$

So a motor that is $10\times$ larger in every direction has roughly:

$$10^3 = 1000\times$$

more mass.

Many useful physical limits, such as magnetic shear area or heat-dissipation area, scale more like area:

$$Area \propto L^2$$

So a $10\times$ length scale gives only about:

$$10^2 = 100\times$$

more area.

The practical intuition: a bigger motor becomes heavy very quickly, while some of the useful electromagnetic or thermal capability does not grow as cheaply. Scaling up is therefore a trade-off, not a free improvement.

QUESTION The exact torque scaling depends on which dimensions are scaled and which constraints are held fixed. The safe takeaway from Lecture 1 is not “torque is always exactly $L^2$,” but “mass grows aggressively with size, so motor scaling has a real mass penalty.” Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-102.

2. Gear Ratio: Reflected Inertia Grows Quadratically #

A reducer with ratio $N$ means the motor spins $N$ times for one output/joint rotation.

For a $10:1$ reduction:

  • motor rotates 10 turns
  • joint rotates 1 turn
  • output torque is about $10\times$ motor torque

But reflected motor inertia at the joint is:

$$J_{rotor \rightarrow out} \approx N^2J_m$$

This comes from kinetic energy:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}J\omega^2$$

If the joint speed is $\omega$, the motor rotor speed is $N\omega$:

$$E_m = \frac{1}{2}J_m(N\omega)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(N^2J_m)\omega^2$$

From the joint’s point of view, the motor rotor behaves like it has $N^2J_m$ inertia.

Examples:

  • $10:1$ reduction → $10\times$ torque but $100\times$ reflected rotor inertia
  • $100:1$ reduction → $100\times$ torque but $10{,}000\times$ reflected rotor inertia

This is why high reduction can make a legged robot feel heavy, non-backdrivable, inefficient during impact, and easier to damage. See legged-robot-actuation and transmission-transparency.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 118-138.

The slide deck makes this concrete with a power-density trap:

  • Small motor: mass = 38g, power = 1166W
  • HOUND-style stator+rotor example: mass = 1042g ($27\times$), power = 2700W ($2.3\times$)

Figure: power density can make the small high-speed motor look better, but the joint torque/inertia requirement changes the conclusion. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 13.

If power were the metric, the small motor would look categorically better. The lecture’s point is that once the joint torque requirement is imposed, the small motor needs much higher reduction, and the $N^2$ reflected inertia can dominate.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 12-13.

Seok et al. make the same argument more formally: EM motors can achieve high power density at high speed and relatively low torque, but high-force robot joints need delivered torque. If higher voltage/speed requires higher gear reduction, actuator impedance rises and delivered force control worsens. The paper therefore recommends torque density over power density as the clearer robotics actuator metric.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, pages 1-3.

Thought Experiment #

Two motors, same max torque target:

  • Motor 1: mass = 8 ($2\times2\times2$ cube), direct drive → $J_{out} = mr^2 = 8 \times 4 = 32$
  • Motor 2: mass = 1, needs 8:1 gear ratio → $J_{out} = 1 \times 8^2 = 64$

Motor 1 is heavier but has half the output inertia. Bigger motor + lower gear ratio wins on inertia.

Note: this is an illustrative calculation using the $N^2$ reflected-inertia relation, not a copied motor spec.

Source: related lecture example in sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22.

Application Dependence #

The same Pareto front can imply different choices:

  • Legged robots: reflected inertia is critical because impacts dominate, so heavier high-torque actuators can be justified to keep joint inertia low.
  • Manipulators: static load, precision, and force control can dominate more than repeated impact survival, so higher reduction and lower motor mass may remain acceptable in many designs.

Related #

  • pareto-optimality
  • motor-constant
  • actuator-requirement-selection
  • torque-mass-inertia-selection
  • actuator-design-principles
  • legged-robot-actuation
  • motor-actuator-structure
  • transmission-transparency

pages.title: Actuator Requirement Selection
pages.html:
Actuator Requirement Selection #

Actuator requirement selection means choosing what the actuator must be good at before optimizing the hardware. Lecture 4 separates this from actuator optimal design itself.

The core rule:

Choose the target point on the current Pareto front first; push the Pareto front later.

Requirement Selection vs Hardware Improvement #

Requirement selection answers:

  • What robot are we building?
  • What payload, speed, and task envelope matter?
  • Where should this actuator sit among the trade-offs?

Hardware improvement answers:

  • Can better materials improve the front?
  • Can better reducer manufacturing improve friction/backlash?
  • Can better motor topology raise the achievable performance?

These are different problems. The lecture’s analogy is a bicycle: first decide mountain bike vs road bike, then optimize bearings, frame material, and components.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 51-61.

Design Intentions Before Contributions #

The RAIPAL mechanism lecture adds a practical language layer: design intentions are the pre-build choices that make the robot possible, while contributions are the retrospective performance claims made after the robot exists.

This matters because many requirement-selection decisions are not glamorous metrics. Modularity, manufacturability, serviceability, simple dynamics, repeated subcomponents, and clear assembly paths may decide whether the robot becomes controllable hardware at all. The lecture explicitly warns that intentions and contributions are not mutually exclusive; a good hardware engineer circulates between them while samples are limited and expensive.

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 4; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 37-59.

Primary Requirements #

Lecture 4 identifies four primary actuator requirements:

Figure: Lecture 4 primary actuator requirements and corrected normalized relationship. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6.

  • Mass
  • Rated torque
  • Joint/output inertia
  • Peak or saturation torque

These are primary because they are fundamentally coupled by the motor/actuator physics. At the same design level, improving one usually worsens another.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 5-6.

Secondary Requirements #

The lecture lists secondary requirements such as:

  • backlash
  • volume
  • stiffness
  • velocity / power

They are still important, but they are not coupled in the same clean fundamental relationship as the primary requirements. They are often limited by:

  • manufacturing precision
  • assembly tolerances
  • reducer tooth/profile design
  • driver voltage/current capability
  • cost and available components

Backlash is a useful example. It matters a lot for robot positioning, but an ideal zero-tolerance manufacturing world could reduce it without violating a Maxwell-equation-style actuator metric. In practice, cost, thermal expansion, wear, and manufacturing quality set the limit.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 81-89; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 300-315.

Corrected Actuator Metric #

Lecture 4 supersedes the earlier lecture notes and gives the current normalized relationship as:

$$ C \approx \frac{K_m^2\tau_{sat}^2}{M I^2} $$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6.

where:

  • $K_m = \frac{K_t}{\sqrt{R_s}}$ is the motor constant
  • $\tau_{sat}$ is the torque limit from core saturation
  • $M$ is copper mass
  • $I$ is joint/output inertia

This formula is why the lecture says the four primary requirements are coupled.

For off-the-shelf frameless motor selection, $K_m$ and $\tau_{sat}$ are usually already coupled inside a motor family. In that practical case, the designer mainly chooses among torque, mass, and inertia.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 95-117.

Iteration #

Requirement selection is not a one-pass calculation. If a robot becomes too heavy or cannot meet the task:

  • step back in the distal-to-proximal chain
  • lower payload or total-mass goals
  • choose better motors/materials/reducer topology
  • improve manufacturing quality if the issue is friction/backlash rather than the primary motor trade-off

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 7.

Related #

  • pareto-optimality

  • robot-actuator-reducer-selection
  • design-intentions-and-contributions
pages.text:
# Actuator Requirement Selection

Actuator requirement selection means choosing what the actuator must be good at before optimizing the hardware. Lecture 4 separates this from actuator optimal design itself.

The core rule:

Choose the target point on the current Pareto front first; push the Pareto front later.
# Requirement Selection vs Hardware Improvement

Requirement selection answers:
- What robot are we building?
- What payload, speed, and task envelope matter?
- Where should this actuator sit among the trade-offs?

Hardware improvement answers:
- Can better materials improve the front?
- Can better reducer manufacturing improve friction/backlash?
- Can better motor topology raise the achievable performance?

These are different problems. The lecture's analogy is a bicycle: first decide mountain bike vs road bike, then optimize bearings, frame material, and components.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 51-61.
# Design Intentions Before Contributions

The RAIPAL mechanism lecture adds a practical language layer: design intentions are the pre-build choices that make the robot possible, while contributions are the retrospective performance claims made after the robot exists.

This matters because many requirement-selection decisions are not glamorous metrics. Modularity, manufacturability, serviceability, simple dynamics, repeated subcomponents, and clear assembly paths may decide whether the robot becomes controllable hardware at all. The lecture explicitly warns that intentions and contributions are not mutually exclusive; a good hardware engineer circulates between them while samples are limited and expensive.

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 4; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 37-59.
# Primary Requirements

Lecture 4 identifies four primary actuator requirements:

Figure: Lecture 4 primary actuator requirements and corrected normalized relationship. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6.
- Mass
- Rated torque
- Joint/output inertia
- Peak or saturation torque

These are primary because they are fundamentally coupled by the motor/actuator physics. At the same design level, improving one usually worsens another.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 5-6.
# Secondary Requirements

The lecture lists secondary requirements such as:
- backlash
- volume
- stiffness
- velocity / power

They are still important, but they are not coupled in the same clean fundamental relationship as the primary requirements. They are often limited by:
- manufacturing precision
- assembly tolerances
- reducer tooth/profile design
- driver voltage/current capability
- cost and available components

Backlash is a useful example. It matters a lot for robot positioning, but an ideal zero-tolerance manufacturing world could reduce it without violating a Maxwell-equation-style actuator metric. In practice, cost, thermal expansion, wear, and manufacturing quality set the limit.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 81-89; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 300-315.
# Corrected Actuator Metric

Lecture 4 supersedes the earlier lecture notes and gives the current normalized relationship as:

$$
C \approx \frac{Km^2\tau{sat}^2}{M I^2}
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6.

where:
- $Km = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{Rs}}$ is the motor constant
- $\tau{sat}$ is the torque limit from core saturation
- $M$ is copper mass
- $I$ is joint/output inertia

This formula is why the lecture says the four primary requirements are coupled.

For off-the-shelf frameless motor selection, $Km$ and $\tau{sat}$ are usually already coupled inside a motor family. In that practical case, the designer mainly chooses among torque, mass, and inertia.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 95-117.
# Iteration

Requirement selection is not a one-pass calculation. If a robot becomes too heavy or cannot meet the task:
- step back in the distal-to-proximal chain
- lower payload or total-mass goals
- choose better motors/materials/reducer topology
- improve manufacturing quality if the issue is friction/backlash rather than the primary motor trade-off

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 7.

pages.title: Analytical Torque Constant for SPMSM
pages.html:
Analytical Torque Constant for SPMSM #

Analytical torque-constant calculation estimates $K_t$ from motor geometry and magnet/winding assumptions before fabrication or full finite-element analysis. Lecture 5 derives the first-order SPMSM result:

$$ K_t = 3 k_w \frac{t}{g} B_r N R l $$

where $k_w$ is winding factor, $t$ is magnet thickness, $g$ is effective air gap, $B_r$ is magnet remanence, $2N$ is turns per phase, $R$ is air-gap radius, and $l$ is axial length.

Figure: SPMSM cross-section and analytical torque-constant variables. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20.

Purpose #

The design reason for this formula is speed and interpretability. Real testing requires building hardware, and FEM can become a black box during early search. A low-dimensional analytical model lets the designer see which geometry and material variables are actually controlling torque production.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 52-72 and 86-90.

Derivation Chain #

Lecture 5’s chain is:

  1. Start from motional EMF, $e=Blv$.
  2. Accumulate conductor-level EMF into phase flux linkage through a winding function.
  3. Use magnetic co-energy or virtual work to convert rotor-angle-dependent flux linkage into torque.
  4. Sum the three balanced phase torques under $i_d=0$.
  5. Correct the ideal winding with $k_w$.

The transcript gap covers part of the co-energy and flux-linkage derivation, so this chain is grounded partly in the slide sequence and partly in the resumed transcript.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 207-224 and 271-316; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 22-30.

Three-Phase Torque Result #

For the ideal winding and sinusoidal radial field model, the phase torque is written in the slide as:

$$ \tau_k #

-2 i_k R l B_m N \sin p(\theta-\theta_k) $$

with balanced q-axis current:

$$ i_k = -i_q \sin p(\theta-\theta_k) $$

Summing phases gives:

$$ \tau #

\sum_k \tau_k #

3B_m RlN i_q $$

Using $B_m \approx \frac{t}{g}B_r$ and adding $k_w$ gives the lecture result:

$$ \tau = K_t i_q, \qquad K_t = 3 k_w \frac{t}{g}B_rNRl $$

Figure: phase torque, balanced phase currents, and total torque result. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 359-386; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

Assumptions #

This is a first-order analytical model, not a replacement for FEM. The key assumptions are:

  • SPMSM-like surface magnet geometry.
  • Sinusoidal or fundamental-only radial air-gap flux waveform.
  • Effective magnet field amplitude approximated as $B_m \approx \frac{t}{g}B_r$.
  • Ideal or winding-factor-corrected winding linkage.
  • Balanced three-phase current with $i_d=0$ for the displayed torque result.
  • Lossless energy conversion in the derivation step.
  • Cogging torque, saliency/reluctance torque, saturation, leakage, fringing, and detailed magnetic-circuit effects are ignored or deferred.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 22-25 and 30-32; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 310-316 and 445-454.

Design Reading #

The formula says $K_t$ grows with useful air-gap radius, axial length, magnet field, and effective turns. Pole-pair count is not an independent free multiplier once the same effective conductor inventory is fixed. More poles can still improve real motors through shorter end turns, better feasible winding factors, thinner magnetic paths, and reduced mass, but those effects belong to winding and magnetic-circuit design rather than to a bare pole-count coefficient.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 388-424.

Related #

  • motor-constant
  • winding-function-and-factor
  • motional-emf-and-flux-linkage
  • spmsm-torque-equation
  • spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions

# Analytical Torque Constant for SPMSM

Analytical torque-constant calculation estimates $Kt$ from motor geometry and magnet/winding assumptions before fabrication or full finite-element analysis. Lecture 5 derives the first-order SPMSM result:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

where $kw$ is winding factor, $t$ is magnet thickness, $g$ is effective air gap, $Br$ is magnet remanence, $2N$ is turns per phase, $R$ is air-gap radius, and $l$ is axial length.

Figure: SPMSM cross-section and analytical torque-constant variables. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20.
# Purpose

The design reason for this formula is speed and interpretability. Real testing requires building hardware, and FEM can become a black box during early search. A low-dimensional analytical model lets the designer see which geometry and material variables are actually controlling torque production.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 52-72 and 86-90.
# Derivation Chain

Lecture 5's chain is:

1. Start from motional EMF, $e=Blv$.
2. Accumulate conductor-level EMF into phase flux linkage through a winding function.
3. Use magnetic co-energy or virtual work to convert rotor-angle-dependent flux linkage into torque.
4. Sum the three balanced phase torques under $id=0$.
5. Correct the ideal winding with $kw$.

The transcript gap covers part of the co-energy and flux-linkage derivation, so this chain is grounded partly in the slide sequence and partly in the resumed transcript.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 207-224 and 271-316; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 22-30.
# Three-Phase Torque Result

For the ideal winding and sinusoidal radial field model, the phase torque is written in the slide as:

$$
\tauk
=
-2 ik R l Bm N \sin p(\theta-\thetak)
$$

with balanced q-axis current:

$$
ik = -iq \sin p(\theta-\thetak)
$$

Summing phases gives:

$$
\tau
=
\sumk \tauk
=
3Bm RlN iq
$$

Using $Bm \approx \frac{t}{g}Br$ and adding $kw$ gives the lecture result:

$$
\tau = Kt iq,
\qquad
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g}BrNRl
$$

Figure: phase torque, balanced phase currents, and total torque result. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 359-386; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.
# Assumptions

This is a first-order analytical model, not a replacement for FEM. The key assumptions are:
- SPMSM-like surface magnet geometry.
- Sinusoidal or fundamental-only radial air-gap flux waveform.
- Effective magnet field amplitude approximated as $Bm \approx \frac{t}{g}Br$.
- Ideal or winding-factor-corrected winding linkage.
- Balanced three-phase current with $id=0$ for the displayed torque result.
- Lossless energy conversion in the derivation step.
- Cogging torque, saliency/reluctance torque, saturation, leakage, fringing, and detailed magnetic-circuit effects are ignored or deferred.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 22-25 and 30-32; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 310-316 and 445-454.
# Design Reading

The formula says $Kt$ grows with useful air-gap radius, axial length, magnet field, and effective turns. Pole-pair count is not an independent free multiplier once the same effective conductor inventory is fixed. More poles can still improve real motors through shorter end turns, better feasible winding factors, thinner magnetic paths, and reduced mass, but those effects belong to winding and magnetic-circuit design rather than to a bare pole-count coefficient.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 388-424.

pages.title: Backdrivability
pages.html:
Backdrivability #

Backdrivability is the ability to drive an actuator backward from the output side. In robot actuator discussions, the word is useful but ambiguous.

Lecture 4 explicitly separates three meanings:

Figure: Lecture 4 separates backdrivability into backward efficiency, Coulomb friction/reverse-drive starting torque, and reflected inertia. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.

Meaning 1: Backward Efficiency #

Forward efficiency measures how much input power becomes output power. Backward efficiency measures how well output-side mechanical power transmits back toward the motor.

These can differ. The lecture uses screw/bottle-cap intuition: turning the cap can move it axially, but pushing axially may not spin it backward. A mechanism can have nonzero forward efficiency and nearly zero backward efficiency.

Meaning 2: Reverse-Drive Starting Torque #

Reverse-drive starting torque is the output torque needed to start motion from rest. This is close to a Coulomb/static-friction idea.

It depends on preload, tooth contact, bearings, seals, lubrication, and assembly. It is not the same as backward efficiency under motion, although both are friction-related.

For QDD/proprioceptive control, this meaning is often the most important: if friction torque is small enough, the motor current can be used as a good proxy for output torque.

Meaning 3: Reflected Inertia #

Sometimes people include output-reflected rotor inertia in “backdrivability.” Lecture 4 recommends keeping it separate.

Reflected inertia is:

$$ J_{out}=N^2J_r $$

It affects impact response and dynamic motion, but it is not friction. This wiki uses backdrivability mainly for friction/reverse-drive behavior and discusses inertia separately.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239 and 317-345; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.

QDD Interpretation #

The lecture clarifies a common misconception. QDD does not fundamentally mean “the reduction ratio must be small.” It means the actuator should preserve the control idea of direct drive: output torque can be inferred without a dedicated torque sensor because friction and unmodeled transmission effects are small.

Low reduction ratio is a common way to achieve that, but not the only theoretical way. A high-ratio reducer with excellent low-friction design could still behave like a QDD actuator. In practice, high ratio makes this harder, so designers often impose a realistic reduction-ratio limit.

Related #

  • transmission-transparency
  • torque-mass-inertia-selection
  • robot-actuator-reducer-selection
  • legged-robot-actuation
  • actuator-performance-metrics

pages.title: BLDC Electronic Commutation
pages.html:
BLDC Electronic Commutation #

Electronic commutation means switching stator winding currents with electronics instead of using mechanical brushes and a commutator.

Brush-Type DC Motor vs BLDC #

In a brush-type DC motor:

  • brushes make sliding electrical contact
  • a mechanical commutator switches which rotor coils receive current
  • brush wear, dust, friction, electrical noise, and maintenance are real costs

In a BLDC motor:

  • the windings are typically on the stator
  • the rotor usually carries permanent magnets
  • a motor driver switches phase currents electronically
  • rotor position sensing or estimation tells the driver when to switch

Figure: BLDC rotor magnets and sensor IC arrangement. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 1.

Honeywell describes BLDC motors as brush-type motors “turned inside out”: power goes to stationary windings, while the permanent-magnet rotor rotates.

Six-Step Intuition #

In a common three-phase six-step BLDC drive:

  • two phases are energized at a time
  • one phase is left off/floating
  • the active phase pair creates a stator magnetic field
  • the driver changes the active pair as the rotor moves, using rotor-position feedback or estimation
  • the rotor magnets keep moving toward the next stator field orientation

Beginner analogy: the controller keeps creating the next magnetic “target” slightly ahead of the rotor, so the rotor keeps chasing it.

What the Driver Needs #

The motor driver needs rotor position or rotor sector information to energize the correct winding. It can get that from:

  • Hall effect sensors
  • encoders
  • sensorless estimation from electrical signals

Figure: simplified three-phase BLDC driver circuit using six power transistors and Hall-effect sensor IC feedback. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 4.

This is the control-facing reason why motor-actuator-structure says BLDC/SPMSM-style motors need a driver and commutation logic.

Relation to Winding #

Electronic commutation determines when current goes through each phase. Winding design determines the motor’s voltage/current matching and electromagnetic constraints. See bldc-pmsm-spmsm for the naming distinction between BLDC/PMSM/SPMSM.

Related #

  • bldc-commutation-and-hall-sensors-chatgpt
  • hall-effect-position-sensing
  • bldc-pmsm-spmsm
  • motor-actuator-structure

pages.title: BLDC Electronic Commutation — ChatGPT Notes
pages.html:
BLDC Electronic Commutation — ChatGPT Notes #

Source: sources/chatgpt-shares/BLDC Electronic Commutation - ChatGPT notes.md - user-provided shared ChatGPT conversation about BLDC commutation and Hall sensors.

Main Questions Covered #

  • What is an electronic commutation circuit?
  • What are DC and AC?
  • How can a BLDC controller continuously switch windings?
  • What is winding?
  • What are BLDC motor sensor ICs?
  • What are brushes and commutators?
  • How does the controller know rotor position?
  • Why does a BLDC rotor usually use permanent magnets rather than electromagnets?
  • What is a Hall sensor?
  • How do Hall signals map to coil switching?

Useful Explanation Captured #

The final ChatGPT explanation frames BLDC six-step commutation as:

  • Hall sensor signals identify rotor sector.
  • Controller chooses which two of three phases are energized.
  • One phase is positive, one negative, and one off in a common six-step scheme.
  • The energized stator field is moved ahead of the rotor.
  • The rotor permanent magnets keep “chasing” the next stator field position.

This is a useful beginner analogy for bldc-electronic-commutation, but should be treated as a learning note rather than an authoritative source.

Related #

  • bldc-electronic-commutation
  • hall-effect-position-sensing
  • bldc-pmsm-spmsm

- The energized stator field is moved ahead of the rotor.
- The rotor permanent magnets keep "chasing" the next stator field position.

This is a useful beginner analogy for bldc-electronic-commutation, but should be treated as a learning note rather than an authoritative source.
