searchIndex.title: Robot Actuator Reducer Selection
searchIndex.text:
# Robot Actuator Reducer Selection

Reducer selection chooses the transmission topology after the actuator's torque, mass, and inertia targets are roughly known.

Lecture 4 focuses on robot actuator reducers:

Figure: common robot actuator reducer types and reducer-level primary/secondary requirements. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 15.
- planetary drive
- cycloidal drive
- harmonic drive
# Reducer Requirements

Primary reducer requirements:
- Torque density: maximum transmissible torque per reducer mass
- Efficiency: output power divided by input power

Lecture 4 treats these as fundamentally traded off.

Secondary reducer requirements:
- backlash
- manufacturability
- packaging
- wiring path
- cost and precision

Backlash matters more in robot positioning than in many vehicle transmissions, but the lecture treats it as secondary because manufacturing precision, preload, thermal expansion, and wear dominate it.
# Why Not Just Use Serial Spur Gears?

A chain of external spur gears is simple, but it is poor for compact robot joints:
- input and output are not coaxial by default
- each gear mesh creates radial loads on shafts and bearings
- bearing radial load creates friction
- external gear contact is generally less efficient than internal gear contact
- backlash accumulates across stages
- packaging and wiring become awkward

Planetary, cycloidal, and harmonic reducers are attractive because they package reduction coaxially and can distribute load around the axis.
# Single-Stage Planetary Drive

Pros:
- high efficiency
- easy to design
- compact and familiar

Cons:
- limited reduction ratio, typically below about $10:1$
- limited torque density compared with high-contact-ratio reducers
- sun gear cannot be made arbitrarily small because of tooth count, undercut, and packaging/wiring limits

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, reducer-type notes around planetary drive selection; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 15.
# Two-Stage Planetary Drive

Pros:
- high efficiency compared with 3K compound planetary designs

Cons:
- still limited in practical reduction range
- many parts
- low torque density because multiple stages and bearings add mass

The output-side stage dominates mass because it carries the larger torque.
# 3K Compound Planetary Drive

Pros:
- wide reduction-ratio range
- higher torque density than a two-stage planetary drive at comparable reduction

Cons:
- lower efficiency than a two-stage planetary drive at similar design quality
- sensitive to detailed gear-tooth efficiency and backlash requirements

The lecture notes that high-quality designs, such as bilateral-drive-gear-style reducers, can be highly backdrivable, but measured performance depends strongly on backlash tolerance and manufacturing quality.
# Cycloidal Drive

Pros:
- high ideal efficiency if power is transmitted through rolling contacts
- potentially good torque density

Cons:
- requires very high manufacturing precision
- sensitive to tolerances and overconstraint
- difficult to build well in a lab without specialized manufacturing experience

The lecture's practical warning is strong: do not casually decide to make a cycloidal drive unless the team has the required machining and design capability.
# Harmonic Drive

Pros:
- very high torque density
- low backlash is possible
- many teeth share load

Cons:
- low efficiency compared with QDD-friendly reducers
- difficult flexspline design and manufacturing
- friction/preload can hurt reinforcement-learning or proprioceptive-control style robots

Harmonic drives were excellent for CNC-style position-controlled robot arms because high-gain position control could treat friction as a disturbance. Lecture 4 argues they fit less naturally with QDD-style robots that try to minimize friction and infer torque from motor current.
# Related
- actuator-requirement-selection
- torque-mass-inertia-selection
- backdrivability
- transmission-transparency
- legged-robot-actuation

searchIndex.title: Robot Hardware Iteration
searchIndex.text:
# Robot Hardware Iteration

Robot hardware iteration is the loop that turns a concept into a robot that can be manufactured, assembled, modeled, controlled, and demonstrated. It is not just "more CAD"; it includes tangible prototypes, analytic checks, specialized tests, drawings, URDF conversion, middleware, and demos.

The RAIPAL special lecture gives a concrete sequence:

1. compare concepts with rough but relevant metrics
2. make the concept tangible as quickly as possible
3. iterate CAD around mistakes and structural evidence
4. freeze a production-ready design only when time and money are worth spending
5. transfer manufacturing intentions into drawings
6. assemble prototypes and run basic tests
7. build the digital interface for simulation and policies
8. devise demonstrations that verify requirements

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 8-17.
# Broad Strokes First

The first CAD draft should preserve the concept's important topology while becoming tangible quickly. The point is not a finished part; it is to make the idea interactable so errors in packaging, motion, task space, assembly, and intuition appear early.

The RAIPAL lecture calls this "broad strokes first": concept to crude real-life version, tangible as soon as possible, with multiple iterations intended from the start. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 9; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 127-137.
# Catch Mistakes Quickly

The lecture's CAD iteration rule is blunt: first ideas are always terrible. The task is not to make perfect decisions from the beginning, but to expose mistakes quickly and convert them into improvements.

RAIPAL's structural iteration shows the pattern. The slide compares v0-1 with minimum safety factor 1.8 and roughly 15 mm maximum deformation at peak torque against v1-1 with minimum safety factor 3.2 and roughly 1 mm maximum deformation. The transcript explains that these were not exact full-assembly truth claims; the simulations were simplified enough to answer the design question quickly. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 10-11; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 139-177; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 182-193.
# Use Three Tool Types

The retrospective groups useful iteration tools into three categories:

Figure: tangible, analytic, and specialized tools used during RAIPAL iteration. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 28.
- Tangible: miniatures, prototypes, and physical manipulation expose geometry and assembly failures.
- Analytic: simple FEA and mass/inertia estimates catch structural or performance problems before spending production time.
- Specialized: dedicated variations and test fixtures answer narrow questions such as fits, bearings, backlash, thermal behavior, or actuator characterization.

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 28; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 88-95.
# Drawings Are Part of Design

Production drawings transfer design intentions into a common manufacturing language. They are not just administrative output. A drawing decides which dimensions, fits, tolerances, surfaces, and assembly relations must be preserved by the manufacturer.

The RAIPAL transcript notes that the act of making drawings can force another design iteration when the manufacturer cannot make the part or when the drawing cannot express the intended precision cleanly. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 14; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 232-240.
# Digital Interface Is Hardware Completion

For RL-oriented robot hardware, the iteration loop is incomplete until the robot can enter simulation and receive policies back through real middleware. RAIPAL's digital-interface work included URDF conversion, mass agreement around 5 g, rotor inertia calculation, real-time communication, and crossed-4-bar calculation through the middleware layer.

This connects directly to motor-operating-region and transmission-transparency: the hardware may be mechanically good, but a bad digital model or delayed middleware can create a sim-to-real gap large enough to hide that quality from the controller. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 16; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 9-30.
# Plan Around Real Time, Not Ideal Time

RAIPAL's development statistics show how expensive integration is:

Figure: RAIPAL time allocation by phase: concept, draft, design iteration, production, digital interface, and testing. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 25.

Design iteration was the largest named category at 30.6%, but production, digital interface, and testing together took more than half of the non-delay work. A separate chart shows delay at 54.5% of the calendar. The planning lesson is conservative: even a main hardware project may receive only part of a week after demos, unrelated lab work, proposal work, renovation, health, and coordination bottlenecks. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 25-26; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 42-69; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 81-84.
# Related

searchIndex.title: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
searchIndex.text:
# Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf - Sangok Seok, Albert Wang, David Otten, and Sangbae Kim, "Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control in Fast Legged Locomotion," IEEE/RSJ IROS 2012.
# Core Question

How should electromagnetic actuators be designed for fast legged locomotion, where the actuator must produce high force while staying mechanically transparent enough for force control without a foot force sensor?
# Main Claim

Power density is not the right design metric for legged-robot actuators. The more useful design target is high torque density with low mechanical impedance, especially low reflected inertia, friction, and damping. This enables transmission-transparency and proprioceptive force control.
# Why Power Density Misleads

The paper argues that EM motors can show very high power density because high power is available at high speed and relatively low torque. But if a robot joint needs large torque, simply adding high reduction increases actuator passive impedance:
- reflected inertia
- friction
- damping
- gear-train mass and complexity

The paper therefore prefers torque density and transmission transparency as actuator-level metrics, matching the lecture's actuator-performance-metrics argument.
# Gap Radius Scaling

The paper identifies gap radius $r{gap}$ as a critical motor design parameter. Under the paper's electromagnetic-similarity assumptions:
- Motor mass $\propto r{gap}$
- Motor torque $\propto r{gap}^2$
- Rotor inertia $\propto r{gap}^3$
- Torque density $\propto r{gap}$
- Acceleration capability, torque/inertia $\propto \frac{1}{r{gap}}$

The motor constant relation used for the scaling is:

$$
KM^2 = \frac{Kt^2}{R} = \frac{\tau^2}{I^2 R}
$$

For a given wire gauge, the paper gives $KM^2 \propto r{gap}^3$. Empirical motor data showed the same qualitative trend, though with manufacturing-dependent exponents.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Section II and Fig. 2.
# Reflected Inertia Thought Experiment

If output torque and motor mass are fixed:
- Doubling motor radius halves motor length.
- Motor torque doubles, so required gear ratio halves.
- Rotor inertia increases by $4\times$.
- Reflected rotor inertia through the gearbox scales by $\frac{1}{4}$, because reflected inertia depends on gear ratio squared.

So in the ideal model, total output torque and reflected rotor inertia can stay the same. But real high-ratio gearboxes add mass, inertia, and friction loss. This is why the paper recommends the largest feasible gap radius and the least gearing needed to meet the torque requirement.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Section II and Fig. 3.
# MIT Cheetah Front Leg Design

The prototype applies this analysis:
- Motor selected: largest radius fitting a 5 inch space constraint
- Gear ratio: 1:5.8, compared with more than 1:100 in many EM legged-robot actuators
- Desired peak joint torque: roughly 100 Nm
- Motor peak torque: 21 Nm
- Gearbox: one-stage planetary gearing with four planet gears
- Architecture: hip and knee motors/gear trains coaxially located at the hip; knee driven by steel linkage
- Structure: foam-core composite humerus/radius and tendon-reinforced foot to reduce leg inertia without sacrificing strength
- Shoulder module: 4.2 kg
- Humerus: 160 g
- Lower limb including foot: 300 g
- Whole-leg moment of inertia at shoulder in straight configuration: $0.058\ \mathrm{kg\,m^2}$

This design keeps drive components near the shoulder and minimizes distal leg inertia.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Section III and Figs. 1, 4, and 5.
# Proprioceptive Force Control Result

The paper tests whether low mechanical impedance enables force control from motor current and encoder position rather than a foot force sensor.

The leg is mounted on a material testing device, calibrated for torque-dependent friction, then tested with virtual spring control. In impact tests:
- Mean absolute error / full-scale sensor force = 0.041 for a 3406 N/m virtual stiffness
- Mean absolute error / full-scale sensor force = 0.049 for a 5038 N/m virtual stiffness

The result supports the paper's claim that a transparent, low-impedance actuator can produce useful proprioceptive force estimates during dynamic interaction.
# Takeaways for This Wiki
- legged-robot-actuation should treat low gearing as a practical route to transparency, not a standalone goal.
- actuator-design-principles should distinguish ideal reflected-inertia scaling from real gearbox penalties.
- motor-constant and torque-production efficiency connect strongly to gap radius and material/topology choices.
- transmission-transparency is the control-facing reason why actuator hardware choices matter.

searchIndex.title: Space Vector PWM
searchIndex.text:
# Space Vector PWM

Space vector PWM (SVPWM) is a modulation method for a three-phase inverter. It approximates a desired voltage vector by spending controlled fractions of each switching period in nearby inverter switch states.

In Lecture 3B, Juminchan's motor-driver seminar, SVPWM is the practical bridge between a d-q voltage command from motor-current-control and real MOSFET switching in motor-driver-inverter.

Source: seminar boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 12.

For the textbook source route, use chapter-07-pwm-inverters. Chapter 7 grounds the same lecture picture in voltage-source inverter switching states, modulation index, common-mode offset injection, overmodulation, dead time, and PWM-synchronized current measurement.
# Time-Averaged Voltage

Within one switching period $Ts$, SVPWM mixes active vectors and zero vectors. In one sector, the idea is:

$$
\mathbf V{ref}
=
\frac{1}{Ts}
\left(
T1\mathbf V1
+T2\mathbf V2
+T{0a}\mathbf V0
+T{0b}\mathbf V7
\right)
$$

where $\mathbf V1$ and $\mathbf V2$ are adjacent active vectors and $\mathbf V0$, $\mathbf V7$ are zero-voltage vectors.

The zero-vector time does not create net phase voltage, but it fills the switching period. Symmetric SVPWM splits this zero time so the pulses are distributed more evenly.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 12.

The textbook expresses the same volt-second balance as:

$$
\mathbf V^Ts = \mathbf VnT1 + \mathbf V{n+1}T2
$$

$$
T0=Ts-(T1+T2)
$$

The two zero states $(0,0,0)$ and $(1,1,1)$ do not apply effective load voltage, while the six active vectors have magnitude $\frac{2}{3}V{dc}$ and are spaced by 60 electrical degrees in the stationary d-q plane.

Figure: output voltage vectors for a three-phase voltage-source inverter in the stationary d-q plane. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 37, Fig. 7.38.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 37-41.
# Why Use Both Zero Vectors

The seminar emphasizes ripple. Current in the motor winding rises and falls according to the applied voltage pulse pattern. If the active-vector time is bunched together, current ripple increases. If the zero-vector time is split symmetrically around the active vectors, current ripple is reduced.

Lower current ripple means lower torque ripple.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-181; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 12.
# Voltage Hexagon and Normal Modulation

The active space vectors form a voltage hexagon. For smooth non-overmodulated operation, the commanded voltage vector should stay inside the hexagon's inscribed circle.

Figure: non-overmodulated SVPWM uses the inscribed circle inside the space-vector hexagon. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 13.

Under the lecture's convention, the phase-voltage magnitude limit used by the controller is:

$$
\sqrt{Vd^2 + Vq^2} \leq \frac{V{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

The active vector magnitude is shown as $\frac{2}{3}V{dc}$ because the motor neutral is floating, not tied to ground.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 13 and 25.
# Modulation Index and DC-Link Use

Chapter 7 defines modulation index as the ratio between the inverter phase-voltage fundamental peak and half the DC-link voltage:

$$
MI=\frac{V{1,peak}}{V{dc}/2}
$$

Sinusoidal PWM is linear up to $MI=1$, which is 78.5% of the six-step fundamental. Symmetrical SVPWM reaches the same linear voltage increase as third-harmonic injection, about 15.5% more than SPWM, and the normal linear boundary corresponds to 90.7% of six-step operation. That is the textbook basis for the lecture's practical voltage-margin warning.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 25-41.
# Common-Mode Offset View

SVPWM can also be implemented as carrier PWM plus a common-mode offset voltage. Adding the same offset to all three phase references changes pole-voltage duty cycles, but it cancels from line-to-line voltage in a floating-neutral three-phase motor. This is the common framework that connects SPWM, third-harmonic injection, symmetrical SVPWM, and discontinuous PWM.

Discontinuous PWM intentionally clamps one inverter leg to a DC rail for part of the electrical cycle. Its goal is usually lower switching loss; the trade is changed ripple and common-mode behavior.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 50-59.
# Overmodulation and Margin

Overmodulation uses voltage vectors outside the normal inscribed-circle region. Lecture 3B warns that it can make the motor vibrate or behave poorly, so practical drives usually leave a margin instead of commanding the full theoretical boundary.

The seminar's rule of thumb is to leave about 1-5% phase-voltage margin.

Chapter 7 separates two reasons a controller may enter overmodulation. Dynamic overmodulation handles transient current-loop demands, such as a speed-command or load-torque change. Steady-state overmodulation intentionally uses more inverter voltage near six-step operation, which can extend high-speed operation but breaks the linear voltage-command assumption and increases harmonics.

That margin has two consequences:
- it reduces the practical motor operating region
- it preserves sampling windows for low-side current sensing

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 185-205; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 13 and 16.

Textbook cross-check: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 60-67.

searchIndex.title: SPMSM d-q Voltage Equations
searchIndex.text:
# SPMSM d-q Voltage Equations

The d-q voltage equations are the compact rotor-frame model used to describe SPMSM electrical dynamics, draw the motor operating region, and build current control. For the homework derivation path from phase equations, see abc-to-dq-transform-derivation.

Figure: Lecture 2 target form of the SPMSM d-q voltage equations. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.
# Why d-q Coordinates Exist

A three-phase motor has A, B, and C terminals, but the phase voltages/currents are constrained by circuit laws. After removing the redundant degree of freedom, the electrical state can be represented with two coordinates.

The d-q frame rewrites the three-phase variables into two axes attached to the rotor frame:
- d-axis: direct axis, aligned with the rotor magnetic flux convention
- q-axis: quadrature axis, perpendicular to the d-axis

The exact sign and scale can vary by Park/Clarke transform convention, so do not mix equations from different books or software without checking definitions.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 12-13.
# Ideal SPMSM Rotor-Frame Model

For the lecture's ideal SPMSM model:

$$
v{ds}^{r} = Rs i{ds}^{r} + Ls\frac{d i{ds}^{r}}{dt} - \omegar Ls i{qs}^{r}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{r} = Rs i{qs}^{r} + Ls\frac{d i{qs}^{r}}{dt} + \omegar Ls i{ds}^{r} + \omegar\phif
$$

where:
- $Rs$ = phase resistance under the chosen convention
- $Ls$ = phase inductance under the chosen convention
- $\omegar$ or $\omegae$ = electrical angular velocity of the rotor frame
- $\phif$ = rotor permanent-magnet flux linkage
- superscript $r$ = rotor-frame quantity

The torque relation for the ideal surface-magnet case is:

$$
\tau{rotor} = \frac{3}{2}\frac{P}{2}\phif i{qs}^{r}
$$

and the electrical/mechanical speed relation is:

$$
\omegae = \frac{P}{2}\omegam
$$

where $P$ is the number of poles.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; torque equation cross-check: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.
# PMSM Flux-Linkage Cross-Check

Chapter 4 of Electric Motor Control derives the PMSM d-q model from the same rotating-frame voltage identity but emphasizes that the flux-linkage model depends on rotor structure. In the rotor reference frame, an IPMSM has:

$$
\lambdad^r = Ld id^r + \phif
$$

$$
\lambdaq^r = Lq iq^r
$$

The SPMSM model is the non-salient special case where $Ld \approx Lq$. This is the source of the lecture simplification $\lambdad=Ls id+\phif$ and $\lambdaq=Ls iq$.

Figure: IPMSM d-axis and q-axis equivalent circuits in the rotor reference frame. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 48, Fig. 4.27.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 46-48.
# How Later Textbook Chapters Use the Same Equations

Chapter 6 uses the speed-dependent terms as feedforward targets for motor-current-control:

$$
v{d,ff}^r=-\omegar Lq iq^r
$$

$$
v{q,ff}^r=\omegar Ld id^r+\omegar\phif
$$

Chapter 8 uses the steady-state version of the same equations to draw the PMSM voltage-limit boundary in the $id$-$iq$ plane. Chapter 9 uses the stator voltage and flux-linkage equations in the opposite direction: sensorless observers estimate rotor position or flux from measured current and estimated applied voltage. This is why parameter convention mistakes in this page propagate into current-control tuning, flux weakening, and sensorless control.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 15-17; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 21-23; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 12-13.
# What Makes It Different from Brushed DC

The key terms are the cross-coupling and back-EMF terms:
- $-\omegar Ls i{qs}^{r}$ in the d-axis voltage equation
- $+\omegar Ls i{ds}^{r}$ in the q-axis voltage equation
- $+\omegar\phif$ in the q-axis voltage equation

These are why an SPMSM operating boundary is not just the brushed DC parallelogram. If inductance and cross-coupling are ignored, the model collapses toward a brushed-DC-like form; if they are kept, the voltage limit becomes curved.

Figure: SPMSM d-q voltage equations and the motor operating region with and without field weakening. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 10.
# Field Weakening

In the ideal SPMSM torque equation, torque is mainly controlled by q-axis current. D-axis current can be used to reduce effective magnet flux at high speed, limiting back EMF and extending the feasible high-speed region.

Lecture 2 notes that field weakening is common in EV-style high-speed operation, but most robotics applications do not need to rely on it heavily because their joint-speed demands are usually lower.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 185-189; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 10.
# Parameter and Convention Cautions

The equations use $Rs$ and $Ls$ as phase quantities under a particular equivalent-circuit convention. A datasheet or measurement may report:
- phase resistance
- terminal-to-terminal resistance
- wye-equivalent values
- delta branch values

See motor-winding-configuration before mixing these into the d-q equations.

The lecture also asks whether d-q variables are real physical quantities like a measured phase resistance or convention-dependent coordinates. The practical answer is: their physical meaning is tied to the rotor field, but the numerical definitions include transform conventions chosen by the modeler.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 477-482; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 95-99.
# Linear-Model Caveat

These equations assume a linear magnetic model. Real iron saturates, so inductance and torque constants can change with current. Lecture 2 explicitly attributes high-current torque nonlinearity to the nonlinear $B$-$H$ curve and saturation, not to hysteresis by itself.

For torque mechanisms, see magnetic-reluctance-torque.

Lecture 3A revisits the same warning from the motor-driver side. The flux-linkage relation $\lambda(i)$ is not guaranteed to be linear; the usual constant-inductance form is the approximation. That matters when deriving torque constants, copper-loss formulas, and current-loop feedforward terms.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 192-215; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.

searchIndex.title: SPMSM Linear Magnetic Assumptions
searchIndex.text:
# SPMSM Linear Magnetic Assumptions

The d-q model is only as trustworthy as its magnetic assumptions. The safest habit is to keep the flux-linkage form first, then state exactly when it is simplified into constant-inductance equations.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 188-217; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.
# Exact After Definitions

These equations are exact after the circuit variables and transform convention are chosen:

$$
\mathbf v{abc}
=
Rs\mathbf i{abc}
- \frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}}{dt}
$$

Constant $Rs$ is still an approximation because copper resistance changes with temperature, but the circuit form itself is the right starting point.

The rotating-frame flux equation is also exact as a coordinate identity:

$$
vd
=
Rs id
- \frac{d\lambdad}{dt}
- \omegae\lambdaq
$$

$$
vq
=
Rs iq
- \frac{d\lambdaq}{dt}
- \omegae\lambdad
$$

The speed relation is exact for a synchronous machine with known pole-pair count:

$$
\omegae = p\omegam
$$

The torque expression from power/co-energy is exact in the flux-linkage form:

$$
\taum
=
\frac{3}{2}p
\left(\lambdad iq - \lambdaq id\right)
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 452-482; torque/flux-linkage discussion in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 352-353.
# Linear Assumptions

The compact SPMSM model assumes:

$$
\lambdad = Ls id + \phif
$$

$$
\lambdaq = Ls iq
$$

Then:

$$
\taum
=
\frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

and:

$$
vd
=
Rs id
- Ls\frac{d id}{dt}
- \omegae Ls iq
$$

$$
vq
=
Rs iq
- Ls\frac{d iq}{dt}
- \omegae Ls id
- \omegae\phif
$$

These are the useful study equations, but they rely on constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.
# Saturation Boundary

In the real nonlinear case, write flux linkage as:

$$
\lambdad = \lambdad(id, iq, \thetae)
$$

$$
\lambdaq = \lambdaq(id, iq, \thetae)
$$

Then the differential terms become partial-derivative terms:

$$
\frac{d\lambdad}{dt}
=
\frac{\partial\lambdad}{\partial id}\frac{d id}{dt}
- \frac{\partial\lambdad}{\partial iq}\frac{d iq}{dt}
- \frac{\partial\lambdad}{\partial \thetae}\omegae
$$

and similarly for $\lambdaq$.

This is the clean answer to the lecture prompt about saturation: the flux-linkage form remains valid, but replacing flux linkage with constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$ is the linear approximation.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 188-217; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.
# Study Implication

When current is small, a constant torque constant can be a useful local slope. At high current, the stator core can saturate, flux linkage is no longer linear in current, and the apparent torque constant changes. This also limits how literally current-control feedforward should trust constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$. The lecture explicitly distinguishes this from hysteresis: the key modeling problem here is the nonlinear $B$-$H$ curve and saturation.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 213-217.

searchIndex.title: SPMSM Torque Equation
searchIndex.text:
# SPMSM Torque Equation

The SPMSM torque equation explains why q-axis current is the main torque command in the ideal surface-magnet model. It is the torque side of the same d-q model used by spmsm-dq-voltage-equations and motor-current-control.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.
# General d-q Torque Form

With the amplitude-invariant convention used in the lecture notes, electrical power in balanced d-q variables is:

$$
pe
=
\frac{3}{2}
\left(vd id + vq iq\right)
$$

The electromechanical power term is:

$$
p{em}
=
\frac{3}{2}\omegae
\left(\lambdad iq - \lambdaq id\right)
$$

Since $p{em} = \taum \omegam$ and $\omegae = p\omegam$:

$$
\taum
=
\frac{3}{2}p
\left(\lambdad iq - \lambdaq id\right)
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; convention warning in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 95-99.
# Ideal SPMSM Cancellation

For ideal SPMSM:

$$
\lambdad = Ls id + \phif
$$

$$
\lambdaq = Ls iq
$$

Then:

$$
\taum
=
\frac{3}{2}p
\left((Ls id + \phif)iq - Ls iq id\right)
$$

The $Ls id iq$ terms cancel:

$$
\taum
=
\frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

Using pole count $P$ instead of pole-pair count $p$:

$$
\taum
=
\frac{3}{2}\frac{P}{2}\phif iq
$$

This is why the ideal SPMSM torque command is mostly a q-axis current command.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

Chapter 5 gives the vector-control consequence: for an SPMSM in the constant-torque region, maximum torque per ampere is achieved by putting all allowed stator current on the q-axis:

$$
id^{r}=0
$$

That rule is not a universal high-speed rule. Once voltage is the active limit, flux weakening may require negative d-axis current even in an SPMSM.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 35-36; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23.
# Salient PMSM Extension

For a salient PMSM where $Ld \ne Lq$:

$$
\lambdad = Ld id + \phif,
\quad
\lambdaq = Lq iq
$$

Then:

$$
\taum
=
\frac{3}{2}p
\left(
\phif iq + (Ld - Lq)id iq
\right)
$$

The second term is reluctance torque. For ideal SPMSM, $Ld \approx Lq$, so the reluctance term is small.

The textbook's PMSM chapter derives the same structure from d-q power. In its notation, the IPMSM torque separates into a permanent-magnet term and a saliency term; setting $Ld=Lq$ collapses the model to the SPMSM magnet-torque-only approximation.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; torque-mechanism background in sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slides 3-4; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 48-49.

Chapter 5 extends this into IPMSM MTPA: with $Lq>Ld$, a negative d-axis current can make the saliency term assist the magnet term. The current command is therefore a lookup or calculation from the motor's flux linkage, inductance, saturation, and temperature behavior, not simply "$id=0$ plus q-axis torque."

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 38-44; see also chapter-05-vector-control.
# Linear-Model Boundary

The flux-linkage torque form is the safer canonical expression. The simplified $\taum = \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq$ relation assumes the ideal linear SPMSM flux model. Once saturation or saliency matters, use the flux-linkage form and check spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions before trusting constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.

searchIndex.title: Three Principles of Actuator Design
searchIndex.text:
# Three Principles of Actuator Design
# Principle 1: Max Torque is Independent of Winding

If stator and rotor dimensions are fixed, the way you wind the coils (thick wire with few turns vs thin wire with many turns) does not affect theoretical torque capability.

Figure: winding changes $N$ and current matching, but fixed stator-rotor geometry constrains the ideal torque capability. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 16.

Why: Magnetic field strength $H \propto NI$ (turns times current). If total copper cross-section is the same, the product $NI$ is the same regardless of winding configuration.

The listening notes usefully clarify the beginner intuition: turns and current trade off because the winding slot has finite space, more turns increase wire length/resistance, and thicker wire allows more current but consumes more slot area. The lecture's stronger claim is that this trade-off leaves the ideal torque capability unchanged when geometry/materials are fixed.

$$KM = \frac{\tau{rotor}}{\sqrt{I^2 R}}$$
- $\tau{rotor} \propto N$
- $R \propto \frac{L}{A} \propto N^2$ (for same total copper volume)
- Therefore $KM$ is independent of $N$ and $A$ (winding parameters)

To actually increase max torque: use stronger permanent magnets, or increase motor size (more volume, more copper).

Relevant geometry from motor-actuator-structure:
- Air gap: the stator-rotor gap where magnetic force is transmitted; larger gap increases magnetic reluctance and weakens torque.
- End turn: coil length outside the active slot region; it adds resistance/heat without producing torque.
# Principle 2: Winding is a Driver-Matching Problem

Winding configuration doesn't change ideal motor performance — it optimizes the motor for the available motor driver's voltage/current range. For BLDC/SPMSM-style motors, that same driver also handles electronic commutation using rotor-position feedback such as Hall sensors or encoders.

Figure: high-turn/small-diameter vs low-turn/big-diameter winding as a voltage/current matching problem. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 20.

| Configuration | $Kt$ | $R$ | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel / Delta / Low turns, big diameter | Low | Low | High-current driver |
| Series / Wye / High turns, small diameter | High | High | High-voltage driver |

The motor constant $Km = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{R}}$ remains the same either way.
# Principle 3: Given Fixed Mass and Max Torque, Joint Inertia is Determined

The most critical principle. Once you fix total mass and output max torque, the output inertia is theoretically fixed — regardless of motor shape.

Figure: pizza vs kimbap motor shapes can meet the same joint torque with different gear ratios under the ideal scaling argument. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22.

Pizza motor (flat, wide, large rgap):
- High motor torque → low gear ratio needed
- Higher rotor inertia, but low $N^2$ reflection

Kimbap motor (long, narrow, small rgap):
- Low motor torque → high gear ratio needed
- Lower rotor inertia, but high $N^2$ reflection

Result: same theoretical output inertia at same technology level.
# But in Practice...
- Kimbap advantage: longer axial length → less end-turn waste → higher Km (better electrical efficiency)
- Pizza advantage: low gear ratio → simpler gearbox → less friction, less added inertia → better backdrivability

> High-reduction gearboxes add real-world penalties (mass, inertia, friction) that break the ideal scaling argument. Pizza motor + low reduction is the easier path to good control performance, not an absolute theoretical law.

The transcript explicitly warns that high reduction can still work if the gearbox/transmission is designed well enough. The lecture cites the idea that a poor 100:1 reducer may lose 40-50% to friction while a high-quality 100:1 design might approach 85-90% efficiency; low reduction mainly makes acceptable friction easier to achieve.

Lecture 4 sharpens the same point with backdrivability terminology. QDD is not fundamentally the statement "small gear ratio." It is the statement that friction and transmission uncertainty are small enough that torque can be inferred from motor current without a dedicated output torque sensor. Low reduction is the common practical route because it makes that condition easier to satisfy.

Seok et al.'s paper provides the clean scaling thought experiment behind this lecture claim: if motor radius is doubled and length is halved while motor mass and output torque are held fixed, motor torque doubles and required gear ratio halves. Rotor inertia increases $4\times$, but reflected rotor inertia through the gear ratio decreases by $\frac{1}{4}$, so ideal reflected inertia stays the same. The practical reason to prefer larger gap radius is that lower required gearing reduces real gearbox mass, inertia, and friction.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 250-264; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 317-345.
# Appendix: Basic Design Parameters

The slide appendix organizes practical actuator design knobs into:
- Stator-rotor dimensions: gap radius, thickness, length
- Reducer: reducer type, reduction ratio
- Winding: delta/wye, serial/parallel, coil diameter/turns

The three design principles explain why these knobs do not independently improve every metric: geometry/materials set ideal torque capability, winding mostly matches the driver, and reducer choice trades torque/speed against reflected inertia and real gearbox losses.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31.

searchIndex.title: Torque-Mass-Inertia Selection
searchIndex.text:
# Torque-Mass-Inertia Selection

Torque-mass-inertia (T-M-I) selection is the Lecture 4 workflow for turning robot-level requirements into actuator-level specs.

The lecture's practical ordering is:

1. torque
2. mass
3. inertia

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 150-155.
# Distal-to-Proximal Workflow

For a QDD-based anthropomorphic manipulator, Lecture 4 proposes choosing actuator specs from the end effector back toward the base.

Figure: example distal-to-proximal requirement procedure for a QDD-based anthropomorphic manipulator. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 7.

The rough sequence is:

1. define kinematic parameters such as link lengths and joint ranges
2. set rated and peak payload
3. define wrist actuator T-M-I
4. define forearm mass
5. define elbow actuator T-M-I
6. define upper-arm mass
7. define shoulder actuator T-M-I

After each distal component is chosen, its mass becomes part of the load for the next proximal actuator.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 7.
# RAIPAL Concept Comparison Example

The RAIPAL mechanism lecture applies the same T-M-I logic before detailed CAD. Its FAST / SIMPLE / LINKED concept table compares each topology using total mass, supporting mass per joint, supporting center-of-mass distance, worst-case holding torque, projected inertia, and worst-case payload.

Figure: RAIPAL compares three early arm concepts using mass, inertia, holding torque, and payload estimates before committing to detailed mechanism CAD. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8.

The slide reports FAST at 8.5 kg with projected inertia <0.30,0.48,0.24> kgm^2 and 7.8 kg worst-case payload, SIMPLE at 7.7 kg with <0.42,0.52,0.10> kgm^2 and 6.9 kg payload, and LINKED at 7.9 kg with <0.33,0.43,0.11> kgm^2 and 7.2 kg payload. The transcript frames this as a rough design-space reduction step: external arm case studies rarely expose exact mass and inertia data, so the comparison used approximate same-assumption modeling rather than exact reverse engineering.

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 113-126; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 197-217.
# Torque From Payload

Torque requirements come from payload and child-link mass. The lecture uses the standard Jacobian relation:

$$
\boldsymbol\tau = J^T\mathbf F
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 10.

Figure: rated/peak actuator torque is set from payload, child-link mass, and the configurations considered. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 10.

The subtle question is which configuration set to design for:
- Fixed-base high-precision manipulator: often design for worst configuration inside the workspace.
- Mobile anthropomorphic manipulator: can design around favorable configurations because the body/base can move to make the task easier.

Rated torque is the torque that can be sustained thermally. Peak torque is the short-duration transient torque. The lecture gives a rough heuristic that peak torque is often about $300-400\%$ of rated torque, but the actual ratio depends on motor saturation, thermal path, and vendor definition.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 10; rated/peak definitions in sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 107-109.
# Mass Selection

For the static QDD manipulator case, Lecture 4 assumes off-the-shelf frameless motors and static rather than highly dynamic behavior.

The practical rules are:
- lighter motors are better once torque is satisfied
- maximize reduction ratio within the chosen QDD/friction limit
- check joint velocity under the given battery voltage
- account for full actuator mass, not only motor mass

The reason full actuator mass matters is that each proximal joint must lift all distal motors, reducers, housings, links, and payload.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 11.
# Reducer Mass Is Mostly Output-Torque Driven

The lecture argues that reducer mass is dominated by the output stage that must withstand peak joint torque. A $100:1$ reducer and a $10:1$ reducer can be similar in mass if both must output the same peak torque, because the high-output-torque stage dominates the structure.

This lets a first-pass workflow separate:
- motor mass selection from off-the-shelf frameless motor choice
- reducer mass estimation from peak output torque

It is an approximation, but it keeps the early design problem tractable.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 223-229; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 11.
# Inertia and Reduction Ratio

Once the frameless motor is chosen, rotor inertia $Jr$ is fixed. The output-reflected inertia is:

$$
J{out} = N^2Jr
$$

where $N$ is reduction ratio.

For static applications, inertia is often secondary, but ignoring it completely produces fragile or sluggish robots. Excessive output inertia makes impacts harder to absorb and can break gear teeth instead of letting the joint move.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 12.
# Dynamic 1-DOF Rule

For a 1-DOF system with fixed link inertia $J{link}$ and rotor inertia $Jr$, output acceleration is maximized when:

Figure: in a 1-DOF system, maximum acceleration occurs when reflected rotor inertia equals link inertia. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 13.

$$
N^2Jr = J{link}
$$

This gives a critical reduction ratio:

$$
N{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{J{link}}{Jr}}
$$

Below this ratio, both static performance and dynamic acceleration can be worse. Above this ratio, static torque improves, but dynamic acceleration starts to trade off.

For multi-DOF manipulators, the lecture presents this as intuition rather than a solved full-system rule. The multi-joint version depends on configuration-dependent inertia and is left as future work.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 13.

searchIndex.title: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
searchIndex.text:
# Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control

Transmission transparency is the degree to which forces generated by the actuator are faithfully transmitted to the end effector, and external forces at the end effector are faithfully "felt" by the actuator.

It is closely related to backdrivability and low passive impedance.
# What Reduces Transparency

Transmission transparency is degraded by:
- reflected rotor inertia
- gear-train inertia
- friction
- damping
- backlash and compliance
- non-collocated force sensing with unmodeled modes

For legged-robot-actuation, these effects are especially damaging because ground contact is frequent, fast, and impulsive.
# Why It Matters

If the actuator/transmission has low enough mechanical impedance, motor torque can approximate end-effector force through the robot Jacobian:

$$\tau \approx J^T F{ext} + fc(q,\tau)$$

where $fc$ captures torque-dependent Coulomb friction. This makes proprioceptive force control possible: the robot estimates contact force from internal signals such as motor current and encoder position, without requiring a force sensor at the foot.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Sections I-II and force-control model discussion.
# Seok et al. 2012 Result

Seok et al. designed the MIT Cheetah front leg to maximize transparency:

Figure: force-displacement and impact force-control results comparing motor-current estimates to force-sensor measurements. Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Fig. 8.
- large-radius, high-torque motors
- low reduction ratio, 1:5.8
- one-stage planetary gearing
- motors and gear trains located at the hip
- lightweight distal leg structure
- friction characterized and compensated experimentally

In impact tests, compensated force estimated from motor current matched force-sensor measurements with mean absolute errors of 0.041 and 0.049 as a ratio of full-scale sensor force for virtual stiffness settings of 3406 N/m and 5038 N/m.
# Design Implication

The point of QDD-style hardware is not merely "use a small gear ratio." The point is to reduce passive impedance enough that the actuator behaves transparently for force control and survives impact. Low gear ratio is one practical route; a high-ratio transmission can compete only if it remains efficient, low-friction, backdrivable, and impact-tolerant.

Lecture 4 clarifies the terminology: use "backdrivability" mainly for friction/reverse-drive behavior, not as a synonym for reflected inertia. Reflected inertia should be discussed separately as:

$$
J{out}=N^2Jr
$$

Both low friction and low reflected inertia improve transparency, but they are different design problems.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 300-345; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.

searchIndex.title: Understanding Hall Effect Sensors in BLDC Motors
searchIndex.text:
# Understanding Hall Effect Sensors in BLDC Motors

Source: sources/web-clippings/Understanding the Hall Effect Sensors in BLDC Motors.md - MECHTEX clipping, "Understanding the Hall Effect Sensors in BLDC Motors."
# Main Points from Source
- BLDC motors avoid brushes and mechanical commutators by using electronic commutation.
- Hall effect sensors detect rotor position and provide feedback to the controller.
- The controller uses rotor-position feedback to energize the correct stator winding at the correct time.
- In a typical three-phase BLDC motor, three Hall sensors are often arranged with a fixed angular relationship so the controller can infer rotor sector.
- Benefits:
- accurate rotor-position feedback
- reliable commutation
- speed control
- direction sensing
- durability and low maintenance
- Limitations:
- high-speed response limitations
- temperature sensitivity
- magnetic interference
- additional packaging/space/wiring complexity

searchIndex.title: Winding Function and Winding Factor
searchIndex.text:
# Winding Function and Winding Factor

The winding function is a compact way to turn a physical slot/conductor layout into a flux-linkage calculation. Lecture 5 uses it to move from conductor-level motional EMF to the phase torque result for an SPMSM.
# Conductor Density

The conductor density function $n(\phi)$ counts conductors per angular interval. If $d\phi$ is a small angular slice at angular position $\phi$, then:

$$
\text{conductors in } d\phi = n(\phi)d\phi
$$

In the idealized slot model, $n(\phi)$ can contain impulse-like positive and negative conductor groups. This is inconvenient for flux linkage, so the lecture introduces the winding function $N(\phi)$ as the more useful integrated representation.

Figure: conductor density and winding function for the simplified SPMSM cross-section. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 26.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 26.
# Flux-Linkage Overlap

For the simplified radial air-gap model, the phase flux linkage can be written as an overlap integral:

$$
\lambda(\theta)
=
R l \int0^{2\pi}
Br(\phi-\theta)N(\phi)\,d\phi
$$

Only the radial component $Br$ is kept because the simplified model is counting flux crossing the air-gap surface and linking axial conductors. As the rotor moves, the magnet-field waveform shifts relative to the winding function. Larger aligned overlap gives larger flux linkage.

Figure: radial magnet field waveform shifted against the winding function. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 29.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 281-316; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 29-30.
# Winding Factor

The winding factor $kw$ is the ratio between the useful fundamental EMF/torque contribution of a real winding and the ideal concentrated full-pitch winding. A winding factor of 1 means the conductor EMF contributions add without cancellation in the modeled fundamental. A lower winding factor means some of the conductor contributions partially cancel.

Lecture 5 first derives the ideal case, then inserts $kw$ into the final torque-constant formula:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 335-353 and 382-386; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 20 and 32.
# Pole Count Is Not a Free Torque Multiplier

The lecture warns against saying "more pole pairs directly increase torque constant." In the ideal formula, pole-pair count drops out when the total effective conductor count and linkage are fixed. More poles can still improve a real motor by allowing shorter end turns, better practical winding choices, thinner magnetic paths, and lower mass, but that is a packaging and winding-realization advantage rather than a direct independent multiplier in the ideal $Kt$ formula.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 388-424.

searchIndex.title: Wye-Delta Parameter Conventions
searchIndex.text:
# Wye-Delta Parameter Conventions

Wye-delta parameter conventions matter because a motor's internal winding topology and its external ABC-terminal equivalent are not the same question. For modeling and control, the practical requirement is to use one consistent equivalent-circuit convention.

For the basic winding/topology explanation, see motor-winding-configuration.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5.
# Terminal Equivalence

For ideal balanced linear passive branches, external ABC-terminal tests cannot uniquely distinguish wye from delta. A wye network and a delta network can have the same terminal behavior.

Practical answer:
- If only the three external ABC terminals are available and the motor behaves like an ideal balanced linear three-terminal network, no terminal-only test proves whether the internal winding is physically wye or delta.
- If the neutral point is externally accessible, the answer changes because wye exposes a node that delta does not.
- Real motors may show nonideal differences from saturation, parasitic capacitance, circulating harmonic currents, manufacturing asymmetry, or temperature gradients, but those effects do not give a clean general topology proof.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 51-59.
# Resistance Conversion

For equal wye phase resistance $Rs$, a line-to-line terminal measurement is:

$$
R{ab,\text{wye}} = 2Rs
$$

For equal delta branch resistance $R{\Delta}$, the line-to-line terminal resistance is:

$$
R{ab,\text{delta}}
=
R{\Delta}
\parallel
\left(R{\Delta} + R{\Delta}\right)
=
\frac{2}{3}R{\Delta}
$$

Equating the external terminal measurement:

$$
2Rs
=
\frac{2}{3}R{\Delta}
$$

So:

$$
Rs = \frac{R{\Delta}}{3}
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.
# Inductance Conversion Caveat

The same equivalence habit can be used for terminal inductance under a simplified linear scalar model:

$$
2Ls
=
\frac{2}{3}L{\Delta}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Ls = \frac{L{\Delta}}{3}
$$

Use this carefully. Real motor inductance includes mutual inductance, rotor-position dependence, saliency, saturation, measurement frequency, and controller transform convention. A datasheet value must be classified before it is inserted into spmsm-dq-voltage-equations.

Source: equivalent-circuit prompt in sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 243-253; Lecture 3A equivalent-circuit conclusion in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 51-59.

headingPath: Principle 1: Max Torque is Independent of Winding
chatChunks.text:
Principle 1: Max Torque is Independent of Winding

If stator and rotor dimensions are fixed, the way you wind the coils (thick wire with few turns vs thin wire with many turns) does not affect theoretical torque capability.

Figure: winding changes $N$ and current matching, but fixed stator-rotor geometry constrains the ideal torque capability. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 16.

Why: Magnetic field strength $H \propto NI$ (turns times current). If total copper cross-section is the same, the product $NI$ is the same regardless of winding configuration.

The listening notes usefully clarify the beginner intuition: turns and current trade off because the winding slot has finite space, more turns increase wire length/resistance, and thicker wire allows more current but consumes more slot area. The lecture's stronger claim is that this trade-off leaves the ideal torque capability unchanged when geometry/materials are fixed.

$$KM = \frac{\tau{rotor}}{\sqrt{I^2 R}}$$
- $\tau{rotor} \propto N$
- $R \propto \frac{L}{A} \propto N^2$ (for same total copper volume)
- Therefore $KM$ is independent of $N$ and $A$ (winding parameters)

To actually increase max torque: use stronger permanent magnets, or increase motor size (more volume, more copper).

headingPath: Principle 1: Max Torque is Independent of Winding
chatChunks.text:
Relevant geometry from motor-actuator-structure:
- Air gap: the stator-rotor gap where magnetic force is transmitted; larger gap increases magnetic reluctance and weakens torque.
- End turn: coil length outside the active slot region; it adds resistance/heat without producing torque.

headingPath: Principle 2: Winding is a Driver-Matching Problem
chatChunks.text:
Principle 2: Winding is a Driver-Matching Problem

Winding configuration doesn't change ideal motor performance — it optimizes the motor for the available motor driver's voltage/current range. For BLDC/SPMSM-style motors, that same driver also handles electronic commutation using rotor-position feedback such as Hall sensors or encoders.

Figure: high-turn/small-diameter vs low-turn/big-diameter winding as a voltage/current matching problem. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 20.

| Configuration | $Kt$ | $R$ | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel / Delta / Low turns, big diameter | Low | Low | High-current driver |
| Series / Wye / High turns, small diameter | High | High | High-voltage driver |

The motor constant $Km = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{R}}$ remains the same either way.

headingPath: Principle 3: Given Fixed Mass and Max Torque, Joint Inertia is Determined
chatChunks.text:
Principle 3: Given Fixed Mass and Max Torque, Joint Inertia is Determined

The most critical principle. Once you fix total mass and output max torque, the output inertia is theoretically fixed — regardless of motor shape.

Figure: pizza vs kimbap motor shapes can meet the same joint torque with different gear ratios under the ideal scaling argument. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22.

Pizza motor (flat, wide, large rgap):
- High motor torque → low gear ratio needed
- Higher rotor inertia, but low $N^2$ reflection

Kimbap motor (long, narrow, small rgap):
- Low motor torque → high gear ratio needed
- Lower rotor inertia, but high $N^2$ reflection

Result: same theoretical output inertia at same technology level.

But in Practice...
- Kimbap advantage: longer axial length → less end-turn waste → higher Km (better electrical efficiency)
- Pizza advantage: low gear ratio → simpler gearbox → less friction, less added inertia → better backdrivability

> High-reduction gearboxes add real-world penalties (mass, inertia, friction) that break the ideal scaling argument. Pizza motor + low reduction is the easier path to good control performance, not an absolute theoretical law.

The transcript explicitly warns that high reduction can still work if the gearbox/transmission is designed well enough. The lecture cites the idea that a poor 100:1 reducer may lose 40-50% to friction while a high-quality 100:1 design might approach 85-90% efficiency; low reduction mainly makes acceptable friction easier to achieve.

Lecture 4 sharpens the same point with backdrivability terminology. QDD is not fundamentally the statement "small gear ratio." It is the statement that friction and transmission uncertainty are small enough that torque can be inferred from motor current without a dedicated output torque sensor. Low reduction is the common practical route because it makes that condition easier to satisfy.

Seok et al.'s paper provides the clean scaling thought experiment behind this lecture claim: if motor radius is doubled and length is halved while motor mass and output torque are held fixed, motor torque doubles and required gear ratio halves. Rotor inertia increases $4\times$, but reflected rotor inertia through the gear ratio decreases by $\frac{1}{4}$, so ideal reflected inertia stays the same. The practical reason to prefer larger gap radius is that lower required gearing reduces real gearbox mass, inertia, and friction.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 250-264; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 317-345.

Appendix: Basic Design Parameters

The slide appendix organizes practical actuator design knobs into:
- Stator-rotor dimensions: gap radius, thickness, length
- Reducer: reducer type, reduction ratio
- Winding: delta/wye, serial/parallel, coil diameter/turns

The three design principles explain why these knobs do not independently improve every metric: geometry/materials set ideal torque capability, winding mostly matches the driver, and reducer choice trades torque/speed against reflected inertia and real gearbox losses.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31.

headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
chatChunks.text:
Actuator Performance Metrics

Lecture 4 refines the actuator primary requirements to four coupled quantities:
- mass
- rated torque
- joint/output inertia
- peak or saturation torque

For off-the-shelf frameless motor selection, the lecture says this often reduces in practice to torque, mass, and inertia because motor constant and saturation torque are coupled inside a motor family. Power and max speed are not valid standalone primary metrics because they can be shifted by winding, driver voltage/current, and gear ratio.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 5-6.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
chatChunks.text:
1. Mass

Total weight of the actuator assembly.

Scaling law:
- Volume $\propto L^3$, magnetic shear area $\propto L^2$, torque arm $\propto L$
- $\tau \propto$ shear stress $\times$ area $\times r \propto L^2 \times L = L^3$? No — torque scales roughly as $L^2$ relative to mass scaling of $L^3$
- $10\times$ size → $1000\times$ mass, roughly $100\times$ torque → torque/mass ratio worsens with scale

Simply making a motor bigger gives diminishing returns.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-102.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
chatChunks.text:
2. Rated Torque and Peak Torque

Lecture 4 separates two torque limits:
- Rated torque: continuous or steady-state torque, limited mainly by heat dissipation under a defined thermal condition.
- Peak / saturation torque: short-duration torque limited by magnetic saturation when heating is ignored or treated as transient.

Rated torque is tied to copper heat:
- Copper heat loss: $P{cu} = I^2R$
- Steady-state thermal equilibrium: $P{cu} = hA\Delta T$
- Heat generation $\propto L^3$ (volume), heat dissipation $\propto L^2$ (surface area)
- Larger motors are harder to cool — max torque is thermally constrained

Peak torque is tied to the nonlinear magnetic limit. Beyond saturation, additional current no longer produces proportional torque.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 104-114; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 107-109.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
chatChunks.text:
3. Joint Inertia (Output Inertia)

The rotational inertia felt at the output shaft.

$$J{out} = N^2 \times Jm + J{gear}$$

Where:
- $J{out}$ = output-side or joint-side inertia of the actuator's internal rotating parts.
- $N$ = reduction ratio: the motor turns $N$ times for one output/joint turn.
- $Jm$ = motor rotor inertia about the motor shaft, not total motor mass.
- $J{gear}$ = gearbox/reducer rotating inertia as seen at the output side.
- Gear ratio $10\times$ → reflected inertia $100\times$ increase
- Power is maintained, torque increases, but backdrivability degrades severely
- This is the fundamental reason QDD (Quasi-Direct Drive) avoids high gear ratios

The formula is usually about the actuator's internal inertia, not the external robot link/load inertia. A larger system model may add the link/load inertia separately.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 116-138.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
chatChunks.text:
Reference Frame Clarification

"Output inertia," "joint inertia," and "total reflected rotor inertia" are often used inconsistently. In this lecture's main design argument, the important quantity is the inertia felt at the joint/output, especially motor rotor inertia reflected through the reducer:

$$J{rotor \rightarrow out} \approx N^2 Jm$$

The inverse statement is also true when looking from the motor side: a load inertia behind a reducer appears smaller to the motor by roughly $\frac{1}{N^2}$. These are not contradictory; they use opposite reference frames.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 118-124.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
chatChunks.text:
Key Insight

> Using power or max angular velocity as performance metrics is a misconception. Electric motors (unlike combustion engines) have no theoretical power limit — you can always adjust the operating region via gear ratio. The three metrics above are what actually matter.

Lecture 4 sharpens this point: velocity/power is a secondary requirement for actuator selection, not a primary physical trade-off like mass, rated torque, peak torque, and inertia. It still must be checked against winding and driver voltage/current limits, especially when using off-the-shelf motors.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 5.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
Why "Bigger Motor + Bigger Gearbox" Is Not Free
chatChunks.text:
Why "Bigger Motor + Bigger Gearbox" Is Not Free

Two scaling traps explain why actuator design becomes a Pareto trade-off instead of a simple "make it stronger" problem.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
1. Motor Scale: Mass Grows Very Fast
chatChunks.text:
1. Motor Scale: Mass Grows Very Fast

If every linear dimension of a motor scales by $L$, its volume and mass scale roughly as:

$$Mass \propto Volume \propto L^3$$

So a motor that is $10\times$ larger in every direction has roughly:

$$10^3 = 1000\times$$

more mass.

Many useful physical limits, such as magnetic shear area or heat-dissipation area, scale more like area:

$$Area \propto L^2$$

So a $10\times$ length scale gives only about:

$$10^2 = 100\times$$

more area.

The practical intuition: a bigger motor becomes heavy very quickly, while some of the useful electromagnetic or thermal capability does not grow as cheaply. Scaling up is therefore a trade-off, not a free improvement.

> QUESTION
> The exact torque scaling depends on which dimensions are scaled and which constraints are held fixed. The safe takeaway from Lecture 1 is not "torque is always exactly $L^2$," but "mass grows aggressively with size, so motor scaling has a real mass penalty."
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-102.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
2. Gear Ratio: Reflected Inertia Grows Quadratically
chatChunks.text:
2. Gear Ratio: Reflected Inertia Grows Quadratically

A reducer with ratio $N$ means the motor spins $N$ times for one output/joint rotation.

For a $10:1$ reduction:
- motor rotates 10 turns
- joint rotates 1 turn
- output torque is about $10\times$ motor torque

But reflected motor inertia at the joint is:

$$J{rotor \rightarrow out} \approx N^2Jm$$

This comes from kinetic energy:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}J\omega^2$$

If the joint speed is $\omega$, the motor rotor speed is $N\omega$:

$$Em = \frac{1}{2}Jm(N\omega)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(N^2Jm)\omega^2$$

From the joint's point of view, the motor rotor behaves like it has $N^2Jm$ inertia.

Examples:
- $10:1$ reduction → $10\times$ torque but $100\times$ reflected rotor inertia
- $100:1$ reduction → $100\times$ torque but $10{,}000\times$ reflected rotor inertia

This is why high reduction can make a legged robot feel heavy, non-backdrivable, inefficient during impact, and easier to damage. See legged-robot-actuation and transmission-transparency.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 118-138.

The slide deck makes this concrete with a power-density trap:
- Small motor: mass = 38g, power = 1166W
- HOUND-style stator+rotor example: mass = 1042g ($27\times$), power = 2700W ($2.3\times$)

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
2. Gear Ratio: Reflected Inertia Grows Quadratically
chatChunks.text:
Figure: power density can make the small high-speed motor look better, but the joint torque/inertia requirement changes the conclusion. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 13.

If power were the metric, the small motor would look categorically better. The lecture's point is that once the joint torque requirement is imposed, the small motor needs much higher reduction, and the $N^2$ reflected inertia can dominate.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 12-13.

Seok et al. make the same argument more formally: EM motors can achieve high power density at high speed and relatively low torque, but high-force robot joints need delivered torque. If higher voltage/speed requires higher gear reduction, actuator impedance rises and delivered force control worsens. The paper therefore recommends torque density over power density as the clearer robotics actuator metric.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, pages 1-3.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
chatChunks.text:
Thought Experiment

Two motors, same max torque target:
- Motor 1: mass = 8 ($2\times2\times2$ cube), direct drive → $J{out} = mr^2 = 8 \times 4 = 32$
- Motor 2: mass = 1, needs 8:1 gear ratio → $J{out} = 1 \times 8^2 = 64$

Motor 1 is heavier but has half the output inertia. Bigger motor + lower gear ratio wins on inertia.

Note: this is an illustrative calculation using the $N^2$ reflected-inertia relation, not a copied motor spec.

Source: related lecture example in sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
headingPath: Actuator Performance Metrics: Mass, Torque, Joint Inertia
chatChunks.text:
Application Dependence

The same Pareto front can imply different choices:
- Legged robots: reflected inertia is critical because impacts dominate, so heavier high-torque actuators can be justified to keep joint inertia low.
- Manipulators: static load, precision, and force control can dominate more than repeated impact survival, so higher reduction and lower motor mass may remain acceptable in many designs.
