BLDC, PMSM, and SPMSM Terminology

Use this mapping when reading robot-actuator notes:
- PMSM names the machine family.
- SPMSM and IPMSM name rotor topologies inside that family.
- BLDC is a loose historical/control label, not a topology class.

In robot-hardware notes, if a source says "BLDC motor" but the hardware is a brushless PM actuator with inverter drive, the safe default is to read it as an SPMSM unless the source is explicitly contrasting commutation style.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 36-42; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - transcript.md, lines 486-494.

What Each Term Names

| Term | Names what | Does not specify | Safe reading in this wiki |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| BLDC | A brushless permanent-magnet motor label used in product and drive language | Rotor magnet placement, torque mechanism, exact control waveform | Usually a surface-PM motor driven with electronic commutation; do not infer topology from the label alone |
| PMSM | The permanent-magnet synchronous machine family | Surface vs interior magnets | Umbrella term for SPMSM and IPMSM |
| SPMSM | Surface-mounted rotor magnets | Drive waveform | Common robotics topology; often the hardware behind casual "BLDC" naming |
| IPMSM | Interior rotor magnets | Drive waveform | Interior magnets plus saliency; can produce both magnet torque and reluctance torque |

Strict topology view:

text
PMSM
├── SPMSM (surface-mounted rotor magnets)
└── IPMSM (interior rotor magnets)

BLDC is omitted from the tree because it is not a clean physical branch; it usually names how the motor is talked about or driven.

Textbook Taxonomy Check

The Electric Motor Control textbook makes the ambiguity visible. In its classification figure, "Brushless DC" is shown beside the DC/AC split while the permanent-magnet synchronous branch is under AC motors; the same figure also annotates the permanent-magnet synchronous branch with "BLAC motor." This supports the wiki rule: BLDC and BLAC/PMSM language often mixes waveform/control naming with physical topology.

Figure: motor classification tree showing brushless DC near the DC/AC boundary and permanent-magnet synchronous machines under AC motors. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 2, Fig. 1.1.

Chapter 4 then treats SPMSM, IPMSM, PM-assisted SynRM, and SynRM as rotor/topology categories on a spectrum from magnet torque to reluctance torque. That is the topology language to prefer when discussing d-q torque, saliency, or actuator selection.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 2; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 12-14.

Figure: SynRM, PM-assisted SynRM, IPMSM, inset SPMSM, and SPMSM arranged from reluctance-torque-dominant to magnet-torque-dominant. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 3.

Control and High-Speed Implications

Chapter 5 makes the topology distinction operational. In ideal SPMSM vector control, $id^{r}=0$ is the maximum-torque-per-ampere condition because surface-magnet saliency is small and torque is mainly q-axis magnet torque. In IPMSM vector control, saliency adds a reluctance-torque term, so the MTPA command can include negative d-axis current.

Chapter 8 adds the high-speed distinction: surface magnets and lower inductance make SPMSMs less favorable for very high-speed flux weakening, while IPMSMs are often preferred when wide constant-power speed range matters. This supports the wiki's habit of naming SPMSM and IPMSM explicitly instead of hiding topology behind the casual BLDC label.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 35-44; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23; see chapter-05-vector-control and chapter-08-high-speed-operation.

Practical Reading Rules
- Use SPMSM/IPMSM when the source is about rotor geometry, torque generation, d-q models, or actuator selection.
- Use BLDC only when the source is deliberately talking about six-step or block commutation, trapezoidal back-EMF, or casual product naming.
- Do not infer waveform from topology or topology from waveform label alone. The same motor can be driven with six-step/block commutation or sinusoidal/FOC control.

> WARNING
> "BLDC is DC" and "PMSM is AC" are useful shortcuts, but they are not clean taxonomy. A brushless motor in a robot still needs an inverter/driver in either case.

Unified Lecture Reading

The three lectures are not making different claims; they are layering the same idea from three levels of abstraction.

| Lecture | Role in the unified picture |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | Actuator-level intuition: the robot motor people casually call BLDC is better read as SPMSM because the driver needs rotor position to commutate current and make torque in the desired direction. |
| 2 | Formal taxonomy and model: SPMSM is the surface-magnet topology used in robotics, IPMSM adds saliency and reluctance torque, and SynRM has no magnets. The lecture treats SPMSM as the default robotics model and leaves IPMSM as the more common EV-style alternative. |
| 4 | Design-language correction: the same motor is more accurately SPMSM, while BLDC has become an overloaded industry/paper label that can hide the actual topology. |

Put together, this wiki should read BLDC as a loose control/product label and SPMSM as the physical rotor topology. Use BLDC only when the source is explicitly talking about six-step commutation or casual naming; use SPMSM/IPMSM when the source is about rotor geometry, torque generation, or d-q modeling.

The same actuator-design lecture also uses backdrivability as a second example of overloaded terminology, which is why this page keeps the taxonomy strict even when the source language is loose.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - transcript.md, line 395; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 36-42, 132-141, 179, 189; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - transcript.md, lines 486-494.

DC Motor Control

DC motors are a useful minimal model for understanding motor-operating-region, back-EMF, current control, and PWM drives before moving to three-phase PMSM control. The same structure appears repeatedly in robot actuator notes: voltage creates current, current creates torque, motion creates back-EMF, and the driver must respect voltage and current limits.

Dynamic Model

The textbook models a DC motor drive with four coupled equations:

$$
Va = Ra ia + La\frac{d ia}{dt} + ea
$$

$$
ea = ke\phif\omegam
$$

$$
Te = kT\phif ia
$$

$$
Te = J\frac{d\omegam}{dt} + B\omegam + TL
$$

Here $Va$ is armature voltage, $ia$ is armature current, $Ra$ and $La$ are armature resistance and inductance, $ea$ is back-EMF, $\phif$ is field flux, $\omegam$ is mechanical angular speed, $J$ is inertia, $B$ is viscous friction, and $TL$ is load torque. In the textbook's SI convention, $kT=ke$.

Figure: equivalent circuit for the armature equation and moving-conductor back-EMF. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 5, Figs. 2.5-2.6.

This is why a DC motor feels like the scalar version of a PMSM q-axis model. Back-EMF subtracts from the voltage available for current, and current is the torque-producing quantity.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 4-6.

Speed-Torque Curve

At steady state, set $dia/dt=0$ and $d\omegam/dt=0$. Neglecting viscous friction, the textbook gives:

$$
\omegam = \frac{Va}{k\phif} - \frac{Ra}{(k\phif)^2}TL
$$

The slope is negative: as load torque increases, the armature current and resistive voltage drop increase, leaving less voltage margin for back-EMF and speed. This is the DC-motor version of the high-speed torque limit in motor-operating-region.

Speed Control Regions

Below base speed, a DC motor normally uses armature voltage control. The field flux is held constant, and speed changes roughly linearly with armature voltage. Torque is controlled by armature current, so the same current can produce the same torque across this region.

Above base speed, the terminal voltage has reached its limit. A wound-field DC motor can reduce field flux to keep back-EMF manageable and extend speed. This is field-flux control or field weakening. Torque per ampere falls as flux is reduced, so the high-speed region is approximately a constant-power region rather than a constant-torque region. A permanent-magnet DC motor cannot use this field-winding method.

Figure: DC motor constant-torque and constant-power capability curve. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 11, Fig. 2.11.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 7-11.

Cascaded Control Structure

A motor control system commonly nests loops from fastest to slowest:
- current loop inside the drive
- speed loop around current
- position loop around speed

The current loop must have the widest bandwidth because torque depends directly on current. The speed and position loops can be slower because mechanical states cannot change as quickly as electrical current.

For a DC current loop with back-EMF feedforward and pole-zero cancellation, the textbook gives:

$$
K{pc}=La\omega{cc}
$$

$$
K{ic}=Ra\omega{cc}
$$

where $\omega{cc}$ is the desired current-control bandwidth. The gains depend on the motor's armature inductance and resistance, so the same numeric controller gains do not transfer cleanly between motors.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 19 and 31-34.

Bandwidth and Anti-Windup

The current-loop bandwidth is limited by switching frequency and current sampling. The textbook rule of thumb is up to about one-tenth of switching frequency when current is sampled twice per switching period, and lower when sampled once per period. It also recommends keeping the bandwidth below about one twenty-fifth of the sampling frequency in the once-per-period case.

Integrator windup appears when a PI controller keeps integrating error while the voltage command is already saturated. Back-calculation anti-windup feeds the difference between the pre-limit and post-limit voltage command back into the integrator path. The textbook gives the common anti-windup gain choice:

$$
Ka = \frac{1}{K{pc}}
$$

This is the DC-motor version of the same saturation problem handled in motor-current-control for PMSM drives.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 34-36.

H-Bridge PWM

A four-quadrant chopper, or H-bridge, lets a DC motor operate in forward/reverse motoring and braking. The textbook describes two PWM schemes:
- Bipolar switching: diagonal switch pairs alternate, so the motor voltage jumps between $+V{dc}$ and $-V{dc}$. This gives smooth average-voltage control but larger current ripple.
- Unipolar switching: the two bridge legs are modulated separately, so the motor sees $0$ and $+V{dc}$ or $0$ and $-V{dc}$. This reduces current and torque ripple, but the average-voltage sign transition can be less smooth.

Figure: H-bridge circuit for four-quadrant DC motor operation. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 45, Fig. 2.47.

Figure: unipolar H-bridge switching waveforms and current ripple comparison. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 47, Figs. 2.50-2.51.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 45-47.

Electrical Angle

Electrical angle measures position in the motor's magnetic/electrical cycle, not just the physical shaft angle.

Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Hall-effect commutation placement discussion and Fig. 7.

Mechanical angle is the actual rotor rotation: one shaft revolution is 360 mechanical degrees.

Electrical angle counts how far the rotor magnetic field has advanced relative to the stator phase pattern. A motor with $p$ pole pairs goes through $p$ electrical cycles per one mechanical revolution:

$$
\theta{electrical} = p\theta{mechanical}
$$

Equivalently, with pole count $P$:

$$
\thetae=\frac{P}{2}\thetam
$$

$$
\theta{mechanical} = \frac{\theta{electrical}}{p}
$$

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 10.

Examples:
- 2-pole motor = 1 pole pair: 30 electrical degrees = 30 mechanical degrees.
- 8-pole motor = 4 pole pairs: 30 electrical degrees = 7.5 mechanical degrees.
- 8-pole motor = 4 pole pairs: one full 360-degree shaft revolution = 1440 electrical degrees.

This matters for Hall sensor placement because commutation depends on magnetic/electrical phase, not only physical package angle. Two motors may need the same Hall-sensor spacing in electrical degrees but different mechanical spacing if their pole counts differ.

Related
- bldc-electronic-commutation
- hall-effect-position-sensing

Induction Motor Basics

An induction motor is an AC motor whose stator is energized directly but whose rotor current is produced by electromagnetic induction. It is less central than PMSM/SPMSM in the robot-actuator lecture path, but it is important background for rotating magnetic fields, slip, equivalent circuits, and reference-frame modeling.

Rotating Magnetic Field

Balanced three-phase stator currents in windings separated by $120^\circ$ electrical produce a single rotating air-gap magnetomotive-force vector. For ideal sinusoidal phase currents, the resultant mmf has constant amplitude and rotates at the electrical angular frequency of the currents.

For a motor with $P$ poles and stator frequency $f$, the synchronous speed is:

$$
ns = \frac{120f}{P}\quad\text{r/min}
$$

The electrical and mechanical angles satisfy:

$$
\thetae = \frac{P}{2}\thetam
$$

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 6-10.

Why Slip Is Required

The rotating field induces voltage in the rotor conductors. Since the rotor bars are shorted in a squirrel-cage rotor, that induced voltage drives rotor current. Rotor current in the stator's rotating magnetic field creates torque.

If the rotor reached the same speed as the rotating field, the rotor conductors would no longer see a changing field. No rotor EMF would be induced, rotor current would vanish, and torque would disappear. That is why an induction motor under load runs slightly below synchronous speed.

Slip is usually defined as:

$$
s = \frac{ns-nm}{ns}
$$

where $nm$ is the rotor mechanical speed.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 5-6 and 15-17.

Equivalent-Circuit Intuition

The induction motor equivalent circuit separates:
- stator copper resistance and leakage inductance
- magnetizing branch that produces air-gap flux
- rotor resistance and leakage terms reflected to the stator side
- a slip-dependent rotor resistance term

Only the mutual or magnetizing flux crosses the air gap and contributes to energy conversion. Leakage flux links only its own winding and does not directly produce torque.

Because the machine has an air gap, its magnetizing inductance is much lower than a transformer's. The textbook states that transformer magnetizing current is typically about 1-5% of rated current, while induction-motor magnetizing current is about 30-50% of rated current.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 11-12.

Power Flow and Efficiency

Air-gap power $P{ag}$ is the power transferred from the stator field into the rotor circuit. The textbook splits that power into rotor copper loss and mechanical output:

$$
Pr = sP{ag}
$$

$$
P{mech}=(1-s)P{ag}
$$

So lower slip means less rotor copper loss and more mechanical output power. This is why low-slip operation is usually more efficient.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 20-26.

Speed Control and Operating Regions

The most fundamental way to change induction-motor speed is to change synchronous speed by changing stator frequency. Below base speed, the drive increases voltage roughly in proportion to frequency so the air-gap flux stays near constant. This is constant $V/f$ control, and it gives a constant-torque region.

Above base speed, the terminal voltage has reached its limit, so flux must weaken as frequency rises. The motor then enters a field-weakening range split into a constant-power region and a breakdown-torque-limited region.

Figure: induction motor torque capability divided into constant-torque, constant-power, and breakdown-torque regions. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 45, Fig. 3.47.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 39-47.

Chapter 8 refines the high-speed picture for vector-controlled induction motors. Under rotor-flux orientation, torque is proportional to $id iq$, so field weakening becomes a constrained current-command problem: choose flux-producing d-axis current and torque-producing q-axis current inside both the current circle and the speed-dependent voltage ellipse. The practical result is more specific than "reduce flux above base speed": the optimal current split depends on speed, voltage limit, current limit, leakage inductance, and torque demand.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 5-16.

d-q Model Connection

Chapter 4 uses the induction motor to motivate reference-frame transformation. In abc variables, mutual inductances between stator and rotor windings vary with rotor angle. Transforming to d-q-n variables makes the mutual-inductance relationships constant in the chosen reference frame and magnetically decouples the d and q axes.

For a squirrel-cage rotor, the rotor voltages are zero because the rotor bars are shorted, but the rotor currents and flux linkages still appear in the d-q dynamic model.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 36-40.

Vector-Control Connection

Chapter 5 turns the induction d-q model into field-oriented control. The usual choice is rotor-flux orientation: align the d-axis with rotor flux, make q-axis rotor flux zero, use d-axis stator current as the flux channel, and use q-axis stator current as the torque channel. That makes an induction motor behave more like a separately excited DC motor from the controller's point of view.

Indirect vector control computes synchronous angle from rotor speed plus slip speed. With constant rotor flux, the slip frequency depends on the current ratio:

$$
\omega{sl}=\frac{1}{Tr}\frac{iq^e}{id^e}
$$

where $Tr=Lr/Rr$ is the rotor time constant. This is powerful but sensitive to detuning: rotor resistance changes with temperature, so the slip calculation can misalign the d-q frame from the true rotor flux. Direct vector control estimates rotor flux instead, trading slip-equation sensitivity for flux-estimator quality.

Figure: induction motor vector-control system with speed controller, flux controller, current controller, coordinate transformation, and flux estimator. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, page 32, Fig. 5.22.
Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 6-34; see chapter-05-vector-control.

Magnetic Torque and Reluctance Torque

Electric motors can produce torque through permanent-magnet field alignment, magnetic reluctance alignment, or both.

Figure: magnetic torque aligns a magnet with an electromagnet; reluctance torque aligns an iron rotor with the lower-reluctance flux path. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 4.

Magnetic Torque

Magnetic torque comes from interaction between the stator's current-generated magnetic field and permanent magnets on the rotor.

In the lecture's simple picture:
- the stator winding acts like an electromagnet
- the rotor permanent magnet has a preferred magnetic direction
- current makes the stator field pull the rotor magnet into alignment

This is the dominant ideal torque mechanism in SPMSM motors used in many robot actuators.

Reluctance Torque

Reluctance torque comes from a rotor moving toward the orientation that gives magnetic flux the easier path.

The lecture's intuition is:
- iron is a much easier flux path than air/vacuum
- a rotor with direction-dependent iron/air paths has different reluctance depending on angle
- applying stator current makes the rotor rotate toward the lower-reluctance orientation

So reluctance torque is not "magnet pulls magnet"; it is the motor using a geometry-dependent magnetic-resistance difference.

The textbook formalizes this with magnetic reluctance $\mathcal R$. For a coil with $N$ turns:

$$
L=\frac{\lambda}{i}=\frac{N^2}{\mathcal R}
$$

So geometry that changes the air-gap reluctance also changes inductance. In a rotating machine, torque can come from the rotor moving toward the angle where the magnetic system has lower reluctance and higher inductance for the applied current.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 10 and 23-25.

headingPath: Why Magnets Can Be Ignored in the Reluctance Argument
chatChunks.text:
Why Magnets Can Be Ignored in the Reluctance Argument

For the reluctance-torque intuition, the lecture treats permanent magnets as vacuum-like obstacles rather than as extra iron.

The reason is local and qualitative: in a good permanent magnet, the magnetic domains are already aligned. Additional stator field does not align much more material in the same way it does in soft iron. For deciding whether a flux path has easy iron or difficult non-iron regions, the magnet region can be grouped with high-reluctance regions.

This does not mean a permanent magnet is physically the same as vacuum. It means the magnet is not the part that creates the useful incremental saliency for reluctance torque in this simplified argument.

Motor-Type Spectrum

Figure: SynRM, PM-assisted SynRM, IPMSM, inset SPMSM, and SPMSM arranged from reluctance-torque-dominant to magnet-torque-dominant. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 3.

The slide places motor types on a rough spectrum:
- SynRM: pure reluctance motor; no permanent magnet
- PM-assisted SynRM / IPMSM / inset SPMSM: mixed magnet and reluctance torque
- SPMSM: mostly pure magnet torque in the ideal symmetric model

For an ideal SPMSM, if you ignore the surface magnets for the reluctance calculation, the remaining rotor iron looks nearly rotationally symmetric. There is no strong angle-dependent flux path, so ideal reluctance torque is near zero.

For an IPMSM, the internal magnet cavities and iron bridges create anisotropic flux paths. That saliency lets the motor use reluctance torque in addition to magnet torque.

Chapter 4 presents the same point in PMSM modeling language: for an IPMSM, $Ld$ and $Lq$ differ, so the torque equation contains both a permanent-magnet term and a reluctance/saliency term. For an ideal SPMSM, $Ld \approx Lq$, so the reluctance term largely cancels.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 48-49.

Control Consequences

In vector-control terms, saliency gives IPMSM one extra design lever. Chapter 5 shows that when $Lq>Ld$, negative d-axis current can make reluctance torque add to magnet torque, so maximum-torque-per-ampere control is not simply $id=0$ as it is for the ideal SPMSM. Chapter 8 adds the high-speed consequence: larger d-axis inductance also makes negative d-axis current more effective for PMSM flux weakening.

That is why IPMSM is attractive in applications that need wide constant-power speed range. It does not automatically mean IPMSM is the right robot actuator choice; the robotics question also depends on manufacturability, rotor stress, torque ripple, saliency maps, sensor/control complexity, thermal behavior, and the joint speed range.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 38-44; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23; see chapter-05-vector-control and chapter-08-high-speed-operation.

> QUESTION
> Lecture 2 asks why robotics usually uses SPMSM rather than IPMSM, even though IPMSM can combine magnet torque and reluctance torque. Chapter 8 explains why IPMSM is favored for high-speed operation, but it still does not fully answer the robotics-vs-EV topology trade-off. Revisit this when a source covers robot actuator motor topology selection directly.

Motor and Actuator Structure

Basic terminology needed for actuator-design-principles and legged-robot-actuation.

Motor vs Actuator

Motor = the energy-conversion core. In this lecture, it mainly means the electromagnetic machine made from stator + rotor that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation.

Actuator = the usable motion unit. For robot joints, this usually includes:
- motor
- reducer / gearbox
- motor driver or controller electronics
- sensing such as encoder or rotor-position sensing
- housing and mechanical support

The lecture prefers keeping these terms separate because the third design principle is about the whole actuator, not just the bare motor.

Gearbox / reducer = a mechanical transmission between the motor shaft and output joint. It usually reduces speed and multiplies torque: a $10:1$ gearbox makes the motor rotate about 10 times for one output rotation and ideally gives about $10\times$ output torque before losses. Real gearboxes also add inertia, friction, backlash/compliance, mass, and efficiency loss.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - transcript.md, motor/actuator terminology discussion; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 223-239.

Core Motor Parts

Stator = fixed part. It usually contains the iron core/teeth and copper windings. Current in the windings creates the magnetic field.

Rotor = rotating part. In BLDC/SPMSM-style motors, it often carries permanent magnets and back iron, and it connects mechanically to the shaft.

Figure: basic BLDC motor system elements: controller, driver circuit, windings, Hall-effect sensor ICs, and power input. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 2.

For the naming distinction, see bldc-pmsm-spmsm. For how the driver replaces mechanical brushes/commutators, see bldc-electronic-commutation.

Air gap = the small gap between stator and rotor. Magnetic force is transmitted across this gap. A larger gap increases magnetic reluctance, weakens the air-gap flux, and reduces torque for the same excitation.

Winding = copper wire wound on the stator. Turns, wire diameter, series/parallel choice, and wye/delta connection change voltage/current matching, but the ideal torque capability is constrained by geometry/materials. See actuator-design-principles and bldc-electronic-commutation for how current is switched through the phases.

End turn = the part of the winding outside the active slot region that bends around to the next slot. It adds wire length, resistance, heat, mass, and packaging cost without directly producing useful torque. Long, narrow "kimbap" motors reduce the relative penalty of end turns.

Housing = external mechanical structure. It holds the stator, protects the motor, supports bearings and assembly interfaces, and often provides a heat path to the environment.

Textbook Drive-System View

The Electric Motor Control textbook frames the motor as one part of a larger electromechanical drive system: power source, power electronic converter, digital controller, voltage/current/position sensing, motor, and mechanical load. That matches the lecture convention that a robot actuator is not "just a motor"; the usable joint behavior depends on electronics, sensing, thermal limits, transmission, and load dynamics.

Figure: motor-drive-system block diagram tying source, converter, controller, sensors, motor, and load together. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 35, Fig. 1.32.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 3 and 35.

Design Parameters from Slides

The slide appendix groups the hardware knobs as:
- Stator-rotor dimensions: gap radius, thickness, length
- Reducer: reducer/gearbox type, reduction ratio
- Winding: delta/wye, serial/parallel, coil diameter/turns

This grouping is useful because it maps directly onto the lecture's three principles: geometry sets the electromagnetic capability, reducer choice sets the actuator-level torque/speed/inertia trade-off, and winding is mainly driver matching.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31.

Motor Driver

The motor driver is the power electronics/control unit that supplies timed voltage and current to the motor phases.

For a three-phase motor, the driver must:
- regulate voltage/current
- switch phase currents with the correct timing
- use rotor position or commutation logic to produce torque in the desired direction

Lecture 3B makes this more concrete: a DC bus is switched by a three-phase inverter, SVPWM turns d-q voltage commands into timed switch states, current sensing closes the feedback loop, and current control makes the q-axis current that creates torque. This is also why winding is a driver-matching problem. A low-turn, thick-wire or delta/parallel configuration is suitable for high-current drivers; a high-turn, thin-wire or wye/series configuration is suitable for high-voltage drivers.

BLDC/SPMSM-style motors need rotor-position information for commutation. This can come from Hall effect sensors, encoders, or sensorless estimation.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-205; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 8 and 12-18.

Motor Constant Unit Conversions

Motor constant conversion is easy to get wrong because datasheets may mix speed constant $Kv$, back-EMF constant $Ke$, torque constant $Kt$, terminal voltage, phase voltage, peak current, RMS current, and controller-defined d-q current.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-235; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33.

Energy Consistency Rule

With matching SI definitions, torque constant and back-EMF constant have the same numerical value.

For the one-axis brushed-DC-style convention:

$$
e = Ke\omegam
$$

$$
\tau = Kt i
$$

Power balance gives:

$$
ei = \tau\omegam
$$

Therefore:

$$
Ke = Kt
$$

when $Ke$ is in $\mathrm{V}/(\mathrm{rad}/\mathrm{s})$ and $Kt$ is in $\mathrm{N\,m}/\mathrm{A}$ under the same current and voltage convention.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-235; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 46-55.

Convert $Kv$ to $Kt$

If $Kv$ is given in $\mathrm{rpm}/\mathrm{V}$ and represents the matching no-load speed constant:

$$
Kv
=
\frac{\omegam}{V}
\frac{60}{2\pi}
$$

Since $V \approx Ke\omegam$ at no load:

$$
Ke
=
\frac{60}{2\pi Kv}
$$

So the common conversion is:

$$
Kt
=
\frac{60}{2\pi Kv}
\approx
\frac{9.55}{Kv}
$$

This conversion is only safe when the datasheet's $Kv$ and $Kt$ use matching terminal/current conventions.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 403-421.

Oscilloscope Back-EMF Method

Lecture 2 and Lecture 3A both ask how to estimate torque constant without a torque sensor. The answer is to measure back EMF: spin the motor, measure an open-circuit line-to-line voltage waveform, infer electrical speed from the waveform, then convert the back-EMF constant to the torque constant under a stated convention.

For a sinusoidal SPMSM measured with an oscilloscope, suppose the motor is spun at known mechanical speed $\omegam$ and the open-circuit line-to-line RMS voltage is $V{LL,rms}$. Then:

$$
K{e,LL,rms}
=
\frac{V{LL,rms}}{\omegam}
$$

For ideal sinusoidal back EMF:

$$
V{LL,rms}
=
\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}p\phif\omegam
$$

Thus:

$$
\phif
=
\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}
\frac{K{e,LL,rms}}{p}
$$

The torque constant per q-axis peak current is:

$$
K{t,q\text{-peak}}
=
\frac{3}{2}p\phif
=
\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}K{e,LL,rms}
$$

The torque constant per sinusoidal line RMS current is:

$$
K{t,line\text{-}rms}
=
\sqrt{3}K{e,LL,rms}
$$

This is why the homework asks for both the method and the formula: the measurement is simple, but the numerical constant depends on whether voltage is phase or line-line, peak or RMS, and whether current is q-axis peak or line RMS. Winding and terminal conventions are handled separately in motor-winding-configuration.

Source: measurement method in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33; oscilloscope and line-to-line detail in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 29-74.

Motor Operating Region

A motor operating region (MOR) is the set of speed-current or speed-torque points the motor, driver, and power source can actually produce.

Figure: brushed DC warm-up model and speed-current operating region. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 8.

Brushed DC Warm-Up

The lecture starts from the brushed DC motor model:

$$
V = iR + Kb\omega + L\frac{di}{dt}
$$

For many MOR sketches, the inductive term is small compared with the resistance and back-EMF terms:

$$
V \approx iR + Kb\omega
$$

The two key limits are:

$$
-V{dc} < V < V{dc}
$$

$$
-i{max} < i < i{max}
$$

Together, these create a slanted feasible region in the $\omega$-$i$ plane. At high speed, back EMF consumes more of the voltage budget, so less current and less torque can be produced.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 8; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 125-155.

headingPath: Why It Is Not Just a Max-Torque / Max-RPM Box
chatChunks.text:
Why It Is Not Just a Max-Torque / Max-RPM Box

A simple simulator description often stores only maximum torque and maximum speed. That implies a rectangle: max torque is available at any speed up to max RPM.

Lecture 2 emphasizes that this is wrong near the voltage boundary. At high speed, the motor may have almost no voltage margin left for torque-producing current. The rectangle includes unreachable high-speed/high-torque points.

This is especially important for sim-to-real:
- torque command is not a mechanical state and can change quickly
- velocity is a state and cannot jump instantly
- if a policy reaches high velocity in simulation, it may command torque that the real motor cannot produce there

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 197-209; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 352-368.

Voltage Limit vs Current Limit

The lecture separates the constraints:
- Voltage limit: mostly hard, because the inverter cannot command arbitrary voltage outside the available DC bus without special circuitry.
- Current limit: semi-hard, because it is often an imposed thermal or saturation limit rather than an instantaneous physical impossibility.

Current is still dangerous to treat casually because copper heating scales as:

$$
P{heat} \propto i^2R
$$

Small current-limit violations can create much larger heat increases.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 149-155.

Regeneration and Real Batteries

The MOR is not a symmetric mirrored version of a one-quadrant datasheet plot. Dynamic robots spend time in negative-power regions when a joint is braking and absorbing mechanical energy.

If current is commanded opposite the rotation direction and the inverter tracks it, energy can flow back toward the battery. The lecture describes this as ordinary regenerative behavior of a current-controlled motor drive, not as a separate feature that only exists when a special "regeneration circuit" is added.

Real batteries also have internal resistance, so the DC bus is not an ideal voltage source:
- during positive work, heavy discharge can sag the voltage and shrink the real operating region
- during strong regeneration, bus voltage can rise and temporarily move measured points outside the nominal region

This is why high-discharge-rate, lower-internal-resistance batteries make the simple voltage-limit model fit better.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 145-167.

Textbook Capability Curves

The DC motor chapter gives the clean textbook version of the same idea. Below base speed, armature voltage control gives a constant-torque region because the field flux is fixed and torque is proportional to armature current. Above base speed, field weakening reduces flux so speed can rise after voltage reaches its limit; torque per ampere falls, so the drive enters an approximately constant-power region. A permanent-magnet DC motor cannot use wound-field weakening, which is one reason the voltage boundary matters strongly for PM actuators.

Figure: DC motor capability curve showing voltage-control/constant-torque and field-flux-control/constant-power regions. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 11, Fig. 2.11.

The induction motor chapter gives the AC-machine counterpart: below base speed, constant $V/f$ control keeps air-gap flux approximately constant; above base speed, voltage cannot keep increasing, so flux weakens and the drive transitions through constant-power and breakdown-torque-limited regions.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 7-11; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 45-47.

SPMSM Connection

For SPMSM, the MOR is no longer the simple brushed DC parallelogram because d-q cross-coupling and back EMF change the voltage boundary. Field weakening can extend the high-speed feasible region, but Lecture 2 says most robotics applications do not rely on field weakening as heavily as EV drives.

Lecture 3B adds the driver-side view: the usable voltage limit is not just the nominal DC bus. With SVPWM, the normal non-overmodulated d-q command should satisfy:

$$
\sqrt{Vd^2+Vq^2} \leq \frac{V{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Practical drivers often leave an additional 1-5% margin for modulation and current sensing, which shrinks the real MOR. The Lecture 3 homework asks students to plot SPMSM MOR from the d-q voltage equations and then test whether $id=0$ remains optimal at every speed.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 25 for the d-q saturation limit; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 185-205 for voltage margin and sensing; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - homework.pdf, page 2 for the MOR homework prompt.

Textbook Flux-Weakening Geometry

Chapter 8 turns the SPMSM homework intuition into the general AC-drive constraint picture. For a PMSM, magnet flux cannot be reduced directly. The controller instead commands negative d-axis current so stator-produced flux opposes the permanent-magnet flux:

$$
\lambdad^r=Ld id^r+\phif
$$

Figure: PMSM flux weakening by using negative d-axis current to oppose permanent-magnet flux. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, page 18, Fig. 8.16.

At a given speed, the feasible d-q current commands must satisfy both a current circle and a voltage boundary:

$$
id^2+iq^2\le I{smax}^2
$$

$$
vd^2+vq^2\le V{smax}^2
$$

For an IPMSM in steady state, neglecting stator resistance, the voltage equations become:

$$
vd^r=-\omegar Lq iq^r
$$

$$
vq^r=\omegar(Ld id^r+\phif)
$$

So the voltage limit is an ellipse in the $id$-$iq$ plane centered at:

$$
\left(-\frac{\phif}{Ld},0\right)
$$

As speed increases, that voltage ellipse shrinks. The MOR is the intersection of the current circle and the voltage ellipse, not a rectangular torque-speed box.

Figure: current-limit circle and speed-dependent voltage-limit ellipse for PMSM high-speed operation. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, page 22, Fig. 8.20.

Chapter 8 also makes the maximum-speed condition explicit. If $\phif>LdI{smax}$, the ideal voltage/current model gives a finite maximum electrical speed:

$$
\omega{r,max}=\frac{V{smax}}{\phif-LdI{smax}}
$$

If $\phif<LdI{smax}$, the ideal model does not impose the same finite speed ceiling, but mechanical strength, losses, thermal limits, demagnetization, sensing, and PWM voltage margin still set practical limits. The source-level route for this material is chapter-08-high-speed-operation.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23.

headingPath: Lecture 5 Homework Solution: $id=0$ vs Field Weakening
chatChunks.text:
Lecture 5 Homework Solution: $id=0$ vs Field Weakening

Lecture 5 opens with the Lecture 3 homework solution. The MOR problem uses the previous SPMSM voltage equation and asks for two boundaries: one with $id=0$, and one with the best d-axis current at each speed. The prompt specifies $V{DC}=100.8\,\mathrm{V}$, 30 poles, terminal-to-terminal $R{tt}=0.42\,\Omega$, $L{tt}=1.58\,\mathrm{mH}$, and $Kt=0.5881\,\mathrm{Nm/A}$.

Figure: SPMSM MOR homework prompt and parameters. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 5.

The solution plot shows the expected shape: the $id=0$ boundary is narrower at high electrical speed, while field weakening expands the feasible q-axis current region by spending some current on negative d-axis flux reduction. The useful conclusion is not "always use field weakening"; it is that $id=0$ is not globally optimal once voltage becomes the active limit.

Figure: $id=0$ current boundary compared with the field-weakening-current boundary. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 9.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 5 and 7-9.

Motor Winding Configuration

Motor winding configuration means how the copper coils are connected electrically. In the lecture, this is mainly a driver-matching knob: it changes the voltage/current operating point more than it changes the motor's ideal torque capability.

Figure: high-turn/small-diameter vs low-turn/big-diameter winding as a voltage/current matching problem. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 20.

How to Read the Slide

The orange circles are a simplified cross-section of copper wire in the stator slot.
- Low turns, big diameter: fewer thick wires fit in the same slot.
- High turns, small diameter: more thin wires fit in the same slot.

Both choices use roughly the same available copper space, but they ask different things from the motor driver. Few thick turns have low resistance and can carry high current. Many thin turns have higher resistance and need higher voltage to push current through the longer, thinner wire.

The slide's Korean sentence means: the best winding is chosen by the motor driver's maximum line voltage and line current. In other words, choose the winding so the motor's required voltage/current fits what the driver can actually output.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 20; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 164-174.

Series vs Parallel

Series/parallel describes how coil groups are connected inside a phase.

Series connection puts coil sections end-to-end. It behaves like more effective turns:
- higher torque constant $Kt$
- higher resistance $R$
- lower speed constant $Kv$
- needs more voltage and less current
- fits a high-voltage, lower-current motor driver

Parallel connection creates multiple current paths. It behaves like fewer effective turns with thicker current paths:
- lower torque constant $Kt$
- lower resistance $R$
- higher speed constant $Kv$
- needs less voltage and more current
- fits a low-voltage, high-current motor driver

Wye vs Delta

Wye/delta describes how the three phase windings connect to each other.

Wye, also called Y or star, ties one end of the A, B, and C phase windings together at a shared neutral point. The other three ends connect to the motor driver terminals.

text
 A
 |
 o
 / \
 B C

Wye behaves like the high-voltage, lower-current option: higher effective $Kt$ and $R$, lower $Kv$.

Delta connects the three phase windings end-to-end in a triangle. The motor driver terminals connect at the three triangle corners.

text
 A
 / \
 C-----B

Delta behaves like the low-voltage, high-current option: lower effective $Kt$ and $R$, higher $Kv$.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 166-174.

Equivalent-Circuit Convention

Lecture 2 adds a parameter-definition caution: wye and delta windings can be represented with equivalent circuits, but the reported resistance/inductance value depends on what exactly was measured. For the terminal-equivalence derivation, see wye-delta-parameter-conventions.

Figure: wye/star and delta equivalent-circuit conventions for the d-q voltage equation. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

Before using a datasheet value in spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, check whether it is:
- phase resistance/inductance
- terminal-to-terminal resistance/inductance
- wye-equivalent value
- delta branch value

For equal resistive branches, a wye line-to-line resistance is:

$$
R{ab,\text{wye}} = 2Rs
$$

and a delta line-to-line resistance is:

$$
R{ab,\text{delta}} = \frac{2}{3}R{\Delta}
$$

So a delta network with branch resistance $R{\Delta}$ is equivalent to a wye network with phase resistance:

$$
Rs = \frac{R{\Delta}}{3}
$$

The same habit of checking definitions is needed for inductance, with extra care because mutual inductance and geometry can make the simple scalar picture incomplete.

Lecture 3A resolves the black-box question directly: using only the three external ABC terminals, a wye and delta stator can be externally equivalent. By Thevenin-equivalent reasoning, the outside measurement does not reveal the internal connection uniquely. For motor modeling and driver control, the practical requirement is not to know the hidden physical connection; it is to use a consistent equivalent-circuit convention and matching parameter values.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 51-59.

headingPath: Why Low $Kt$ and Low $R$ Match a High-Current Driver
chatChunks.text:
Why Low $Kt$ and Low $R$ Match a High-Current Driver

Torque is:

$$
T = Kt I
$$

So if $Kt$ is low, the motor needs more current $I$ to make the same torque.

Winding heat is:

$$
P{cu} = I^2R
$$

So low $R$ is what makes that higher current usable without immediately creating excessive copper heat. Low $R$ also means less voltage drop for a given current:

$$
V{drop} = IR
$$

Simple idealized comparison for the same 1 N·m target:

| Winding direction | $Kt$ | $R$ | Current for 1 N·m | Copper heat |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| High-turn / wye / series | 1.0 N·m/A | 1.0 Ω | 1 A | 1 W |
| Low-turn / delta / parallel | 0.5 N·m/A | 0.25 Ω | 2 A | 1 W |

The second row needs more current, but its lower resistance keeps the heat comparable in this simplified motor-constant argument. That is why low-turn/delta/parallel windings are matched to drivers that can supply high current.

Note: numeric values in the table are illustrative, chosen to show the ideal constant-$Km$ trade-off rather than copied motor specs.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, line 166.

headingPath: Why This Does Not Magically Improve the Motor
chatChunks.text:
Why This Does Not Magically Improve the Motor

For fixed geometry, magnets, iron, and copper volume, winding choices mostly trade voltage against current. The lecture's simplified grouping is:

| Configuration direction | Effect | Driver match |
|---|---|---|
| Series / Wye / high turns, small wire | higher $Kt$, higher $R$, lower $Kv$ | high-voltage driver |
| Parallel / Delta / low turns, large wire | lower $Kt$, lower $R$, higher $Kv$ | high-current driver |

The motor constant $Km = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{R}}$ stays roughly the same in the ideal argument. To improve the motor's fundamental torque capability, change geometry/materials: more useful copper/iron volume, larger effective air-gap radius, better magnets, or better magnetic topology.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166.

Wye-Delta Parameter Conventions

Wye-delta parameter conventions matter because a motor's internal winding topology and its external ABC-terminal equivalent are not the same question. For modeling and control, the practical requirement is to use one consistent equivalent-circuit convention.

For the basic winding/topology explanation, see motor-winding-configuration.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5.

Terminal Equivalence

For ideal balanced linear passive branches, external ABC-terminal tests cannot uniquely distinguish wye from delta. A wye network and a delta network can have the same terminal behavior.

Practical answer:
- If only the three external ABC terminals are available and the motor behaves like an ideal balanced linear three-terminal network, no terminal-only test proves whether the internal winding is physically wye or delta.
- If the neutral point is externally accessible, the answer changes because wye exposes a node that delta does not.
- Real motors may show nonideal differences from saturation, parasitic capacitance, circulating harmonic currents, manufacturing asymmetry, or temperature gradients, but those effects do not give a clean general topology proof.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 51-59.

Resistance Conversion

For equal wye phase resistance $Rs$, a line-to-line terminal measurement is:

$$
R{ab,\text{wye}} = 2Rs
$$

For equal delta branch resistance $R{\Delta}$, the line-to-line terminal resistance is:

$$
R{ab,\text{delta}}
=
R{\Delta}
\parallel
\left(R{\Delta} + R{\Delta}\right)
=
\frac{2}{3}R{\Delta}
$$

Equating the external terminal measurement:

$$
2Rs
=
\frac{2}{3}R{\Delta}
$$

So:

$$
Rs = \frac{R{\Delta}}{3}
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

Inductance Conversion Caveat

The same equivalence habit can be used for terminal inductance under a simplified linear scalar model:

$$
2Ls
=
\frac{2}{3}L{\Delta}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Ls = \frac{L{\Delta}}{3}
$$

Use this carefully. Real motor inductance includes mutual inductance, rotor-position dependence, saliency, saturation, measurement frequency, and controller transform convention. A datasheet value must be classified before it is inserted into spmsm-dq-voltage-equations.

Source: equivalent-circuit prompt in sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 243-253; Lecture 3A equivalent-circuit conclusion in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 51-59.

ABC to d-q Transform Derivation

ABC to d-q derivation is the route from three physical phase variables to the rotor-frame equations used in spmsm-dq-voltage-equations. The main study point is to separate physical terminal quantities from convention-dependent d-q coordinates.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2 and 7.

Start From Phase Equations

For a three-phase stator, collect phase quantities as:

$$
\mathbf v{abc} =
\begin{bmatrix}
va \\
vb \\
vc
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\mathbf i{abc} =
\begin{bmatrix}
ia \\
ib \\
ic
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\boldsymbol\lambda{abc} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\lambdaa \\
\lambdab \\
\lambdac
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The phase voltage equation is:

$$
\mathbf v{abc}
=
Rs\mathbf i{abc}
- \frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}}{dt}
$$

Expanded by phase:

$$
va = Rs ia + \frac{d\lambdaa}{dt},
\quad
vb = Rs ib + \frac{d\lambdab}{dt},
\quad
vc = Rs ic + \frac{d\lambdac}{dt}
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 452-459.

Flux Linkage Split

The useful flux-linkage split is:

$$
\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}
=
\mathbf L{abc}(\thetae, \mathbf i{abc})\mathbf i{abc}
- \boldsymbol\lambda{pm,abc}(\thetae)
$$

For the linear, ideal SPMSM derivation, this is simplified to:

$$
\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}
=
\mathbf L{abc}(\thetae)\mathbf i{abc}
- \boldsymbol\lambda{pm,abc}(\thetae)
$$

where $\thetae$ is electrical rotor angle. The relation to mechanical angle is:

$$
\thetae = p\thetam,
\quad
\omegae = \frac{d\thetae}{dt} = p\omegam,
\quad
p = \frac{P}{2}
$$

Here $P$ is pole count and $p$ is pole-pair count.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; convention discussion in sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 473-482.

Why Trigonometric Terms Appear

The trigonometric structure comes from projecting a rotating magnetic field onto three stator phase axes separated by $120^\circ$ electrical.

For an ideal sinusoidal permanent-magnet flux:

$$
\boldsymbol\lambda{pm,abc}
=
\phif
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \thetae \\
\cos \left(\thetae - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\
\cos \left(\thetae + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The same projection idea explains common mutual-inductance terms. If two ideal phase axes are separated by $120^\circ$, their projection is:

$$
\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right) = -\frac{1}{2}
$$

So an ideal balanced inductance matrix often contains off-diagonal terms like:

$$
\mathbf L{abc}
\approx
\begin{bmatrix}
L{\ell} + Lm & -\frac{1}{2}Lm & -\frac{1}{2}Lm \\
-\frac{1}{2}Lm & L{\ell} + Lm & -\frac{1}{2}Lm \\
-\frac{1}{2}Lm & -\frac{1}{2}Lm & L{\ell} + Lm
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This $-\frac{1}{2}$ is a symmetry result for an ideal sinusoidal, balanced, linear model. It is not a universal law for every real motor geometry.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 7-10; transcript caution in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 123-161.

headingPath: Reduce Three Phases to Two Coordinates
chatChunks.text:
Reduce Three Phases to Two Coordinates

For a balanced three-wire motor with no neutral current:

$$
ia + ib + ic = 0
$$

So the three phase currents only contain two independent degrees of freedom. Park/Clarke transformation rewrites those two degrees of freedom into a rotor-aligned frame.

One common amplitude-invariant convention is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
xd \\
xq \\
x0
\end{bmatrix}
=
\frac{2}{3}
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\thetae & \cos\left(\thetae - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) & \cos\left(\thetae + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\
-\sin\thetae & -\sin\left(\thetae - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) & -\sin\left(\thetae + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\
\frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
xa \\
xb \\
xc
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For a balanced three-wire motor, $x0 = 0$.

> WARNING
> Other books and control libraries change signs, scale factors, axis order, or whether the transform is amplitude-invariant or power-invariant. Those choices change numerical definitions of $id$, $iq$, $\phif$, $Ls$, and torque constants. They do not change the physical motor.
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 12-13.

The textbook uses the same amplitude-invariant $2/3$ matrix for arbitrary rotating reference-frame transformation:

headingPath: Reduce Three Phases to Two Coordinates

chatChunks.text:
Rotating-Frame Derivative

With the above convention, use the rotor-frame flux vector:

$$
\boldsymbol\lambda{dq}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\lambdad \\
\lambdaq
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The rotating-frame derivative identity is:

$$
\mathbf v{dq}
=
Rs\mathbf i{dq}
- \frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{dq}}{dt}
- \omegae
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & -1 \\
1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\boldsymbol\lambda{dq}
$$

So:

$$
vd
=
Rs id
- \frac{d\lambdad}{dt}
- \omegae\lambdaq
$$

$$
vq
=
Rs iq
- \frac{d\lambdaq}{dt}
- \omegae\lambdad
$$

This form is the safest intermediate result because it keeps flux linkage explicit.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.

Ideal SPMSM Substitution

For an ideal non-salient SPMSM:

$$
\lambdad = Ls id + \phif
$$

$$
\lambdaq = Ls iq
$$

Assuming $Ls$ and $\phif$ are constant:

$$
vd
=
Rs id
- Ls\frac{d id}{dt}
- \omegae Ls iq
$$

$$
vq
=
Rs iq
- Ls\frac{d iq}{dt}
- \omegae Ls id
- \omegae\phif
$$

The terms to memorize structurally are:

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $Rs id$, $Rs iq$ | copper resistance voltage drop |
| $Ls\frac{d id}{dt}$, $Ls\frac{d iq}{dt}$ | current-changing inductive voltage |
| $-\omegae Ls iq$, $+\omegae Ls id$ | d-q cross-coupling from rotating coordinates |
| $+\omegae\phif$ | permanent-magnet back EMF |

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.

Related
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations
- spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions
- spmsm-torque-equation
- motor-operating-region
- motor-current-control

Analytical Torque Constant for SPMSM

Analytical torque-constant calculation estimates $Kt$ from motor geometry and magnet/winding assumptions before fabrication or full finite-element analysis. Lecture 5 derives the first-order SPMSM result:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

where $kw$ is winding factor, $t$ is magnet thickness, $g$ is effective air gap, $Br$ is magnet remanence, $2N$ is turns per phase, $R$ is air-gap radius, and $l$ is axial length.

Figure: SPMSM cross-section and analytical torque-constant variables. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20.

Purpose

The design reason for this formula is speed and interpretability. Real testing requires building hardware, and FEM can become a black box during early search. A low-dimensional analytical model lets the designer see which geometry and material variables are actually controlling torque production.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 52-72 and 86-90.

Derivation Chain

Lecture 5's chain is:

1. Start from motional EMF, $e=Blv$.
2. Accumulate conductor-level EMF into phase flux linkage through a winding function.
3. Use magnetic co-energy or virtual work to convert rotor-angle-dependent flux linkage into torque.
4. Sum the three balanced phase torques under $id=0$.
5. Correct the ideal winding with $kw$.

The transcript gap covers part of the co-energy and flux-linkage derivation, so this chain is grounded partly in the slide sequence and partly in the resumed transcript.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 207-224 and 271-316; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 22-30.

Three-Phase Torque Result

For the ideal winding and sinusoidal radial field model, the phase torque is written in the slide as:

$$
\tauk
=
-2 ik R l Bm N \sin p(\theta-\thetak)
$$

with balanced q-axis current:

$$
ik = -iq \sin p(\theta-\thetak)
$$

Summing phases gives:

$$
\tau
=
\sumk \tauk
=
3Bm RlN iq
$$

Using $Bm \approx \frac{t}{g}Br$ and adding $kw$ gives the lecture result:

$$
\tau = Kt iq,
\qquad
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g}BrNRl
$$

Figure: phase torque, balanced phase currents, and total torque result. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 359-386; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

Assumptions

This is a first-order analytical model, not a replacement for FEM. The key assumptions are:
- SPMSM-like surface magnet geometry.
- Sinusoidal or fundamental-only radial air-gap flux waveform.
- Effective magnet field amplitude approximated as $Bm \approx \frac{t}{g}Br$.
- Ideal or winding-factor-corrected winding linkage.
- Balanced three-phase current with $id=0$ for the displayed torque result.
- Lossless energy conversion in the derivation step.
- Cogging torque, saliency/reluctance torque, saturation, leakage, fringing, and detailed magnetic-circuit effects are ignored or deferred.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 22-25 and 30-32; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 310-316 and 445-454.

Design Reading

The formula says $Kt$ grows with useful air-gap radius, axial length, magnet field, and effective turns. Pole-pair count is not an independent free multiplier once the same effective conductor inventory is fixed. More poles can still improve real motors through shorter end turns, better feasible winding factors, thinner magnetic paths, and reduced mass, but those effects belong to winding and magnetic-circuit design rather than to a bare pole-count coefficient.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 388-424.

Motional EMF and Flux Linkage

Motional EMF is the electromotive force induced when a conductor moves through a magnetic field. Lecture 5 uses it as the entry point for deriving analytical SPMSM torque constant from geometry.

EMF Is Not Terminal Voltage

For a straight conductor of active length $l$ moving perpendicular to flux density $B$ at speed $v$, the induced EMF magnitude is:

$$
e = B l v
$$

The lecture emphasizes that $e$ is not simply the same thing as terminal voltage. A moving rod in a uniform magnetic field can develop EMF between its ends, but without a closed return path there is no steady circuit current. Once rails, a load, and a voltmeter are added, the measured voltage depends on the actual circuit and the reference frame used for the measurement.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 140-187.

The DC motor chapter uses the same conductor-level relation to introduce back-EMF, then writes the armature back-EMF as:

$$
ea = ke\phif\omegam
$$

That is the machine-level version of $e=Blv$: the conductor speed scales with rotor speed, and the summed winding EMF scales with field flux and angular velocity.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 4-5.

Why Lecture 5 Starts Here

Each active conductor in a motor can be treated as a small moving-conductor EMF source. Summing those small contributions over the winding gives a phase EMF. Since EMF is the time derivative of flux linkage, the same calculation can be integrated into phase flux linkage:

$$
e = \frac{d\lambda}{dt}
$$

The transcript resumes after the recording gap by stating exactly this bridge: the lecture had just computed EMF, while flux linkage is recovered by integrating that EMF in time.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 271-279.

The textbook introduces flux linkage as $\lambda=N\phi$ for an $N$-turn coil and inductance as $L=\lambda/i$. It also shows why this relation is only locally linear: once the iron core saturates, extra current produces much less extra flux, and the effective inductance drops.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 9-10.

Stationary-Frame Convention

Lecture 5 keeps the discussion in a stationary reference frame. The moving conductor sees charge redistribution until the body-frame electric field reaches the steady-state condition, while the stationary-frame observer accounts for the velocity-cross-magnetic-field term. For the wiki, keep the sign convention attached to the chosen current, path, and phase definitions; the durable magnitude relationship is the conductor-level link between motion, magnetic field, EMF, and flux linkage.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 95-133.

Connection to Torque

Flux linkage is the bridge from motion-induced voltage to torque. Once the winding flux linkage is expressed as a function of rotor angle, magnetic co-energy or virtual work can produce torque by differentiating with respect to rotor angle. Lecture 5 uses that route to separate useful current-generated torque from cogging and reluctance effects before returning to the compact $Kt$ formula.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 207-224; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 22-25.

SPMSM d-q Voltage Equations

The d-q voltage equations are the compact rotor-frame model used to describe SPMSM electrical dynamics, draw the motor operating region, and build current control. For the homework derivation path from phase equations, see abc-to-dq-transform-derivation.

Figure: Lecture 2 target form of the SPMSM d-q voltage equations. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.

Why d-q Coordinates Exist

A three-phase motor has A, B, and C terminals, but the phase voltages/currents are constrained by circuit laws. After removing the redundant degree of freedom, the electrical state can be represented with two coordinates.

The d-q frame rewrites the three-phase variables into two axes attached to the rotor frame:
- d-axis: direct axis, aligned with the rotor magnetic flux convention
- q-axis: quadrature axis, perpendicular to the d-axis

The exact sign and scale can vary by Park/Clarke transform convention, so do not mix equations from different books or software without checking definitions.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 12-13.

Ideal SPMSM Rotor-Frame Model

For the lecture's ideal SPMSM model:

$$
v{ds}^{r} = Rs i{ds}^{r} + Ls\frac{d i{ds}^{r}}{dt} - \omegar Ls i{qs}^{r}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{r} = Rs i{qs}^{r} + Ls\frac{d i{qs}^{r}}{dt} + \omegar Ls i{ds}^{r} + \omegar\phif
$$

where:
- $Rs$ = phase resistance under the chosen convention
- $Ls$ = phase inductance under the chosen convention
- $\omegar$ or $\omegae$ = electrical angular velocity of the rotor frame
- $\phif$ = rotor permanent-magnet flux linkage
- superscript $r$ = rotor-frame quantity

The torque relation for the ideal surface-magnet case is:

$$
\tau{rotor} = \frac{3}{2}\frac{P}{2}\phif i{qs}^{r}
$$

and the electrical/mechanical speed relation is:

$$
\omegae = \frac{P}{2}\omegam
$$

where $P$ is the number of poles.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; torque equation cross-check: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

PMSM Flux-Linkage Cross-Check

Chapter 4 of Electric Motor Control derives the PMSM d-q model from the same rotating-frame voltage identity but emphasizes that the flux-linkage model depends on rotor structure. In the rotor reference frame, an IPMSM has:

$$
\lambdad^r = Ld id^r + \phif
$$

$$
\lambdaq^r = Lq iq^r
$$

The SPMSM model is the non-salient special case where $Ld \approx Lq$. This is the source of the lecture simplification $\lambdad=Ls id+\phif$ and $\lambdaq=Ls iq$.

Figure: IPMSM d-axis and q-axis equivalent circuits in the rotor reference frame. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 48, Fig. 4.27.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 46-48.

headingPath: How Later Textbook Chapters Use the Same Equations
chatChunks.text:
How Later Textbook Chapters Use the Same Equations

Chapter 6 uses the speed-dependent terms as feedforward targets for motor-current-control:

$$
v{d,ff}^r=-\omegar Lq iq^r
$$

$$
v{q,ff}^r=\omegar Ld id^r+\omegar\phif
$$

Chapter 8 uses the steady-state version of the same equations to draw the PMSM voltage-limit boundary in the $id$-$iq$ plane. Chapter 9 uses the stator voltage and flux-linkage equations in the opposite direction: sensorless observers estimate rotor position or flux from measured current and estimated applied voltage. This is why parameter convention mistakes in this page propagate into current-control tuning, flux weakening, and sensorless control.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 15-17; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 21-23; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 12-13.

headingPath: What Makes It Different from Brushed DC
chatChunks.text:
What Makes It Different from Brushed DC

The key terms are the cross-coupling and back-EMF terms:
- $-\omegar Ls i{qs}^{r}$ in the d-axis voltage equation
- $+\omegar Ls i{ds}^{r}$ in the q-axis voltage equation
- $+\omegar\phif$ in the q-axis voltage equation

These are why an SPMSM operating boundary is not just the brushed DC parallelogram. If inductance and cross-coupling are ignored, the model collapses toward a brushed-DC-like form; if they are kept, the voltage limit becomes curved.

Figure: SPMSM d-q voltage equations and the motor operating region with and without field weakening. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 10.

Field Weakening

In the ideal SPMSM torque equation, torque is mainly controlled by q-axis current. D-axis current can be used to reduce effective magnet flux at high speed, limiting back EMF and extending the feasible high-speed region.

Lecture 2 notes that field weakening is common in EV-style high-speed operation, but most robotics applications do not need to rely on it heavily because their joint-speed demands are usually lower.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 185-189; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 10.

Parameter and Convention Cautions

The equations use $Rs$ and $Ls$ as phase quantities under a particular equivalent-circuit convention. A datasheet or measurement may report:
- phase resistance
- terminal-to-terminal resistance
- wye-equivalent values
- delta branch values

See motor-winding-configuration before mixing these into the d-q equations.

The lecture also asks whether d-q variables are real physical quantities like a measured phase resistance or convention-dependent coordinates. The practical answer is: their physical meaning is tied to the rotor field, but the numerical definitions include transform conventions chosen by the modeler.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 477-482; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 95-99.

Linear-Model Caveat

These equations assume a linear magnetic model. Real iron saturates, so inductance and torque constants can change with current. Lecture 2 explicitly attributes high-current torque nonlinearity to the nonlinear $B$-$H$ curve and saturation, not to hysteresis by itself.

For torque mechanisms, see magnetic-reluctance-torque.

Lecture 3A revisits the same warning from the motor-driver side. The flux-linkage relation $\lambda(i)$ is not guaranteed to be linear; the usual constant-inductance form is the approximation. That matters when deriving torque constants, copper-loss formulas, and current-loop feedforward terms.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 192-215; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.

SPMSM Linear Magnetic Assumptions

The d-q model is only as trustworthy as its magnetic assumptions. The safest habit is to keep the flux-linkage form first, then state exactly when it is simplified into constant-inductance equations.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 188-217; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.

Exact After Definitions

These equations are exact after the circuit variables and transform convention are chosen:

$$
\mathbf v{abc}
=
Rs\mathbf i{abc}
- \frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}}{dt}
$$

Constant $Rs$ is still an approximation because copper resistance changes with temperature, but the circuit form itself is the right starting point.

The rotating-frame flux equation is also exact as a coordinate identity:

$$
vd
=
Rs id
- \frac{d\lambdad}{dt}
- \omegae\lambdaq
$$

$$
vq
=
Rs iq
- \frac{d\lambdaq}{dt}
- \omegae\lambdad
$$

The speed relation is exact for a synchronous machine with known pole-pair count:

$$
\omegae = p\omegam
$$

The torque expression from power/co-energy is exact in the flux-linkage form:

$$
\taum
=
\frac{3}{2}p
\left(\lambdad iq - \lambdaq id\right)
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 452-482; torque/flux-linkage discussion in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 352-353.

Linear Assumptions

The compact SPMSM model assumes:

$$
\lambdad = Ls id + \phif
$$

$$
\lambdaq = Ls iq
$$

Then:

$$
\taum
=
\frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

and:

$$
vd
=
Rs id
- Ls\frac{d id}{dt}
- \omegae Ls iq
$$

$$
vq
=
Rs iq
- Ls\frac{d iq}{dt}
- \omegae Ls id
- \omegae\phif
$$

These are the useful study equations, but they rely on constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

Saturation Boundary

In the real nonlinear case, write flux linkage as:

$$
\lambdad = \lambdad(id, iq, \thetae)
$$

$$
\lambdaq = \lambdaq(id, iq, \thetae)
$$

Then the differential terms become partial-derivative terms:

$$
\frac{d\lambdad}{dt}
=
\frac{\partial\lambdad}{\partial id}\frac{d id}{dt}
- \frac{\partial\lambdad}{\partial iq}\frac{d iq}{dt}
- \frac{\partial\lambdad}{\partial \thetae}\omegae
$$

and similarly for $\lambdaq$.

This is the clean answer to the lecture prompt about saturation: the flux-linkage form remains valid, but replacing flux linkage with constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$ is the linear approximation.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 188-217; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.

Study Implication

When current is small, a constant torque constant can be a useful local slope. At high current, the stator core can saturate, flux linkage is no longer linear in current, and the apparent torque constant changes. This also limits how literally current-control feedforward should trust constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$. The lecture explicitly distinguishes this from hysteresis: the key modeling problem here is the nonlinear $B$-$H$ curve and saturation.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 213-217.
