Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar

Sources: Lecture 3 Notion export, transcript, homework-solution slide PDF, motor-driver seminar slide PDF, and homework PDF.

Lecture 3 should be treated as two related but separate blocks, not as one continuous lecture flow.

| Block | Treat as | Main route |
|---|---|---|
| Lecture 3A | Lecture 2 homework/modeling review: torque constant, wye/delta equivalence, waveform assumptions, d-q conventions, and linear magnetic approximations | lecture-02-homework-workthrough |
| Lecture 3B | Juminchan's separate motor-driver/current-control seminar: inverter, SVPWM, current sensing, d-q PI loop, and driver nonidealities | lecture-03-study-path |

The transcript explicitly closes the homework review before introducing Juminchan at about 37:05, and the following section is framed as a motor-driver seminar on PMSM/IPMSM current control.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
Core Question

For Lecture 3B:

How does a torque command become real phase voltage/current in a PMSM driver, and what hardware effects break the ideal model?

The short chain is:

$$
v \rightarrow i \rightarrow \lambda \rightarrow \tau
$$

For an ideal SPMSM, torque is mainly controlled through q-axis current:

$$
\tau \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

where $p$ is pole-pair count and $\phif$ is permanent-magnet flux linkage. The driver therefore regulates current by commanding motor voltage through an inverter.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-181; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 352-355; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
1. Lecture 3A: Lecture 2 Homework Solutions

The first slide deck answers several modeling questions that were left open in Lecture 2.

Torque constant without a torque sensor. The lecture says to measure back EMF instead of torque. Torque constant and back-EMF constant are tied by the motor equations, so an oscilloscope measurement of line-to-line back EMF while spinning the motor can identify $\phif$, then $Kt$. The waveform frequency gives electrical speed; the waveform amplitude, after the chosen phase/line and transform convention is handled, gives the voltage term proportional to $\omegae\phif$.

Wye vs delta from the outside. A black-box stator cannot be distinguished as wye or delta using only the three external ABC terminals. Thevenin-equivalent networks can present the same external behavior. For modeling, use a consistent equivalent-circuit convention, commonly a serial wye-equivalent model, and keep parameter definitions explicit.

Sinusoidal flux is not guaranteed by nature. Three phases are separated by $120^\circ$ electrical, but the waveform does not have to be sinusoidal. Distributed windings and flux-leakage effects can make back EMF close to sinusoidal; trapezoidal BLDC-style machines deliberately differ.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
d-q variables are convention-dependent. The lecture's practical rule is that it is acceptable if the forward and inverse transforms are consistent: $x = \mathrm{Park}(\mathrm{Clarke}(x))$ under the selected convention. Many modeling and firmware mistakes come from mixing conventions.

The d-q voltage equations contain approximations. Ideal SPMSM can use $Ld = Lq$, but saliency, IPMSM geometry, rotor-angle-dependent inductance, and current-dependent saturation can break that equality. The linear flux-linkage relation is the approximation to watch.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 29-74, 123-161, and 290-316; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
2. Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar

The motor-driver material after the transition is Juminchan's separate practical seminar. It should be connected to the actuator-design and SPMSM-modeling sequence as a driver/current-control branch, while keeping Lecture 3A as the homework-review closure for Lecture 2.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 19-21 and 135-139; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 324-340.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
3. Motor Driver as Current Controller

The motor driver is not just a power amplifier. It is a current-control system that uses voltage commands to force measured phase currents to track desired values.

See motor-driver-inverter for the inverter view and motor-current-control for the d-q PI-loop view.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-181; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
4. Inverter and PWM

Real drivers usually do not synthesize smooth analog phase voltages. They switch MOSFETs rapidly and use PWM. The motor winding is a large inductor, so the applied voltage pulses produce smoother current than the voltage waveform itself.

A three-phase inverter is built from three half-bridges, or six switches. Its legal switch states form eight space vectors:
- two zero-voltage vectors, $(0,0,0)$ and $(1,1,1)$
- six active voltage vectors

High-side MOSFETs require gate-drive circuitry because their source can sit near $V{dc}$; the gate must still be driven above the source. The lecture contrasts older bootstrap circuits with charge-pump gate drivers.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
5. SVPWM and Voltage Margin

SVPWM combines adjacent active vectors and zero vectors over each switching period to create the desired average voltage vector. Splitting the zero-vector time symmetrically reduces current ripple, and lower current ripple means lower torque ripple.

For normal non-overmodulated SVPWM, the usable voltage region is the inscribed circle inside the space-vector hexagon. The lecture recommends not using the full theoretical boundary; leave roughly 1-5% phase-voltage margin. That margin matters because it:
- avoids overmodulation vibration
- preserves current-sensing windows for low-side shunts
- shrinks the practical motor operating region

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
6. Current Sensing

The lecture focuses on triple-shunt low-side current sensing because it is low-cost and practical.

The signal path is:

$$
i \rightarrow V{shunt}=iR{shunt} \rightarrow \text{op-amp} \rightarrow \text{ADC}
$$

Important implementation details:
- shunt resistors are usually around $1-10\ \mathrm{m\Omega}$
- low-side shunts have a convenient ground reference
- high-side or in-line shunts need better common-mode rejection
- in-line sensing is electrically cleaner but more expensive
- Hall sensors can be attractive in high-current drivers because shunt heating changes resistance
- low-side sensing only works when the relevant low-side FET is on
- ADC samples must be synchronized and placed inside the valid sensing window

Noise, op-amp gain, low-pass filters, ADC scaling, Clarke/Park convention, and ground bounce can all corrupt the current estimate. See motor-current-sensing.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 185-223; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 15-18.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
7. Current Control PI Loop

Ignoring speed-dependent terms at first, each d-q current channel looks like a first-order electrical plant:

$$
G(s)=\frac{i}{V}=\frac{1}{Ls+R}
$$

The lecture uses a PI controller with pole-zero cancellation:

$$
KP = L\omega{des}
$$

$$
KI = R\omega{des}
$$

The resulting closed-loop form is a first-order low-pass response:

$$
Gs(s)=\frac{\omega{des}}{s+\omega{des}}
$$

The speed-dependent cross-coupling and back-EMF terms can be added as feedforward. In practice, the lecture recommends tuning from small gains and starting without feedforward, because incorrect feedforward can make debugging harder.

The command voltage must be saturated to the available inverter voltage, and the integrator needs anti-windup. The lecture mentions backward Euler integration and back-calculation anti-windup.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 21 and 24-25.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
8. Real Inverter Nonidealities

The ideal command voltage is not the real motor terminal voltage:

$$
V{ref} \neq V{real}
$$

Lecture 3B flags the main causes:
- sensor noise and low-pass-filter delay
- discrete-time control delay
- MOSFET/gate-driver dead time
- body-diode voltage drop during dead time
- MOSFET turn-on/off delay
- gate charge and switching ringing from parasitic capacitance/inductance
- PCB layout and ground-bounce problems

Current feedback can often tolerate these errors, but voltage-model-based sensorless control is more sensitive because it depends on the actual applied voltage. See motor-driver-nonidealities.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 26-31.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chatChunks.text:
Homework Assigned

The Lecture 3 homework asks for:
- derivation of copper loss and torque-constant formulas, including where the linear magnetic approximation enters
- SPMSM MOR plotting from d-q voltage equations, including whether $id=0$ is optimal at all speeds
- resistive braking current and torque when all three phase terminals are shorted

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - homework.pdf.

Lecture 4: Actuator Design Process

Sources: Lecture 4 Notion export, transcript, and slide PDF.

Lecture 4 shifts from motor modeling to a practical actuator design workflow. It covers the first half of the design process: requirements, torque-mass-inertia selection, and reducer type selection. Reducer detailed design and structural-component design are deferred.

Core Question

Given a robot concept, how do we choose actuator requirements before designing or selecting hardware?

The lecture's process is:

1. define requirements
2. define torque-mass-inertia (T-M-I)
3. select reducer type
4. design reducer
5. design structural components

Figure: Lecture 4 actuator design process. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 2.

1. Define Requirements Before Optimization

Requirements must be chosen before actuator optimization. Lecture 4 distinguishes two different activities:
- choosing where to sit on the current Pareto front
- pushing the Pareto front outward by better topology, materials, manufacturing, or reducer quality

The lecture uses the mountain-bike analogy: decide whether the product is an MTB or road bike before optimizing materials and bearings. For robot actuators, this means deciding what kind of robot and task envelope you want before trying to improve hardware quality.

See actuator-requirement-selection.

2. Primary vs Secondary Requirements

Lecture 4 states that actuator primary requirements are:
- mass
- rated torque
- joint/output inertia
- peak or saturation torque

These are fundamentally coupled. Improving one generally worsens at least one other at the same design/manufacturing level.

Secondary requirements include:
- backlash
- volume
- stiffness
- velocity / power

The lecture calls these secondary because their limits are practical rather than fundamental in the same way: manufacturing precision, driver voltage/current capability, assembly quality, and cost can improve them without the same clean Maxwell/F=ma coupling.

3. Corrected Normalized Actuator Metric

Lecture 4 explicitly says the earlier normalized actuator metric was corrected again. The slide gives the current relationship as:

$$
C \approx \frac{Km^2\tau{sat}^2}{M I^2}
$$

where $Km = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{Rs}}$, $\tau{sat}$ is torque limited by core saturation, $M$ is copper mass, and $I$ is joint inertia.

For off-the-shelf frameless motors, $Km$ and $\tau{sat}$ are usually coupled by the motor family/design. That reduces practical selection to torque, mass, and inertia, with one relationship constraining them. See motor-constant and actuator-performance-metrics.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 65-117; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6.

4. T-M-I Selection Workflow

For a QDD-based anthropomorphic manipulator, the lecture gives a distal-to-proximal workflow:

1. define kinematic parameters
2. set rated and peak payload
3. define wrist actuator T-M-I
4. define forearm mass
5. define elbow actuator T-M-I
6. define upper-arm mass
7. define shoulder actuator T-M-I

If the system becomes too heavy or fails the goal, iterate backward. If iteration cannot meet the target, lower the goal or push the Pareto front with better motor, material, or reducer design.

Torque is set from payload and child-link mass using:

$$
\tau = J^T F
$$

For fixed-base industrial manipulators, the design often considers worst configuration. For mobile anthropomorphic manipulators, the lecture argues it is acceptable to consider favorable configurations because the base/body can move to make the task mechanically reasonable.

See torque-mass-inertia-selection.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 150-155 and 223-229; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 7 and 10-11.

5. Static vs Dynamic Design

For static QDD manipulator design with off-the-shelf frameless motors:
- lighter motors are better
- maximize reduction ratio within the QDD/backdrivability limit
- check joint velocity under the given battery voltage
- reducer mass depends mainly on peak joint/output torque, not motor torque

For dynamic design, the metric is not maximum speed but maximum angular acceleration. In a 1-DOF system, acceleration is maximized when reflected rotor inertia equals link inertia:

$$
N^2Jr = J{link}
$$

Below that critical ratio, both static torque and dynamic acceleration can be worse. Above it, static performance improves while dynamic performance trades off.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 12-13.

6. Reducer Type Selection

Lecture 4 covers common robot reducer families:
- single-stage planetary drive
- two-stage planetary drive
- 3K compound planetary drive
- cycloidal drive
- harmonic drive

Reducer primary requirements are torque density and efficiency; these have a fundamental trade-off. Backlash is important in robotics, but the lecture treats it as secondary because precision/manufacturing and preload decisions dominate it.

See robot-actuator-reducer-selection.

7. Backdrivability Is Ambiguous

The lecture warns that "backdrivability" is used loosely. It can mean:
- backward efficiency
- reverse-drive starting torque / Coulomb friction
- reflected joint inertia

For this wiki, use backdrivability mainly for friction/reverse-drive behavior, and discuss reflected inertia separately.

Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chatChunks.text:
Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant

This source bundle contains a Lecture 3 homework-solution prelude and Lecture 5, "Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant (Part 1)." The source files are organized under sources/lectures/lecture-05/; their contents remain unchanged.

Sources: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md and sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf.

> WARNING
> The transcript has a large gap from 22:48 to 54:56, caused by recording trouble. The pages covering magnetic co-energy, flux linkage setup, and part of the winding-function derivation are therefore summarized from the slide deck and the later resumed transcript rather than from continuous spoken explanation.
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 220-240.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chatChunks.text:
Homework Solution Prelude

The first twelve slides are Lecture 3 homework solutions. The durable items for the wiki are:
- The motor operating region (MOR) homework uses the SPMSM d-q voltage equation, ignores heating and magnetic saturation, and compares the $id=0$ boundary against a field-weakening-current (FWC) boundary.
- The example parameters are $V{DC}=100.8\,\mathrm{V}$, 30 poles, terminal-to-terminal $R{tt}=0.42\,\Omega$, $L{tt}=1.58\,\mathrm{mH}$, and $Kt=0.5881\,\mathrm{Nm/A}$.
- The plotted solution shows that $id=0$ is not the best high-speed strategy; choosing negative d-axis current extends the feasible q-axis current region at high electrical speed.

Figure: MOR homework prompt and parameters. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 5.

Figure: $id=0$ boundary compared with the field-weakening-current boundary. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 9.

Problem 3 introduces resistive braking as a safety behavior: shorting or otherwise resistively loading the motor can remove kinetic energy quickly and create braking current. The lecture frames this as a HOUND2 safety behavior for stopping a leg before self-collision or damage.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chatChunks.text:
Figure: resistive-braking q-axis current plot and HOUND2 demonstration image. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 12.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 46-54; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 5-12.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chatChunks.text:
Lecture Goal

Lecture 5 asks why an analytical torque-constant model matters. Physical testing can measure $Kt$ after fabrication, and finite-element analysis can estimate it numerically, but a design workflow needs a low-dimensional hand model before testing every candidate geometry. The lecture's target is to estimate SPMSM torque constant from cross-section geometry, axial length, magnet grade, and winding pattern before manufacturing or full FEM.

The slide-level result is:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

where $kw$ is winding factor, $t$ is magnet thickness, $g$ is effective air gap, $Br$ is magnet remanence, $2N$ is turns per phase, $R$ is air-gap radius, and $l$ is axial length.

Figure: analytical torque-constant target formula and variable definitions. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 52-72 and 86-90; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 13 and 20.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
Motional EMF and Voltage Are Different
chatChunks.text:
Motional EMF and Voltage Are Different

The lecture revisits $e=Blv$ from a moving-conductor viewpoint. The important distinction is that $e$ is electromotive force, not ordinary terminal voltage. In the lecture's examples, a rod moving through a uniform magnetic field can have an induced EMF between its ends, but current does not necessarily flow unless the physical circuit provides a return path. The rail example is assigned as a way to separate EMF, voltage, resistance, and the chosen stationary reference frame.

This matters because the later motional-EMF derivation treats each conductor segment in the motor as a small generator, then accumulates those contributions into phase flux linkage and torque.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 95-191.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chatChunks.text:
From EMF to Flux Linkage

After the transcript gap, the lecture resumes by saying the derivation had just computed EMF, while the desired quantity was flux linkage. The bridge is that EMF is the time derivative of flux linkage, so integrating the motional-EMF result gives the flux-linkage form.

For the simplified radial-field model, the relevant overlap is:

$$
\lambda(\theta)
=
R l \int0^{2\pi}
Br(\phi-\theta)N(\phi)\,d\phi
$$

Only the radial component of the air-gap flux density is retained in this model. The winding function $N(\phi)$ acts like the angular window that counts how much conductor linkage sees each part of the rotor magnet field.

Figure: conductor density and winding function for the simplified SPMSM cross-section. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 26.

Figure: radial magnet field waveform overlapped with the winding function. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 29.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 271-316; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 26-30.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chatChunks.text:
Winding Factor and Pole-Count Insight

The derivation first uses an ideal concentrated full-pitch winding with winding factor $kw=1$, then introduces $kw$ as the efficiency of a real winding pattern relative to that ideal. The lecture's practical interpretation is that a winding factor below 1 means some conductor EMF contributions partially cancel instead of adding perfectly.

The most important design warning is about pole count: increasing pole-pair count does not directly multiply torque constant if total conductor count and effective linkage are fixed. More poles can still help real motors by enabling shorter end turns, better practical concentrated windings, thinner magnetic paths, and lower mass, but the final ideal formula does not contain pole-pair count as an independent torque-constant multiplier.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 318-424; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 31-34.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chatChunks.text:
Torque Result

For three balanced phases with $id=0$, the slide result sums the phase torques into:

$$
\tau = 3 Bm R l N iq
$$

and then substitutes the simplified magnet field amplitude $Bm \approx \frac{t}{g}Br$ and real-winding correction $kw$ to obtain:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

Figure: three-phase torque summation and the final analytical $Kt$ expression. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

The lecture closes by framing this as one half of a larger actuator-limit argument. Mass and inertia can be computed directly from geometry, while torque constant and saturation torque need electromagnetic modeling. Lecture 5 handles the EMF/flux-linkage side; magnetic-circuit modeling and saturation are deferred. In the broader wiki, this geometry-level $Kt$ result feeds the later motor-constant discussion rather than replacing it.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 382-386 and 468-483; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

RAIPAL Mechanism Development Special Lecture

Choi Hyung-ho's RAIPAL special lecture is a mechanism-development retrospective: how a control-facing arm platform moved from blank concept, through CAD iteration and production, into URDF/middleware integration and demonstrations. It complements the actuator-design lectures by showing the non-ideal hardware labor between requirements and a working robot.

The speaker frames the talk as a "bolt-turner" view: not primarily how the actuators were optimized, but how individually developed actuators, drivers, mechanism parts, CAD revisions, drawings, prototypes, and tests were packaged into a usable arm platform. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 1-2; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 23-32.

Design Intentions

The lecture separates design intentions from contributions. A design intention is a pre-build decision such as where to place bolts, how to keep the system modular, or how to make manufacturing and service possible. A contribution is a retrospective claim about what the finished robot is good at, such as top speed, payload, or backdrivability. The two are not mutually exclusive, but the hardware engineer has to circulate between them while samples are expensive and sparse. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 4; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 37-59.

RAIPAL's stated intentions were:
- Control transparency / engineering simplicity: make the platform easy to digitize for RL by keeping dynamics linear, minimizing sim-to-real gap, using QDD-like transparent actuation, high-bandwidth motor drivers, and transparent decoupled mechanisms.
- Streamlined symmetry: reduce subcomponent diversity by reusing actuator families and modular electronics, while actuators and electronics were being developed in parallel.
- Project-level payload target: 5 kg nominal payload and 25 kg peak payload were the major project-level constraints called out in the slides.
- Mechanism-level contribution pressure: because actuator and electronics contributions were already split among other developers, the mechanism needed at least one meaningful component-level or dynamic-capability hook beyond "make it light and not broken."

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 5-6; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 63-103.

Concept Comparison

Before detailed CAD, the lecture compares three arm concepts: FAST, SIMPLE, and LINKED. The comparison is not a final proof; it is a way to shrink the topology design space using mass, center-of-mass distance, worst-case holding torque, projected inertia, and payload estimates.

Figure: FAST, SIMPLE, and LINKED concept comparison using approximate mass, projected inertia, holding torque, and payload estimates. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8.

The table reports the LINKED concept at 7.9 kg with projected inertia <0.33,0.43,0.11> kgm^2 and 7.2 kg worst-case payload. It sits between FAST's higher mass / slightly higher payload and SIMPLE's lower mass / lower payload. The transcript cautions that external case studies rarely expose exact masses or inertias, so these comparisons are rough same-assumption estimates rather than reverse-engineered ground truth. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 113-126; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 197-217.

Draft, CAD, and Production Readiness

The service-level development sequence is:

1. make the concept tangible as a crude real-world draft
2. iterate CAD quickly enough to expose mistakes
3. use analytic checks where they answer a design question fast
4. freeze only when the design is credible enough to spend money and time on production
5. transfer manufacturing intentions into drawings
6. assemble prototypes, run basic tests, then build the digital interface and demos

The lecture's concrete CAD example improved RAIPAL from v0-1 to v1-1 by moving from minimum safety factor 1.8 and roughly 15 mm maximum deformation at peak torque to safety factor 3.2 and roughly 1 mm maximum deformation. The transcript emphasizes that these FEA numbers are decision aids, not exact fracture predictions; simple simulations are useful because they reveal relative weaknesses quickly. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 9-12; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 127-177; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 182-193.

Figure: RAIPAL v1-5 production-ready overview tying mechanism, actuator families, and electronics together. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 13.

By v1-5, the slide presents RAIPAL as production-ready at 7.7 kg plus 1.2 kg, with 5 kg nominal payload and roughly 10 kg peak payload, using upper-arm and forearm actuator families plus Dongdrive electronics. The same slide warns that the SLR reduction ratio was lower than expected, showing that production readiness is still a risk decision rather than a guarantee. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 13; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 207-216.

Digital Interface and Demonstrations

The lecture treats the digital interface as part of hardware completion, not an optional afterthought. For RL-oriented work, the physical robot has to enter simulation accurately and policies have to return to the real system through middleware without adding a new distribution or timing failure.

Figure: RAIPAL digital interface work split into URDF conversion and raisin middleware. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 16.

The slide calls out precise mass agreement around 5 g, rotor inertia calculation, communication / real-time rollout support, and crossed-4-bar calculation. The transcript adds that rotor inertia can be comparable to link mass in relevant robots, so a sloppy URDF can waste a large part of the intended actuator power in simulation-to-real transfer. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 16; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 9-30.

Demonstrations then become requirement-verification sequences. RAIPAL's demo categories include payload, speed, backdrivability, and other specialized tests; the transcript notes that efficiency, backlash, and thermal paths may still need separate specialized measurement. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 17; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 31-39.

Time Allocation

The development statistics make iteration and integration visible. The slide's time allocation is:
- concept design: 4.6%
- draft design: 12.7%
- design iteration: 30.6%
- production: 16.2%
- digital interface: 18.5%
- testing: 17.3%

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 25.

The next chart divides the calendar into 45.5% RAIPAL total work and 54.5% delay. The transcript's practical warning is that even a main research directive may receive only about half of available weekdays after demos, unrelated robot work, health issues, renovation, proposal preparation, and other lab responsibilities are accounted for. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 26; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 42-69; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 81-84.

Retrospective

The retrospective has three durable messages:
- Use a balanced toolset: tangible prototypes, analytic checks, and specialized tests each catch different failures.
- Design first, contribute later: good hardware often reveals publishable contribution framing only after it works and can demonstrate a capability.
- Transfer insights to the next engineer, because a single graduate hardware cycle often cannot build the "next robot" that would fully exploit the lessons.

The slide deck also flags improvement areas: balance generality versus specificity, pursue more holistic actuator-to-mechanism optimization, strengthen forearms, reduce contribution pressure, and remember that simple remains valuable. Parallel hardware development gave speed, more feedback points, and failsafes against mistakes, but required tight coordination, version control, and bottleneck management. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 28-33; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 88-188.

headingPath: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
chatChunks.text:
Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf - Sangok Seok, Albert Wang, David Otten, and Sangbae Kim, "Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control in Fast Legged Locomotion," IEEE/RSJ IROS 2012.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
headingPath: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
chatChunks.text:
Core Question

How should electromagnetic actuators be designed for fast legged locomotion, where the actuator must produce high force while staying mechanically transparent enough for force control without a foot force sensor?

chatChunks.pageTitle: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
headingPath: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
chatChunks.text:
Main Claim

Power density is not the right design metric for legged-robot actuators. The more useful design target is high torque density with low mechanical impedance, especially low reflected inertia, friction, and damping. This enables transmission-transparency and proprioceptive force control.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
headingPath: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
chatChunks.text:
Why Power Density Misleads

The paper argues that EM motors can show very high power density because high power is available at high speed and relatively low torque. But if a robot joint needs large torque, simply adding high reduction increases actuator passive impedance:
- reflected inertia
- friction
- damping
- gear-train mass and complexity

The paper therefore prefers torque density and transmission transparency as actuator-level metrics, matching the lecture's actuator-performance-metrics argument.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
headingPath: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
chatChunks.text:
Gap Radius Scaling

The paper identifies gap radius $r{gap}$ as a critical motor design parameter. Under the paper's electromagnetic-similarity assumptions:
- Motor mass $\propto r{gap}$
- Motor torque $\propto r{gap}^2$
- Rotor inertia $\propto r{gap}^3$
- Torque density $\propto r{gap}$
- Acceleration capability, torque/inertia $\propto \frac{1}{r{gap}}$

The motor constant relation used for the scaling is:

$$
KM^2 = \frac{Kt^2}{R} = \frac{\tau^2}{I^2 R}
$$

For a given wire gauge, the paper gives $KM^2 \propto r{gap}^3$. Empirical motor data showed the same qualitative trend, though with manufacturing-dependent exponents.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Section II and Fig. 2.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
headingPath: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
chatChunks.text:
Reflected Inertia Thought Experiment

If output torque and motor mass are fixed:
- Doubling motor radius halves motor length.
- Motor torque doubles, so required gear ratio halves.
- Rotor inertia increases by $4\times$.
- Reflected rotor inertia through the gearbox scales by $\frac{1}{4}$, because reflected inertia depends on gear ratio squared.

So in the ideal model, total output torque and reflected rotor inertia can stay the same. But real high-ratio gearboxes add mass, inertia, and friction loss. This is why the paper recommends the largest feasible gap radius and the least gearing needed to meet the torque requirement.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Section II and Fig. 3.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
headingPath: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
chatChunks.text:
MIT Cheetah Front Leg Design

The prototype applies this analysis:
- Motor selected: largest radius fitting a 5 inch space constraint
- Gear ratio: 1:5.8, compared with more than 1:100 in many EM legged-robot actuators
- Desired peak joint torque: roughly 100 Nm
- Motor peak torque: 21 Nm
- Gearbox: one-stage planetary gearing with four planet gears
- Architecture: hip and knee motors/gear trains coaxially located at the hip; knee driven by steel linkage
- Structure: foam-core composite humerus/radius and tendon-reinforced foot to reduce leg inertia without sacrificing strength
- Shoulder module: 4.2 kg
- Humerus: 160 g
- Lower limb including foot: 300 g
- Whole-leg moment of inertia at shoulder in straight configuration: $0.058\ \mathrm{kg\,m^2}$

This design keeps drive components near the shoulder and minimizes distal leg inertia.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Section III and Figs. 1, 4, and 5.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
headingPath: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
chatChunks.text:
Proprioceptive Force Control Result

The paper tests whether low mechanical impedance enables force control from motor current and encoder position rather than a foot force sensor.

The leg is mounted on a material testing device, calibrated for torque-dependent friction, then tested with virtual spring control. In impact tests:
- Mean absolute error / full-scale sensor force = 0.041 for a 3406 N/m virtual stiffness
- Mean absolute error / full-scale sensor force = 0.049 for a 5038 N/m virtual stiffness

The result supports the paper's claim that a transparent, low-impedance actuator can produce useful proprioceptive force estimates during dynamic interaction.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
headingPath: Seok et al. 2012: Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control
chatChunks.text:
Takeaways for This Wiki
- legged-robot-actuation should treat low gearing as a practical route to transparency, not a standalone goal.
- actuator-design-principles should distinguish ideal reflected-inertia scaling from real gearbox penalties.
- motor-constant and torque-production efficiency connect strongly to gap radius and material/topology choices.
- transmission-transparency is the control-facing reason why actuator hardware choices matter.

Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
chatChunks.text:
Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors

Chapter 1 is the textbook foundation for motor vocabulary used throughout the robot hardware wiki. It connects machine taxonomy, magnetic energy conversion, load dynamics, four-quadrant operation, and drive-system architecture. Read it as background for motor-actuator-structure, bldc-pmsm-spmsm, motional-emf-and-flux-linkage, magnetic-reluctance-torque, and motor-operating-region.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
chatChunks.text:
Scope

The chapter makes five durable points:
- Electric motors are prime movers that convert electrical energy into mechanical torque.
- Motor taxonomy is not perfectly clean: brushless DC is listed near DC motors because of its DC-like drive characteristics, but its configuration is close to a permanent-magnet synchronous AC motor.
- Useful torque normally comes from the interaction between two magnetic fields, although reluctance motors exploit the interaction between a field and a magnetic material.
- Motor selection is coupled to load inertia, load torque type, transmission ratio, and operating quadrant.
- A drive system is motor plus source, converter, controller, sensors, and mechanical load, not the bare electromagnetic machine alone.

Figure: textbook motor classification tree showing DC, AC, brushless DC, synchronous PM, induction, reluctance, and related subtypes. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 2, Fig. 1.1.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
chatChunks.text:
Motor Structure and Torque Production

The chapter starts from the two basic motor parts: the stator is stationary, the rotor moves, and the air gap lets the rotor spin while carrying magnetic flux between the two sides. Stator and rotor each have magnetic material and electrical conductors or magnets. Current in conductors makes magnetic fields; permanent magnets can also supply field flux.

The simplest torque story is alignment. One magnetic field on the stator and one on the rotor pull toward an energetically favorable relative angle. In a brushed DC motor, brushes and the commutator keep the rotor current distribution effectively fixed in space even while the physical rotor turns. In an AC motor, the stator field rotates, and the rotor field must rotate with it to keep producing torque.

Reluctance torque is a different but related mechanism: the rotor moves toward the position that reduces magnetic reluctance. The chapter treats this as usually lower torque than two-field interaction, but this idea becomes important later for SynRM, IPMSM, and salient-pole machines.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 3-7.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
Magnetic Energy, Flux Linkage, and Reluctance
chatChunks.text:
Magnetic Energy, Flux Linkage, and Reluctance

Chapter 1 introduces the magnetic quantities that reappear in motor modeling:

$$
\lambda = N\phi
$$

$$
L = \frac{\lambda}{i}
$$

$$
L = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}}
$$

Here $\lambda$ is flux linkage, $N$ is turns, $\phi$ is magnetic flux, $L$ is inductance, $i$ is current, and $\mathcal{R}$ is magnetic reluctance. The key design intuition is that a longer air gap increases reluctance and lowers inductance, so the same current produces less flux linkage.

The magnetization curve is also important. In the unsaturated region, flux is roughly proportional to current and inductance is approximately constant. Near and above the knee of the curve, added current produces diminishing flux. Since motor torque depends on flux and current, saturation is where simple linear motor models begin to over-predict torque.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 9-12.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
chatChunks.text:
Mechanical Load Model

The mechanical side is modeled with Newton's law. For a rotating load with constant inertia:

$$
TM - TL = J\frac{d\omega}{dt}
$$

The chapter then adds viscous friction and load torque:

$$
TM = J\frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega + TL
$$

This equation is the textbook version of the robot-actuator idea that motor torque must cover acceleration torque, friction, and external load torque. The same section classifies common load types:
- constant torque loads such as elevators and conveyors
- constant power loads where $TL \propto \frac{1}{\omega}$
- fan/pump loads where $TL \propto \omega^2$

For a translational mass driven by a pulley radius $r$, the load reflects to the motor shaft as:

$$
Je = Mr^2
$$

For gears, reflected inertia and friction scale with the square of the gear ratio. This is the textbook reason reducer ratio affects actuator feel and acceleration so strongly.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 27-32.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
chatChunks.text:
Operating Quadrants

The chapter defines motoring and generating by the direction of torque relative to speed. If torque and speed have the same sign, energy flows from the electrical side into the mechanical load. If torque opposes speed, the motor is braking or generating, so mechanical energy is returned to or dissipated by the electrical side.

This gives the four-quadrant map used by drive engineers:
- Quadrant 1: forward motoring, $+T$, $+\omega$
- Quadrant 2: forward braking/generating, $-T$, $+\omega$
- Quadrant 3: reverse motoring, $-T$, $-\omega$
- Quadrant 4: reverse braking/generating, $+T$, $-\omega$

For robot hardware, this matters because a joint motor is not only an accelerator. It also has to brake, hold, recover energy, or dissipate energy safely depending on the mechanical task.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 32-34.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
chatChunks.text:
Drive-System View

Chapter 1 ends by framing a motor drive as a system: source, converter, controller, motor, sensors, and load. This is the most useful bridge to robot actuators. The power electronic converter adapts the available source to the motor, the controller chooses current/voltage/frequency commands, and sensors provide current, speed, or position feedback.

Figure: source, power electronic converter, digital controller, sensors, motor, and mechanical load as one motor-drive system. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 35, Fig. 1.32.

The chapter also gives practical component tradeoffs. DC motors are simpler to control but have brush/commutator maintenance issues. AC motors are generally smaller, lighter, higher power density, and more maintenance-free, but high-performance AC drives need more complex converters and control.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 35-37.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors

Chapter 2 uses the DC motor as a compact model for motor dynamics and control. It is the cleanest textbook source for armature voltage, back-EMF, torque constant, current-loop design, anti-windup, speed-loop nesting, and H-bridge PWM. Read it before dc-motor-control, motor-operating-region, motor-current-control, and motor-driver-inverter.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Scope

The chapter builds from machine model to controller implementation:
- brushed DC motor construction and commutation
- armature circuit, back-EMF, torque, and load equations
- speed-torque curve and voltage/field speed control
- constant-torque and constant-power operating regions
- cascaded current, speed, and position control
- PI current-controller gain selection and bandwidth limits
- anti-windup for voltage saturation
- four-quadrant H-bridge PWM with bipolar and unipolar switching

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
DC Motor Model

The chapter models a DC motor drive with four coupled equations:

$$
Va = Ra ia + La\frac{d ia}{dt} + ea
$$

$$
ea = ke\phif\omegam
$$

$$
Te = kT\phif ia
$$

$$
Te = J\frac{d\omegam}{dt} + B\omegam + TL
$$

In the textbook's SI convention, $kT=ke$. This is the scalar model behind the intuition that voltage creates current, current creates torque, and speed creates back-EMF that reduces the voltage left to drive current.

Figure: DC motor equivalent circuit and moving-conductor back-EMF sketch. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 5, Figs. 2.5-2.6.

The torque equation is simple because the rotor current conductors are kept perpendicular to the stator flux by commutation. That is why the chapter says DC motors inherently have a maximum-torque-per-ampere characteristic.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 4-6.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Steady-State Speed Control

In steady state, with friction neglected, the speed-torque relation is:

$$
\omegam = \frac{Va}{k\phif} - \frac{Ra}{(k\phif)^2}TL
$$

This gives the standard downward-sloping speed-torque curve. Increasing load torque requires more armature current, which increases resistive voltage drop and lowers available speed.

Two speed-control methods follow directly:
- Armature voltage control: hold field flux constant and vary $Va$. This is the normal below-base-speed method and produces a constant-torque region.
- Field flux control: hold terminal voltage at its limit and reduce field flux. This extends speed above base speed, but torque per amp falls, speed regulation worsens, and the response is slow because the field winding has large inductance.

Permanent-magnet DC motors cannot use field-flux control through a field winding, so they cannot use this textbook field-weakening method.

Figure: DC motor constant-torque and constant-power capability curve. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 11, Fig. 2.11.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 7-11.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Cascaded Control

The chapter's control architecture is current inside speed inside position. Torque is expensive to measure directly, but under constant field flux it is proportional to current, so current control is treated as torque control.

The current loop must have the widest bandwidth. The speed loop should be slower than current control, and position should be slower than speed. This is the same nesting used later in PMSM drives: fast electrical control inside slower mechanical control.

The chapter also contrasts open-loop and closed-loop control. Open-loop control depends on a good model and cannot reject back-EMF or load-torque disturbances reliably. High-performance motor drives therefore use feedback for current, speed, or position.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 18-24.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
PI Current Controller

The chapter designs a PI current loop around the armature $R$-$L$ plant. Back-EMF acts as a disturbance; for low-inertia servo motors, the text recommends feedforward compensation using speed information so the current loop sees a simpler $R$-$L$ circuit.

With pole-zero cancellation and a desired current-loop bandwidth $\omega{cc}$:

$$
K{pc}=La\omega{cc}
$$

$$
K{ic}=Ra\omega{cc}
$$

The gains depend on motor parameters. A motor with larger inductance needs a larger proportional gain for the same current bandwidth.

Bandwidth is constrained by converter switching frequency and current sampling. The textbook rule of thumb is up to about one-tenth of switching frequency when sampling twice per switching period, up to about one-twentieth when sampling once per switching period, and preferably below about one twenty-fifth of sampling frequency in the once-per-period case.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 29-34.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Anti-Windup

The current controller outputs a voltage reference, but the motor and converter impose voltage limits. If the PI output saturates, the integrator can keep accumulating error even though the plant cannot receive the requested voltage. The chapter describes this as integral windup, which causes overshoot and long settling.

The standard fix is back-calculation anti-windup: feed the difference between the unlimited controller output and the saturated output back into the integrator path. The textbook gives the common gain choice:

$$
Ka = \frac{1}{K{pc}}
$$

This maps directly to PMSM current control, where d-q voltage commands also saturate against the DC bus.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 35-36.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
H-Bridge PWM

The chapter's four-quadrant converter is the H-bridge. Four switching devices let the drive apply positive or negative voltage across the armature, enabling forward/reverse motoring and braking.

Figure: H-bridge circuit for four-quadrant DC motor operation. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 45, Fig. 2.47.

The chapter compares two PWM methods:
- Bipolar switching: diagonal pairs switch together, so the armature voltage alternates between $+V{dc}$ and $-V{dc}$. The average voltage changes smoothly across zero, but current ripple is large because the voltage swing is wide.
- Unipolar switching: the two bridge legs are modulated separately, so the motor sees $0$ and $+V{dc}$ or $0$ and $-V{dc}$. Current and torque ripple are smaller, but very small average voltages near sign changes can be harder to realize smoothly.

Figure: unipolar H-bridge switching waveforms and current ripple comparison. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 47, Figs. 2.50-2.51.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 45-47.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
chatChunks.text:

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors

Chapter 3 explains steady-state AC motor behavior, with most of the chapter devoted to induction motors and a final section on synchronous motors. It is useful background for induction-motor-basics, electrical-angle, motor-operating-region, bldc-pmsm-spmsm, and magnetic-reluctance-torque.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Scope

The chapter covers:
- induction-motor structure: stator, squirrel-cage rotor, wound rotor
- rotating magnetic field and synchronous speed
- slip, rotor frequency, equivalent circuit, and power flow
- torque-speed behavior, rotor resistance effects, and speed control
- induction motor constant-torque, constant-power, and breakdown-torque regions
- synchronous motor steady-state model, torque angle, saliency, and starting

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Induction Motor Principle

An induction motor creates torque without externally powering the rotor. Balanced three-phase stator currents produce a rotating air-gap field. That field cuts the rotor conductors, induces EMF, drives rotor current through the shorted rotor circuit, and the rotor current interacting with the field produces force.

The field speed is the synchronous speed:

$$
ns=\frac{120f}{P}\quad\text{r/min}
$$

where $f$ is stator electrical frequency and $P$ is pole count. The rotor must run below this speed for motoring torque. If the rotor caught the rotating field exactly, there would be no changing flux in the rotor, no induced rotor voltage, no rotor current, and no induction torque.

Slip is:

$$
s = \frac{ns-n}{ns}
$$

A typical full-load slip range stated in the chapter is about 1-5%.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 5-10 and 15-16.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Structure and Winding Notes

The stator uses three-phase windings displaced by $120^\circ$ electrical. Chapter 3 notes that induction motor stator phases are usually connected in delta for low supply voltage and wye for high supply voltage. Distributed windings are used to approximate a sinusoidal air-gap mmf and reduce torque ripple compared with concentrated windings.

The most common rotor is the squirrel-cage rotor, where bars are short-circuited by end rings. The chapter states that about 95% of induction motors use this rotor because it is simple and rugged. Wound-rotor machines expose rotor windings through slip rings, allowing external rotor resistance to shape starting and torque-speed behavior.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 3-5.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Equivalent Circuit and Power Flow

The steady-state equivalent circuit separates stator resistance/leakage, magnetizing branch, core loss, and rotor terms reflected to the stator. Only the mutual or magnetizing flux crosses the air gap and participates in energy conversion; leakage flux links only its own winding.

The air gap makes induction motors different from transformers. The chapter states that transformer magnetizing current is typically 1-5% of rated current, while induction motor magnetizing current is about 30-50% of rated current.

The rotor frequency is the slip frequency:

$$
fr = sfs
$$

The induced rotor voltage is also proportional to slip, so it is largest at standstill and goes to zero at synchronous speed.

In power-flow terms, the rotor copper loss and mechanical output split the air-gap power:

$$
Pr=sP{ag}
$$

$$
P{mech}=(1-s)P{ag}
$$

That is the central efficiency intuition: lower slip sends less air-gap power into rotor copper loss and more into mechanical output.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 11-26.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Speed Control and Operating Regions

The basic speed-control lever is stator frequency because synchronous speed is proportional to frequency. Below base speed, the chapter uses constant $V/f$ control. Since induced voltage is proportional to frequency times flux, keeping voltage roughly proportional to frequency keeps air-gap flux approximately constant.

Above base speed, voltage cannot keep increasing, so flux weakens. The induction motor operating range divides into:
- constant-torque region: voltage and frequency rise together, flux and current stay approximately constant
- constant-power region: voltage and current are limited; flux drops with speed and slip frequency is increased to hold current
- breakdown-torque region: slip frequency reaches its stable maximum, current falls, and torque drops faster

Figure: induction motor constant-torque, constant-power, and breakdown-torque regions under variable-frequency operation. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 45, Fig. 3.47.

This is the AC-machine analogue of the DC motor's voltage-control and field-weakening regions.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 39-49.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Synchronous Motors

The synchronous motor section contrasts induction machines with machines whose rotor field locks to the stator rotating field. A synchronous motor has a three-phase stator, like an induction motor, but the rotor field comes from DC field excitation or permanent magnets. It develops steady torque when rotor speed equals synchronous speed.

For a cylindrical rotor with stator resistance neglected, the chapter gives the classic power-angle relation:

$$
P = 3\frac{VsEf}{Xs}\sin\delta
$$

$$
T = 3\frac{VsEf}{\omegas Xs}\sin\delta
$$

The angle $\delta$ is the power angle or torque angle between stator voltage and excitation voltage. It increases with load and is analogous to slip as the load-dependent variable, but the rotor still runs at synchronous speed.

For salient-pole machines, the rotor has different d- and q-axis reluctances, so the torque has both electromagnetic and reluctance components:

$$
T =
3\frac{VsEf}{\omegas Xd}\sin\delta
- 3\frac{Vs^2(Xd-Xq)}{2\omegasXdXq}\sin 2\delta
$$

This is the steady-state version of the magnet-torque plus reluctance-torque split used later in IPMSM/SynRM discussion.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 49-57.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
chatChunks.text:
Starting Synchronous Motors

The chapter notes that synchronous motors are not self-starting from a fixed 50/60 Hz supply because they produce steady torque only once synchronized. Traditional starting methods include an external motor or damper windings that let the machine start as an induction motor. PMSM variable-speed drives avoid this by using a PWM inverter to start at reduced frequency with rotor-position information.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 57-58.

headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
chatChunks.text:
Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame

Chapter 4 is the textbook counterpart to the lecture d-q derivations. It starts from abc phase variables with time-varying inductances, then uses reference-frame transformations to produce constant-coefficient d-q models for induction motors and PMSMs. Read it with abc-to-dq-transform-derivation, spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, spmsm-torque-equation, and induction-motor-basics.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
chatChunks.text:
Scope

The chapter covers:
- abc voltage and flux-linkage equations for induction motors
- why stator-rotor mutual inductance varies with rotor angle
- PMSM structure, magnet materials, SPMSM/IPMSM topology differences, and saliency
- Clarke and Park transformations into stationary and rotating d-q frames
- induction-motor d-q voltage, flux-linkage, and torque equations
- PMSM d-q voltage, flux-linkage, equivalent-circuit, and torque equations

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
chatChunks.text:
Why Reference Frames Are Needed

In abc coordinates, a three-phase AC motor has phase windings fixed in the stator and rotor windings or magnets moving relative to them. For an induction motor, the mutual-inductance matrix between stator and rotor windings depends on rotor angle $\thetar$. Those coefficients change with time whenever the rotor moves.

That is the modeling problem: the physical circuit is symmetric, but the equations in raw phase variables are full of sinusoidal, time-varying coefficients. Reference-frame transformation rewrites the same electrical state in d-q-n coordinates so the main mutual-inductance terms become constant in a selected rotating frame.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 3-11.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
chatChunks.text:
PMSM Structure and Saliency

Chapter 4 also gives a practical PMSM taxonomy. Synchronous motors can be viewed as a spectrum from pure reluctance torque to pure magnet torque: SynRM, PM-assisted SynRM, IPMSM, inset SPMSM, and SPMSM.

For PMSM hardware, the chapter distinguishes:
- distributed vs concentrated stator windings
- radial-flux vs axial-flux layouts
- surface-mounted permanent magnet synchronous motors (SPMSM)
- interior permanent magnet synchronous motors (IPMSM), including parallel and spoke/perpendicular topologies
- inner-rotor vs outer-rotor construction

SPMSMs have magnets on the rotor surface, so the effective air gap includes magnet thickness and the d- and q-axis inductances are approximately equal. IPMSMs bury magnets inside the rotor, usually giving magnetic saliency and stronger flux-weakening capability. The chapter also notes the practical magnet tradeoff: NdFeB has high remanence and coercivity but is temperature sensitive, so thermal limits and demagnetization risk constrain maximum current.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 12-18.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
chatChunks.text:
Clarke and Park Transformations

The chapter uses a magnitude-invariant transform with coefficient $\frac{2}{3}$. In the stationary reference frame, the abc variables are transformed into d-q-n variables. For a balanced three-phase system with no neutral current, the n-axis variable is zero.

Then a rotation matrix maps stationary d-q variables into a rotating frame:

$$
fd^e = fd^s\cos\theta + fq^s\sin\theta
$$

$$
fq^e = -fd^s\sin\theta + fq^s\cos\theta
$$

The chapter labels the stationary transform as Clarke's transformation and the stationary-to-rotating transform as Park's transformation.

Figure: transformations between abc, stationary d-q, and arbitrary rotating reference frames. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 29, Fig. 4.22.

The important convention warning is that magnitude-invariant and power-invariant transforms do not produce the same numerical d-q values or power expressions. The book uses magnitude-invariant scaling for motor variables.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 25-29.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
chatChunks.text:
Induction Motor d-q Model

After transformation, the induction motor stator voltage equations in an arbitrary frame rotating at angular speed $\omega$ are:

$$
v{ds}^{\omega}=Rs i{ds}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{ds}^{\omega}}{dt}-\omega\lambda{qs}^{\omega}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{\omega}=Rs i{qs}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{qs}^{\omega}}{dt}+\omega\lambda{ds}^{\omega}
$$

The rotor equations use the relative speed between the chosen frame and rotor:

$$
v{dr}^{\omega}=Rr i{dr}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{dr}^{\omega}}{dt}-(\omega-\omegar)\lambda{qr}^{\omega}
$$

$$
v{qr}^{\omega}=Rr i{qr}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{qr}^{\omega}}{dt}+(\omega-\omegar)\lambda{dr}^{\omega}
$$

For a squirrel-cage rotor, rotor voltages are zero because the rotor bars are shorted. The d- and q-axis flux linkages become magnetically decoupled and use constant inductances:

$$
\lambda{ds}^{\omega}=Ls i{ds}^{\omega}+Lm i{dr}^{\omega}
$$

$$
\lambda{qs}^{\omega}=Ls i{qs}^{\omega}+Lm i{qr}^{\omega}
$$

The chapter's key modeling payoff is explicit: transformation removes time-varying mutual inductances due to stator-rotor relative motion and decouples d and q flux linkages.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 37-42.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
chatChunks.text:
PMSM d-q Model

The PMSM voltage equations reuse the stator d-q structure:

$$
v{ds}^{\omega}=Rs i{ds}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{ds}^{\omega}}{dt}-\omega\lambda{qs}^{\omega}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{\omega}=Rs i{qs}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{qs}^{\omega}}{dt}+\omega\lambda{ds}^{\omega}
$$

The PMSM difference is in flux linkage. For an IPMSM in the rotor frame:

$$
\lambdad^r=Ld id^r+\phif
$$

$$
\lambdaq^r=Lq iq^r
$$

For an ideal SPMSM, $Ld \approx Lq$, so the saliency term largely disappears and torque is mainly controlled by q-axis current.

Figure: IPMSM d-axis and q-axis equivalent circuits in the rotor reference frame. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 48, Fig. 4.27.

The torque expression separates magnet torque and reluctance torque:

$$
Te=\frac{3}{2}\frac{P}{2}\left(\phif iq^r+(Ld-Lq)id^r iq^r\right)
$$

The exact sign of the reluctance term depends on the book's d-q axis and current sign convention, so use this page as a convention cross-check before mixing formulas with lecture notes or software libraries.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 46-49.

chatChunks.pageTitle: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
headingPath: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
